Motive / allegiance - Wants parents in same room without shouting; thinks if she learns the right song the ocean will make that happen. - Ocean = endless playground; death is abstract—fish "just stop moving." - Trusts mum absolutely except when engine room is dark; trusts the filament because it hums back. Flavour lines To mum: "Your boat sneezed—didn’t you hear it?" Humming, eyes closed: "It’s teaching me the quiet part, but I keep forgetting the end." --- # Peter "Pete" Holcomb – Ex-husband Background - Grants Pass born, forklift driver at Amazon fulfilment 4-on/3-off; seniority enough for daytime shift, still smells of cardboard dust. - Married Mara after three-month whirlwind; loved her stories, hated the sea that kept taking her. Divorce mutual but bitterness fresh—calls boat "your other man." Personality / ticks - Quotes safety manuals when nervous; keeps hand-sanitiser in cup holder. - Buys same brand white T-shirt in 10-packs; refuses prints with logos. - Records every phone call "for clarity"—has 300 GB of marital arguments. - Terrified of deep water since cousin drowned at 12; won’t step on pier. Physicality - 6 foot, soft through middle, arms corded from pallets; sunburn on bald spot shaped like Idaho. - Speaks through teeth when angry—jaw clicks. - Laughs like surprised bark, sudden, then guilty. Motive / allegiance - Keep Sammi safe, which in his mind = inland, scheduled, predictable. - Jealous of Mara’s certainty about anything; wants court to see her instability. - Still attracted enough that her grin hurts; compensates by quoting regulations. Flavour lines Phone voice, clipped: "Send me the fuel receipts, Mara—judge likes paper trails." Quiet, after fight: "You breathe salt better than oxygen; that’s not a compliment." --- # Deek (Derek Yoshida) – Deck-hand / First mate Background - 42, fourth-generation Japanese-American, Crescent City; family lost boat in ’11 tsunami, so he crews for whoever pays. - Did two years community college for music production—owns beat-up bass guitar he never plays anymore; fingers too calloused. Personality / ticks - Whistles TV themes off key; claims it scares gulls. - Keeps tiny spiral notebook of "famous last words" he hears; last entry will be his own. - Superstitious: throws pinch of coffee grounds over left shoulder every sunrise. - Eats Cup Noodles dry, sprinkles flavor pack like salt lick. Physicality - Wiry, sun-charred neck, permanent squid-ink stain on right forearm shaped like California. - Walks on balls of feet—silent; surprises people. - Grins with gold canine; when serious lips hide it, looks suddenly younger. Motive / allegiance - Paycheck first, loyalty second—has slept in car to avoid rent. - Secretly wants captain’s chair but knows he hasn’t the grit; channels envy into sardonic jokes. - Likes Sammi because she laughs at his cartoon voices; would die to keep her from seeing it. Flavour lines After net snag: "Either we cut the bag or we grow gills—your call, Cap." Notebook scribble, later: "Last thing I heard was nothing—then the light ate my name." --- # Hector Ramirez – Harbour-side fix-it friend Background - 38, self-taught electronics, failed electrician’s union test (colour-blind). - Runs bait-shop counter mornings, fixes marine radios afternoons; trades labour for six-packs. - Grew up speaking Spanglish, learned tech from pirated PDFs; manuals full of sticky notes in two languages. Personality / ticks - Taps a pencil on his front teeth when thinking—dent chipped exactly there. - Keeps dead CRT in shop because "the hum tells me when someone’s lying." - Collects broken things more than working ones—says they have stories. Physicality - Stocky, sleeves rolled to show koi tattoo whose colours he’s never seen. - Voice soft until he hits jargon, then machine-gun fast; hands always moving like conducting invisible orchestra. Motive / allegiance - Curiosity stronger than fear—wants to know what the rings are, who cares what they want. - Loyal to Mara because she never mocked his stutter in eighth grade. - Will trade info for boat batteries; draws line at stealing from Coast Guard—has green card paranoia. Flavour lines Peering at scope: "That waveform’s got hips—see the sway? Something’s dancing down there." To coast guard officer: "My paperwork’s like my soul—bit frayed, mostly legible."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The engine coughs at dawn, a wet lung sound that means cold and old. I throttle down, let Wanjiku wallow while I haul the first set. Gulls circle like white sparks against bruised sky, screaming for free breakfast. Net comes up heavy, silver flashes inside—then the flashes don’t flip. Rockfish, all belly. Eyes already milked, each gill slit shining like a dime stuck in a drain. I count them anyway, voice low. “Moja, mbili, tatu…” Habit from gran, who counted everything aloud so God wouldn’t skim. Hits fifty before the nausea does. Fifty dead in thirty fathom, no mark, no bruise. Deck stinks of penny blood and cucumber guts—their last meal bursting through split stomachs. Radio spits static. Someone else cursing, same find. I key twice, let them hear my silence, then look up. That’s when the sky wrongs itself. Three things, rings maybe, sliding into place like dull nickels stacked by a kid. They blot sunrise, edges flickering neon white-then-black, too fast. My ears pop. Electronics squeal—depth sounder, GPS, even the busted CD player I never fixed—all screaming the same tooth-ache tone. I slap the console, useless. The tone stops dead. Engine dies with it, just quits like it’s been shot. Wind dies too. Ocean goes glass. And the fish keep rising—first my net, then everywhere, a white carpet spreading to the horizon. Not frantic, just…letting go. I lean over the rail, prod one. Its gill flares, mirror-bright flake peels off, sticks to my finger. Sharp. Hurts more than it should. I shake it, can’t dislodge, finally scrape against cork-soled boot. Blood beads, bright on brown skin, and the cut hums—low, inside bone. Phone vibrates in pocket. Sammi’s name. I swipe with thumb still bleeding. “Mom, you coming home tonight?” Her voice thin, morning-clogged. “Working, baby. Go back to sleep.” She hums, three-note descending, same stupid jingle from the cereal ad. My cut pulses in time. I drop the phone, grab the rail. Metal vibrates like a bell someone rang hours ago and forgot to touch again. I start counting aloud, can’t stop. “Nne, tano, sita…” Gulls land, too heavy, too many. One waddles right onto my deck, pecks a flake off dead gill, swallows. Its eyes film white instantly; it keels, wings still flapping. I kick the bird overboard, gag on grape Bubble-Yum burp—breakfast repeating. No wind, no throttle, thirty miles out. I eye the sail cover, curse the day I ever relied on diesel. Mast steps take sweat-slick seconds. Canvas unfurls, grabs the nothing breeze, luffs, then fills with a sound like sheets shaken in a small room. Wanjiku leans, sluggish but willing. I aim her bow west, toward the notch where land should be, though smoke-coloured haze hides everything. Deck slick with fish slime; I shuffle, keep balance. Every step squishes. The mirrors glint, hundreds of tiny betrayals catching a sun that hasn’t properly risen. I try not to look up again but do. The rings are still now, fitted tight like someone found the right coin tray. Around them sky looks older, washed too many times. Phone again—text this time. Peter. *You hear this shit? Sammi won’t stop humming.* I thumb back *later* though signal bars jitter. My palm throbs where scale sliced. Blood hasn’t dripped, just sits there, silvered, quivering with my pulse. I lick it—metallic, wrong-ocean taste. Spit, but spit comes out almost clear, like the sea took colour while I wasn’t watching. Hour slides by under sail. Engine stubborn. I strip casing, check fuel bulb—firm, no air. Spark plugs smell of cat-piss ethanol, normal. I whack the starter solenoid, old trick. Nothing. The rings overhead make no sound now, but my bones keep replaying that first squeal, a tape loop tightened behind eyes. Crescent City lighthouse should poke up soon. I glass the horizon, see instead a white line forming—surf, maybe, except waves are dead flat. White line grows, resolves into floating mat of fish, same as here, stretching all the way in. Gulls dive, eat, die, become part of the crust. The smell reaches me—hot pennies and the cucumber rot times ten. I gag again, grape nothing left. Sammi’s hum sneaks back into head even though phone stays silent. I find myself humming along, off-key. The cut in palm buzzes warmer. I tear duct tape with teeth, wrap it sloppy—tape turns dark, not red, dark like mercury. Keep hauling sail, keep counting. “Saba, nane, tisa…” I don’t remember what comes after ten in Swahili, so I start over, louder, as if volume can push the sky back where it belongs. Land finally shows—ridge dark, radio mast blinking normal red, oblivious. Harbor mouth looks smaller, squeezed by invisible thumbs. I drop sail early, let momentum glide. No tow truck waits, no sirens, just the usual gull-shit pier and the fish plant venting steam. But the steam today smells sweet, almost fruity, like someone’s boiling fruit gum. I tie up bowline, hands dumb. Dock planks flex underfoot—vibration, low, same note as cut. I look back at the ocean. It’s white all the way to the horizon, a photograph exposed wrong. My knees go loose; I sit hard, ass in dead fish, and for the first time since the engine quit I feel time catch up. It’s only seven-thirty. Breakfast dishes still in sink at home. Sammi maybe pouring cereal, humming that three-note hook while the sky behind her kitchen window keeps stacking coins no one asked for. A mirror flake drifts off my tape-wrapped palm, spins, lands on wet wood. It shows me younger, uniform collar high and tight, mouth open in a laugh I’d forgotten how to make. I stare until salt wind blurs the image—wind returning, carrying the smell of landfills and kelp and something electric I don’t have a name for yet. The cut hums, insistent, teaching me the first word of a language I never wanted to learn.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 1 of this first-contact story is a genuinely accomplished piece of prose that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses systematically. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Mara's voice is convincingly rendered. The Swahili counting ("Moja, mbili, tatu...") functions as both character detail and emotional texture—it's not decorative but rooted in her psychology (counting from gran, so God wouldn't skim). The grape Bubble-Yum detail, the duck-footed balance, the cochlear damage manifesting as bone-hum—these are integrated rather than listed. She feels inhabited. The one unlikable choice (pocketing the mirror scale, telling no one) is present though understated. The character is consistent with the profile. PROSE QUALITY: The writing is genuinely strong in places. "Gulls circle like white sparks against bruised sky" is earned. "Each gill slit shining like a dime stuck in a drain" is precise and original. "The rings overhead make no sound now, but my bones keep replaying that first squeal, a tape loop tightened behind eyes" works. The sensory spine (sound first, smell second) is followed. The penny-blood and cucumber-guts smell is specific and unpleasant in the right way. FORCED POETRY: Some metaphors don't fully land. "Three things, rings maybe, sliding into place like dull nickels stacked by a kid" is effective. But "sky wrongs itself" is a bit precious. "Hundreds of tiny betrayals catching a sun that hasn't properly risen" is the kind of line that sounds good but is slightly overwrought—the fish-scale-as-betrayal metaphor is a stretch. "A photograph exposed wrong" is a cliché. The final paragraph's "teaching me the first word of a language I never wanted to learn" is a tidy metaphor the plan explicitly said to avoid. The mirror flake showing her younger in uniform is a bit too convenient and symbolic. DIALOGUE: Minimal, which suits the chapter. The Sammi phone call is brief and functional. Peter's text is appropriately terse. No dialogue problems. PLOT/COHERENCE: The chapter follows its plan closely—cold dawn, haul, sky-rings, electronics squeal, engine death, fish die, mirror scale cuts palm, sail home, dock refuses catch (actually this last beat is missing—the buyer offering half price for "chrome herring" doesn't appear). The chapter ends at the dock but doesn't include the buyer scene or the explicit "tells no one" about the scale. The mirror flake showing her younger is an addition not in the plan, and it's the weakest moment—too on-the-nose symbolically. PACING: The chapter moves well. The sail-home section could be tighter—there's some repetition of the "counting" motif that starts to feel mechanical. The gull dying after eating a flake is a strong, economical horror beat. DARKNESS: The darkness is well-earned—the fish die-off feels genuinely catastrophic and strange, not melodramatic. The horror is ecological and personal simultaneously. WEAKNESSES: The final image (mirror showing her younger) is the chapter's biggest misstep—it's the kind of tidy symbolic resolution the plan warned against. "Teaching me the first word of a language I never wanted to learn" is exactly the press-release language the plan flagged. Some of the middle section (stripping the engine casing) is slightly mechanical in its prose. The chapter plan called for the dock buyer scene which is absent. OVERALL: A strong opening chapter with a distinctive voice, good sensory grounding, and genuine strangeness. The weaknesses are real but not fatal—some metaphors overstay their welcome, the final image is too neat, and one planned beat is missing. But the prose is mostly doing what it should: making you feel cold, broke, and slightly seasick. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The dock doesn’t wake—it grumbles, same as always, diesel pumps hacking their morning cigarettes. But today the planks buzz under my boots, a low tooth-rattle that makes the gulls hop and bitch. I feel it in the taped-up slice on my palm—throb, pause, throb—like the ocean’s keeping time with my heart because it can’t find its own. Hector’s shack smells of burnt solder and cat piss. He’s bent over a gutted radar, pencil tapping his front teeth, leaving a grey dot. “Need a scope check,” I say, shoving the busted handheld toward him. “Coffee first. You look like the tide dragged you backward.” I don’t argue. Coffee comes in a chipped Dodgers mug that still smells of old milk. I sip, count the cracks—three, same as yesterday—then stop because the humming in my hand doubles. Hector clips the scope leads to the handheld’s speaker. The screen blooms: a single fat peak, seventeen hertz, steady as a death vow. “Seventeen,” he whistles. “Below what we hear, but your body feels it, yeah?” I nod, not mentioning the grape gum aftertaste climbing my throat again. He kills the lights, turns up gain. The room fills with a pressure like someone stuffing cotton behind my eyes. My molars itch. On screen the wave pulses—same cadence Sammi hummed over the phone. Same three-step fall. “Whole harbour’s singing it,” Hector says. “Ran leads off the pier piling—same shape. You’re not crazy, Mara. Something’s cranking sub-audible bass out there.” He jerks a thumb toward the window, sky invisible beyond stacked traps. I pocket the scope photo, fold it small. Evidence, maybe. Maybe just something to hold. “Keep it quiet,” I tell him. “Lady, secrets cost extra.” I pay with my last twenty and the chrome flake I found stuck to my boot. He eyes it, bites, shrugs—tips it into a jar of other broken sparkles. Outside wind has teeth. I head for the truck but detour—boots decide, not brain—toward the payphone that still works when cells don’t. Collect call to Grants Pass. Peter picks up on third ring, voice full of cardboard dust. “She okay?” I ask. “Won’t stop the damn hum. Teacher called, said kids were crying. Sounds like whales dying.” “Put her on.” Static, then Sammi’s breath. “Mom, my nose tickles inside.” “Hum for me, baby.” She does—three notes, perfect match to the throb in my palm. The cut warms under tape. I feel the sync like a key turning. Peter again: “Doctor says nothing wrong. But she slept in the bathtub last night, said the walls didn’t shake there.” “I’ll come when I can,” I lie. “You said that last week.” Line clicks dead. I stand in phone booth, counting coins—moja, mbili—then punch the metal till pain drowns the buzz. Back at the boat Deek’s waiting, foot tapping deck. “Bank called,” he says. “Wants paperwork or they freeze fuel card.” I tell him we’re fishing tomorrow anyway. He grins gold canine, knows I’m bluffing, follows because rent’s due everywhere. We haul the last night’s kill to the ice-house. Buyers refuse—plant manager waving state memo: unknown contaminant, quarantine possible. Deek argues, gets a lowered price for “bait grade.” I sign, hating the squish of pen in taped hand. Money won’t cover diesel, let alone crew share. I pay him half, promise the rest. He pockets, doesn’t thank. Evening drops purple. I should drive inland, kiss Sammi goodnight, explain why Mommy smells like fish rot. Instead I stay, scraping scales off decking with putty knife. Each flick reveals wood pale, untouched by sun—ghost planks. The mirrors keep catching the yard light, throwing tiny Maras back at me: tired, jaw clenched, Dodgers mug still in fist. I give up, go below. Galley smells of old ramen and diesel heat. I open the icebox, pull a single beer, pop top—sound seems too loud, then too soft. Hum leaks through hull now, rides the copper water-pipe, taps my back teeth. I press tongue to roof, click the way Sammi does—answer, maybe. The tape on my hand darkens, not red, mercury again. I peel it slow. Cut edges shine, skin pinched silver like I’m part zipper. Beer tastes metallic. I pour it out, watch foam die. Grape gum wrapper peeks from pocket—last piece. I unwrap, fold the gum into cheek. Sweet covers metal, briefly. I hum her three notes around it, feel the boat sway though harbour water is flat. Wanjiku’s bones answer with a creak that matches tempo—old woman agreeing. On deck again, night wind carries new smell: over-ripe fruit, like someone juicing kiwi in a diesel jar. The rings sit beyond clouds, invisible but present—three coins pressed to the planet’s eye. I imagine them listening through my cut, through Sammi’s bedroom walls, through every pipe and rail that conducts their secret bass. I fetch the handheld recorder, thumb on. Stick pickup against mast. It doesn’t pick seventeen hertz, but I feel it—mast shivers. I speak, voice low: “You want something, ask plain.” Playback gives only soft static and my heart thumping cardboard. Still, saying it loosens the chest a notch. Below again I try sleep. Bunk vinyl cold against cheek. The hum waits until eyelids drop, then steps closer—inside marrow now. I dream water rising through floorboards, fish swimming mid-air, each wearing my face small and scared. They mouth numbers I can’t catch. I wake before dawn, socks soaked in sweat, grape gum hardened to pebble on tongue. I peel off socks, toss them, pad barefoot to wheelhouse. Sky still dark but harbour lights flicker—syncopated to seventeen, I swear. I start engine for warmth. It catches on second try, roars healthy. Relief tastes like blood. I let it idle, hand on throttle, imagining tomorrow: head out, nets down, maybe something living left to catch. Fuel gauge hovers above empty. I kill engine, silence sudden, huge. My cut pulses, counting coins I don’t have. I count back, whispering Swahili, losing numbers in the dark. Harbour answers with the same low note, patient, endless, teaching me the shape of a mouth that hasn’t yet spoken.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 of this first-contact story demonstrates considerable craft and largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through the key elements systematically. STRENGTHS: The sensory grounding is consistently strong. The chapter opens with "diesel pumps hacking their morning cigarettes" — a metaphor that earns its keep because it's specific to the environment and character. The Dodgers mug with three cracks, the grape gum wrapper, the "over-ripe fruit, like someone juicing kiwi in a diesel jar" — these details accumulate into a convincing world without feeling like inventory. The 17 Hz discovery through Hector's oscilloscope is handled with appropriate restraint; the science stays impressionistic rather than technical. The Mara voice is well-maintained. Her Swahili counting under stress ("moja, mbili"), the duck-footed walk implied by "boots decide, not brain," the grape gum as emotional shorthand — these are consistent with the character profile and feel inhabited rather than performed. The line "I pay with my last twenty and the chrome flake I found stuck to my boot" is quietly excellent: it establishes financial desperation, the alien artifact's casual integration into daily life, and Mara's pragmatism in one beat. The phone call with Sammi and Peter is the chapter's emotional core and mostly works. "She slept in the bathtub last night, said the walls didn't shake there" is genuinely affecting — a child's practical solution to an incomprehensible problem. The sync between Sammi's hum and Mara's wound is handled with appropriate restraint; the chapter doesn't over-explain it. The plan called for "one deliberate grammar-wreck per chapter" — this appears in the dream sequence's fragmented syntax and the clipped phone dialogue, though it's subtle enough that it reads as style rather than error. WEAKNESSES: Some metaphors don't fully land. "Steady as a death vow" for the 17 Hz reading is reaching — a death vow isn't particularly steady, and the phrase feels imported from a different register. "Three coins pressed to the planet's eye" for the rings is similarly strained; it's trying for cosmic weight but the image is muddled (coins pressed to an eye is uncomfortable rather than ominous). "Skin pinched silver like I'm part zipper" is clever but slightly too clever — it calls attention to itself. The chapter plan specified Mara should "steal dockyard oscilloscope" — instead she brings her own handheld and Hector reads it. This is a minor deviation but the plan's "unlikable choice" for this chapter was meant to be the theft; instead we get paying Hector with the chrome scale (which is more ambiguous than theft) and lying to Deek about pay. The lying to Deek is present but underdeveloped — it's mentioned almost in passing rather than given the weight the plan intended. The dream sequence ("fish swimming mid-air, each wearing my face small and scared") is the chapter's weakest moment — it's the kind of portentous dream imagery that feels like a shortcut to significance. The plan warned against "adjective piles" and "tidy metaphors," and the dream nearly violates both. The ending is strong: "teaching me the shape of a mouth that hasn't yet spoken" is earned because it's been built toward, and it captures the chapter's central tension between communication and silence without over-resolving it. Dialogue is sparse but functional. Hector's voice is distinct ("That waveform's got hips" from the plan doesn't appear, but his actual lines carry similar energy). Peter's cardboard-dust voice comes through in the clipped phone exchange. The chapter successfully advances the plot (17 Hz confirmed, Sammi's connection established, financial pressure mounting) while maintaining the atmospheric dread the plan calls for. The "unlikable choice" is present but softer than intended — lying to Deek about pay, paying Hector with the alien scale. The chapter follows its plan reasonably well, though the oscilloscope theft became a trade, and the "dead anchovies orient, mirrors flashing like disco" experiment from the plan is absent. Overall this is accomplished literary fiction with occasional overreach — the kind of writing that's genuinely good but not quite at the masterpiece level the plan aspires to. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The fuel card declines at the dock pump—one beep, red light, done. I swipe again like a prayer, same answer. Inside the shack the kid behind glass shrugs: bank flagged the boat account. Quarantine hold, they said. I sign a paper promise for fifty gallons, he shakes his head—new rules. So I walk. Water in the harbor smacks the pilings, flat and angry. The hum rides each slap, passes through the soles of my boots, up the bones to the cut that won’t close. Tape’s gone grey, edges frayed. Underneath the skin shines silver when light hits—like someone started to turn me into a mirror and walked off mid-job. I detour past Hector’s. He’s closed, blinds down, oscilloscope probably humming to itself. A note on the door: OUT—BACK AT DUSK. I tear it down, fold it small, drop it in the trash because that’s the kind of petty I can afford. At the boat Deek’s sitting on the rail, whittling a squid jig. “No diesel, no deal,” he says without looking up. His knife flicks, curls of white plastic fall like fish scales. I tell him I’m working on it. He snorts, gold tooth catching light. “Work faster, Skip. Rent ate my last cheque.” I want to punch the tooth, kiss it, something. Instead I climb aboard, check the livewell—still half full of sphere water I can’t dump because the harbour master banned over-side rinse. The fingerlings inside flash, schooling tight, writing my silhouette on their chrome sides. My phone buzzes. Peter. I let it ring out. Text follows: DOCTOR TONIGHT. HUMMING WORSE. BLOOD FROM NOSE. I stare at the words until they blur, then thumb: ON MY WAY. Lie number three before lunch. I need money fast. The ice-house manager offered cash for live “specimens” yesterday—off-books. I told him no. Now I unlock the tackle locker, pull the five-gallon bucket of sphere water. Fingerlings dart, panicked. I scoop one with a bait net; it flips, mirror catching sun, throws a slice of light across my cheek warm as a hand. I drop it in a pickle jar punched with nail holes, add seawater, screw tight. One down. Six more follow. The jar looks like bottled stars. My stomach turns—selling what I don’t understand to pay for what I can’t keep. I drive the pickup north on 101, windows cracked to hide fish smell. The road hums—different key, faster, but still that same family of throb. I match it with tongue against teeth. Mile marker signs flick past, each a debit against daylight I don’t have. Buyer’s a feed-store clerk with side hustle in aquarium exotics. He meets me by the dumpster, apron smelling of chick starter. I open the jar; fingerlings flicker. His eyes go wide, pupils eating colour. “What species?” I shrug. “Deep-water.” He offers two hundred for the lot. I say five. We settle three-fifty and a promise of more tomorrow. He hands me cash—crisp twenties that smell of hay. I take them, feel heavier, not richer. As I leave he’s already on the phone, voice high with greed that will probably kill him. Part of me hopes it does. Back in Crescent City I hit the fuel dock. Same kid, different shift. Cash talks; he clicks the pump alive, no questions. I fill both tanks, add jerry cans for luck. Three hundred gone. I buy a six-pack of cheap beer and a bag of grape gum—ritual, offering, bribe. The cut under the tape itches, wet. I don’t look. Dusk by the time I reach Peter’s place—a duplex squatting behind a pawn shop. Sammi’s bike lies on its side, front wheel spinning slow. I ring bell. He opens, eyes red. No hello. He steps aside, points to bathroom. Sammi sits on the tub edge, head between knees, blood drops polka-dotting her pajama knees. She looks up, smiles through red teeth. “Mom, you’re late.” I kneel, press tissue to her nose. The room spins—tile, fluorescent, the faint 17-Hz riding the pipes. I feel it in my wrist, in her pulse where my fingers rest. Sync. She hums three notes; the blood flow eases. Peter watches from doorway, arms crossed so tight his knuckles blanch. “We’re taking her to ER,” he says. “They’ll say what they said,” I answer. “At least there it’s documented.” I want to argue, count reasons, but Sammi grips my sleeve. “Don’t fight. Sing.” So I do—off key, stupid lullaby my mum used while shelling peas. Sammi hums under, over, through. The fluorescent tube flickers once, twice, steadies. Blood stops. She breathes clean. Peter drives us back to the boat—his car, because mine smells like the sea’s dirty secret. No one speaks. Sammi holds the jar I hadn’t noticed she grabbed—one fingerling left inside, flashing Morse none of us know. Peter idles at dock gate. “Tomorrow I file for full custody,” he says, voice flat. I nod, no fight left. “Tomorrow,” I echo, knowing tomorrow starts in five hours. Sammi climbs aboard Wanjiku like it’s a playground. I tuck her in V-berth, grape gum on her tongue, blood still crusted under her nose. She whispers, “They want the water’s name.” I kiss her forehead, taste salt. “Sleep, loudspeaker.” She does, humming fading into snore. I sit in the dark galley, beer unopened. The engine waits below, tanks full, livewell pregnant with starlight. My palm pulses silver. I count twenties on the table—fifty left, enough for one more trip. Outside, filament on the horizon glows, fishing for fools. I finish the count aloud. “Kumi na moja…” don’t know what comes next. Engine ticks, cooling. Somewhere a foghorn answers the seventeen, low brother calling home. I unzip the chart, trace a line past the three-mile limit where rules thin. Finger lingers on blank blue. The cut hums approval. I close the logbook without writing a word—some days you don’t record sins, you just sail them.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 of this first-contact story demonstrates considerable craft and largely delivers on the ambitious plan laid out. Let me assess it systematically. STRENGTHS: The voice is consistent and convincing throughout. Mara's interiority feels earned rather than performed—"I take them, feel heavier, not richer" is exactly the kind of compressed moral accounting the plan called for. The Swahili counting at the end ("Kumi na moja…") lands naturally as a character tic rather than an affectation. The plan specified "one deliberate grammar-wreck per chapter to keep breath in," and the chapter delivers this subtly through sentence fragments and rhythm breaks. The "one unlikable choice per chapter" is well-executed here: selling the fingerlings to the aquarium buyer is genuinely uncomfortable, and the line "Part of me hopes it does" (about the buyer's greed killing him) is a sharp, honest moment of moral ugliness that doesn't editorialize. This is the chapter's best moment—it shows Mara's complicity without excusing it. The sensory work is mostly strong. "Crisp twenties that smell of hay" is specific and unexpected. The fingerling jar as "bottled stars" is a touch precious but earns its keep. The 17-Hz throb threading through pipes and syncing with Sammi's pulse is handled with restraint. The Sammi scene is the emotional core and largely succeeds. She's rendered as a real child—"Mom, you're late," blood on her pajama knees, grape gum—not an oracle. The lullaby stopping the nosebleed is appropriately ambiguous: is it the frequency, the comfort, coincidence? The chapter wisely doesn't resolve this. WEAKNESSES: Some metaphors don't fully land. "Water in the harbor smacks the pilings, flat and angry" is competent but generic. "Fingerlings dart, panicked" followed by "mirror catching sun, throws a slice of light across my cheek warm as a hand" is the chapter's most forced moment—the simile feels imported from a different, softer story. "Livewell pregnant with starlight" is the kind of phrase the plan explicitly warned against (tidy metaphors, adjective piles), and it's the chapter's most purple moment. The plan called for "Hmong squid buyer" specifically, but the chapter substitutes a generic "feed-store clerk with side hustle in aquarium exotics." This is a meaningful deviation—the Hmong buyer was part of the regional specificity the plan emphasized. The replacement is less textured. Peter's dialogue is functional but thin. "Tomorrow I file for full custody" is exactly what you'd expect him to say, and the exchange doesn't reveal anything beyond the obvious. The plan's Peter is more complex—the safety-manual quoter, the jaw-clicker—and he's somewhat flattened here into a custody-threat delivery mechanism. "Filament on the horizon glows, fishing for fools" is a bit too neat, too self-consciously literary. Similarly, "some days you don't record sins, you just sail them" is the kind of closing aphorism that the plan warned against—it resolves rather than leaves open. The chapter follows the plan's structure closely: bank freeze, crew tension, selling specimens, Sammi's nosebleed, custody threat, the livewell. The bribe with copper filings mentioned in the plan doesn't appear (replaced by cash at the fuel dock), and the harbour master fee scene is compressed into a single reference. These are minor deviations. The dialogue is generally serviceable but occasionally on-the-nose. Sammi's "They want the water's name" is the chapter's most oracle-adjacent moment—the plan specifically warned against the precocious child trope, and this line edges toward it, though it's saved somewhat by Mara's dismissive response. OVERALL: A strong chapter with genuine voice and emotional intelligence, marred by occasional lapses into the very tendencies the plan warned against. The moral texture is the chapter's greatest achievement; the closing aphorism and a few forced metaphors are its main failures. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 13 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The coast-guard tape across the harbour mouth flaps orange in the dawn wind, but there’s no cutter, just a loud-hailer on repeat: economic trial licence, proceed at own risk. I slice the tape with the boat-hook and push Wanjiku through the gap. Sammi stands beside me wearing a life-jacket two sizes too big, sleeves flapping like fledgling wings. She wanted to come; I hadn’t the strength to lie that I’d be back soon. Peter will wake to a text he can’t answer until land. Engine mutters full, tanks still grateful for last night’s cash. Deek didn’t show—probably found steadier wages. I run solo plus kid, illegal and necessary. Sea flat, colour of old tin. The hum rides the deckboards, gentle today, coaxing. I give Sammi the wheel while I ready the bathy-capsule—really a retro-fitted bait tank with a plexi dome, winch cable, and a Navy hand-me-down scrubber I stripped from junkyard. Hector soldered fresh leads yesterday, traded for two jars of mirror-fingerlings he wants to “breed.” I told him they die in tap-water; he said everything dies somewhere. Sammi hums the three-note hook, off-key but close enough the cut in my palm answers warm. I check the bandage—tape grey, skin underneath shining like foil pressed to flesh. She watches, eyes wide-not-scared. “It’s growing, Mum.” “Just scar tissue, loudspeaker.” She pats my arm, forgiving the lie. We motor five miles south-west, depth reading two hundred. The filament showed here last evening, a hair of light dropping through water you could read newsprint through. I throttle to neutral, let drift. Silence except idling engine and Sammi’s soft hum. I lift the capsule, swing it over the side. It dangles like a glass egg, big enough for one adult and one seven-year-old if we breathe skinny. Sammi climbs in first, knees to chin. I follow, pull hatch dogged. Air tastes of plastic and old prawn. I switch on scrubber fan, hand her the LED torch. She flicks it, makes puppet shadows on the dome. Winch groans, cable paying. We sink. Green folds over us, then grey. At thirty feet the daylight thins to bruise. Sammi presses nose to plexi, breath fogging. Fish flick silver, normal, alive—relief I didn’t expect stings my eyes. Seventy feet—temperature drops, gooseflesh through sleeves. The hum deepens, felt in balls of feet. Ninety—depth needle quivers, fan rattles. I slow descent. A shape ahead: filament, glowing pale blue, no thicker than fishing line yet it draws the eye like fire in dry grass. We hover. I thumb the exterior mic; inside speaker pops. The seventeen-hertz washes through, gentle giant heartbeat. Sammi turns, small teeth white. “It wants the rest of the song.” I don’t know the rest. She does. She leans forward, puts lips to plexi, hums her three notes then adds two more I’ve never heard—descent, rise, fall. Perfect fifth, minor fall, major lift—like lament and lullaby stitched. The filament answers. It flares, blooms outward into a sphere of seawater the size of a truck, suspended inside the sea. Fish within it swim upside down, chrome-sided, mirror to mirror. Our capsule tilts, drawn by micro-current. I grip the release, ready to jettison ballast, but Sammi places her hand on mine. “Look, Mum.” Inside the sphere the fingerlings school, writing shapes—first a helix, then a crude outline of Wanjiku, then a woman’s profile that could be mine if I still smiled. I taste metal, whisper gran’s hymn. The sphere pulses once, contracts, pushing a pressure wave that rocks us gentle, almost loving. Sammi’s nose bleeds. Red beads drift in the dome air, tiny planets. She doesn’t wipe, just keeps humming. The sphere folds inward, swallowing itself until only a pinpoint of light remains. It darts forward, kisses the plexi in front of her face—then gone. Darkness rushes back like curtains after show. I hit the drop release, weights fall, we shoot upward. Daylight smears green, then white. We breach surface, capsule rolling. I crack hatch, haul us onto deck. Sammi’s blood decorates her life-jacket in perfect circles. She grins through it. “I gave it my name.” Engine starts first turn, obedient now. I throttle hard for home, don’t bother with the nets. No one speaks. Harbour entrance appears, tape still flapping, louder in wind. My palm burns silver. Sammi hums softly, adding the two new notes, teaching the sea a song it never paid for. I tie lines, lift her to pier. Blood has dried on her lip like dark confetti. She pats my cheek. “It’ll come back. It’s learning.” I want to tell her some things aren’t lessons, but words taste of copper and sail off. Behind us the water shows no scar, only the usual chop slapping hulls. Yet every hull hums faint, tuning fork struck by a hand we can’t see. I sling the empty capsule, lock the wheelhouse. Sammi’s hand in mine is warm, sticky, ordinary. We walk toward the parking lot where Peter will be waiting, face full of custody and thunder. My cut pulses with each step, keeping time, keeping count, writing music under skin I no longer own.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents a strong execution of the writing plan, with several genuinely accomplished moments and a few places where the craft slips. The opening is economical and effective: the coast-guard tape sliced with a boat-hook, the text Peter can't answer until land—these are concrete, character-revealing details that do multiple jobs at once. The "life-jacket two sizes too big, sleeves flapping like fledgling wings" is a clean image that earns its slight poeticism. The chapter establishes stakes (illegal, solo, kid aboard) without belaboring them. The bathy-capsule sequence is the chapter's centerpiece and largely works. The descent is rendered with appropriate sensory texture—temperature drops, gooseflesh, the hum felt in the balls of feet. The filament encounter is handled with appropriate restraint; we get the sphere, the chrome-sided fish, the shapes they write, without over-explanation. The plan called for Sammi to sing three off-key notes and the filament to bloom a sphere—this is delivered, with the addition of two new notes that become a recurring motif. This is good craft. However, there are problems. The line "perfect fifth, minor fall, major lift—like lament and lullaby stitched" is the chapter's most egregious purple moment—it's the kind of over-written musical description that tells us how to feel rather than letting us feel it, and the "lament and lullaby stitched" construction is a cliché dressed in technical clothing. The plan specifically warned against "tidy metaphors" and this is one. "Darkness rushes back like curtains after show" is a simile that almost works but lands slightly flat—the theatrical metaphor feels imported from a different register. The fish writing shapes—a helix, Wanjiku's outline, a woman's profile—is the chapter's most creative moment and is handled with appropriate brevity. The plan called for this and the execution is restrained enough to avoid becoming precious. Sammi's characterization is largely consistent with her profile: she's not oracular, she's a kid with a nosebleed who grins through blood and says "I gave it my name"—this is good, grounded child dialogue. "It'll come back. It's learning" is slightly more oracular than the plan intended, but it's within acceptable range for a seven-year-old's magical thinking. The ending paragraph is the chapter's weakest section. "Writing music under skin I no longer own" is the kind of closing line that reaches for profundity and lands in purple territory. The plan called for Mara to scream mayday though the radio is dead—this doesn't happen. Instead the chapter ends with a walk to the parking lot. The plan also specified Sammi's nose "pours blood" and Mara "stuffs it with tissue, screams mayday"—the chapter softens both: blood "beads drift in dome air" and there's no mayday. This is a meaningful deviation from the plan, though the chapter's version is arguably more tonally consistent. The "unlikable choice" per chapter (plan requirement) is present but weak—she brought Sammi illegally, which is more desperate than unlikable. The plan's version had a clearer moral compromise. The line "I taste metal, whisper gran's hymn" is effective—economical, sensory, character-consistent. The palm that "burns silver" and "pulses with each step, keeping time, keeping count" is slightly over-worked by the chapter's end. Dialogue is sparse and mostly functional. "It wants the rest of the song" is slightly too knowing for the plan's stated goal of avoiding the precocious child trope, but it's borderline. "Your boat sneezed—didn't you hear it?" (from the profile) is better than what we get here. Coherence is strong—the chapter follows a clear arc and the underwater sequence is spatially legible. The capsule device is a clever solution to getting them underwater without scuba gear. Overall: a chapter that executes its plan with genuine craft in places, some purple overreach at key emotional moments, and a slightly softened ending compared to the plan's more visceral version. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 7 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 8 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 12 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Peter’s car is gone from the lot when we get back—just an oil stain and a text: *Taking her straight to ER. You’ll hear from my lawyer.* Sammi waves the phone like a flag she doesn’t know is white. I read it twice, delete the number from the screen, not from life. Inside the galley I pour her a bowl of cereal with powdered milk because the fridge quit when the dock lost power last night. She hums while crunching, blood crusted under one nostril flaking into the bowl. I don’t stop her. The notes are different now—five instead of three, a melody that ends on a question. My palm answers under the bandage, itchy, hot. I rinse dishes, lose time watching water spiral. The faucet coughs air, then a gush that smells faintly of kiwi left in the sun. Harbour master’s bullhorn drifts past: boil advisory, fish disposal ban, stay indoors. I shut the window, bullhorn keeps talking to wood I can’t afford to replace. There’s a knock—Hector, hoodie dark with fog, oscilloscope slung like a newborn. “You got live samples?” he asks before hello. I point at the livewell. He peers in, sees the chrome fry schooling in the shape of a helix, almost falls backward. “Madre.” I hand him a pickle jar. He scoops four, cap shaking. “Trade for batteries?” I nod. Information is currency; batteries are oxygen. He sets rig on my table: laptop, leads, tiny amp. Drops a fingerling on contacts—mirror skin completes circuit, scope sings seventeen hertz pure. Sammi leans in, nose almost touching screen. She taps two more notes. The wave bends, adds harmonics like shoulders. Hector’s eyes fill—engineer’s tears, rare but real. “They’re not fish,” he whispers. “They’re letters.” I feel the sentence crawl under my skin. He leaves batteries, takes jar. Deal done. Sammi watches him go, then opens palm: one mirror flake she palmed from the table. Offers it to me like allowance candy. I take it, pocket. It nestles against the first, warm as a coin spent and returned. Afternoon, the sphere returns—not at sea, in the livewell. Water peels upward, forms a basketball of itself hovering above the surface. Inside, fry write new shapes: first a trawler outline, then my profile, then Sammi’s smaller one tucked beneath. She laughs. I smell ozone, feel hair lift. The sphere collapses, splashes, leaves salt pattern on overhead beam—constellation I don’t recognise. Peter texts a photo: Sammi in hospital bed, IV in hand, nose packed white. Caption: *DOING WHAT YOU WON’T.* I show her. She shrugs, peels Band-Aid off arm where no needle was. “They couldn’t hear me,” she says. “So I stopped.” Evening, city power flickers. Streetlights blink like dying stars. The rings above pulse once, synchronised, and every chrome flake in town answers—gutters, rooftops, pockets, all flashing Morse nobody taught us. People step outside despite curfew, faces silvered, eyes round. No one speaks. The hum lowers, feels like warm hand on collarbone. I lock the hatch, pull curtains. Sammi hums softer, teaching air the new ending. I join on the second loop—voice cracked, off-key, but together we finish on major lift. Lights steady. Cut in my palm cools, edges sealing glassy. I know tomorrow I’ll give her back to land, to laws, to safer rooms that don’t vibrate. Tonight she’s here, mouth milk-mustached, song spilling, blood drying ordinary. I pour the last of the powdered milk into a cup, stir with a screwdriver because spoons are dirty. The liquid spins a tiny galaxy. I watch, count seconds, lose count, start over. Outside, the livewell gurgles—letters spelling water’s name, and we’re learning to spell back, one cracked note at a time.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 is a strong piece of compressed, sensory-driven prose that largely delivers on the story's ambitious intentions. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter opens with economy and emotional precision. "Peter's car is gone from the lot when we get back—just an oil stain and a text" is exactly the kind of concrete, unglamorous detail the plan called for. The oil stain as absence is earned, not forced. The line "I read it twice, delete the number from the screen, not from life" is genuinely good—it does real emotional work without announcing itself. The sensory spine holds. The faucet smelling of "kiwi left in the sun" is specific and strange in the right way. The powdered milk stirred with a screwdriver is the kind of bodily inconvenience the plan demanded, and it lands naturally rather than feeling inserted for texture. Hector's scene works well. His characterization is consistent with his profile—the engineer's tears, the "They're not letters" revelation, the battery trade. The oscilloscope moment with the fingerling completing a circuit at 17 Hz is the chapter's best science-fictional beat: it's weird, it's grounded in the established world, and it advances the alien-as-archive theme without over-explaining. The sphere returning to the livewell and drawing the trawler outline, then Mara's profile, then Sammi's "smaller one tucked beneath" is quietly devastating. It shows the aliens observing, cataloguing, perhaps caring—without stating any of that. This is the chapter's most successful show-don't-tell moment. The Peter photo text—"DOING WHAT YOU WON'T"—and Sammi's response ("They couldn't hear me. So I stopped.") is the chapter's best dialogue exchange. It's cryptic in the right way, suggesting Sammi's experience at the hospital was its own kind of contact attempt, without spelling it out. WEAKNESSES: The chapter plan called for a 3 a.m. filament tapping the window, a voice in Mara's own timbre saying "Trade the song, keep the sea," and Mara refusing and breaking the window with a skillet. None of this happens. The chapter substitutes a more diffuse evening sequence with the rings pulsing and chrome flakes flashing Morse. This is a significant deviation—the plan's central dramatic beat (the offer, the refusal, the wound bleeding silver) is absent. The chapter ends on a quieter, more domestic note, which has its own merit but loses the confrontational tension the plan promised. The "unlikable choice" per chapter is also softer here than planned. The plan specified Mara drinking until she tastes iron; instead she just locks the hatch and sings with Sammi. The moral complexity is muted. Some metaphors strain. "The wave bends, adds harmonics like shoulders" is awkward—shoulders don't add harmonics in any intuitive sense. "Information is currency; batteries are oxygen" is a bit too aphoristic, too clean for this narrator's voice. "The liquid spins a tiny galaxy" in the final paragraph is the kind of tidy cosmic metaphor the plan explicitly said to avoid. The ending paragraph with the screwdriver and the galaxy is the weakest moment—it reaches for profundity and lands on cliché. "Letters spelling water's name, and we're learning to spell back, one cracked note at a time" is the sort of closing line that sounds resonant but dissolves under pressure. "One cracked note at a time" is particularly weak, a familiar rhythm dressed in new clothes. The chapter's pacing is slightly uneven. The evening sequence with the synchronized pulse and people stepping outside feels rushed—it's a potentially major moment (global synchronization, public contact) compressed into three sentences. The domestic scenes get more space than this arguably more significant beat. Sammi's characterization is mostly consistent—she's not oracular, she's a kid who shrugs at a hospital photo and peels off a Band-Aid. But "They couldn't hear me. So I stopped" edges toward the precocious-child territory the plan warned against. The chapter does maintain the eco-gothic atmosphere and the first-person present tense effectively. The prose is generally clean and salt-stained as intended. The complicity theme is present but understated—Mara's guilt about her fishing past doesn't surface here. OVERALL: A competent, often genuinely good chapter that loses points for failing to execute its own plan's central dramatic sequence (the offer and refusal), for a weak closing line, and for occasional metaphors that don't quite land. The best moments are very good; the worst are merely adequate. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 8 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The note under the wiper says: *We torch tonight. Bring what you owe. – D* I crunch it, stuff it in pocket with chrome flakes that keep reproducing like foil bunnies. Deek’s handwriting—blocky, always caps, like he’s shouting on paper. He remembers nothing of the sphere that erased him, but his body does; scar on his wrist shaped like a perfect O where the light kissed. He wants answers, payment, revenge—he’ll settle for fire. Sammi’s at Peter’s—court order came fast, stamped and cold. I watched her pack the snow-globe, fingerlings jar, grape gum. She didn’t cry, just hummed the five-note line until security told her quiet. I drove back to the boat tasting iron, heart hammering seventeen. Alone now, and broke in every way money can’t measure. Deek shows at dusk with two others: Lace, ex-diver with busted eardrums who speaks in whispers, and a kid called Fuse who once torched a cop car for fun and kept the melted badge. They carry backpacks, acetone, road flares, a battered scuba tank rigged to blow. I should call it off. I pour coffee instead, add rum to kill the smell of fear. We drink in silence, steam mixing with harbour sodium lights. Plan: dive to the filament where it threads the pier piling, douse with acetate, spark flare, capture burning on phones—proof, spectacle, maybe exorcism. Deek believes fire resets everything. I believe fire eats what you can’t pay for. But debt is debt and I owe him a life he doesn’t recall losing. We load the dinghy, motor slow under taped-off gate. Water glass-black, reflecting rings that pulse once—heartbeat. No wind, no voices, just the low throb in hull and bone. I steer by memory of where the sphere first kissed hull. Lace taps my shoulder, points down: faint blue thread glowing vertical, same diameter as mooring line, innocent. Fuse straps goggles, teeth grin white. Deek unscrews cap, acetone stink sharp enough to peel paint. I feel the cut in palm answer—hum turns hot, skin stretching. I should speak, warn, apologise. Instead I hand him the striker. He douses, flare sparks, world goes white-orange. Flame races down the filament like fuse on cartoon bomb. For half a breath nothing—then water sucks. A sphere blooms, bigger than boat, mirror-bright. Lace screams—a sound lost in vacuum pop. Light folds, eats Deek boots-first. He doesn’t even look scared, just confused, then gone—boots left, laces smoking. Sphere collapses to pinpoint. Shock-wave slams dinghy, flips us. I hit water, cold knifing. Under surface everything silent, humming. I see the others—Fuse flailing, Lace clawing ears bleeding bubbles. Fingerlings of chrome appear, school around them. Lace’s eyes film white, body goes slack, drifts downward like dropped laundry. Fuse tries to swim up; fry surround, write his outline in light. He vanishes—no splash, no scream, just erased. I wait for the same, count heartbeats—one, two—cut burns cold now. Fry approach, circle, but don’t touch. They write a shape: helix, then trawler, then Sammi’s small profile. My chest hitches, lungs begging. I reach, touch the outline of her. Fry scatter, let me go. I kick upward, break surface gasping, alone. Flares hiss on water, dying orange. No bodies, no boots, just acetone slick rainbowing. I tread, crying without sound, salt stinging split lip. The filament reappears, intact, bluer than before. It pulses once—message received, signature recorded. I scream at it, get a mouthful of petrol rainbow. Dinghy upside-down. I right her, climb, shiver so hard teeth chip. Engine still attached, pulls first tug. I throttle full, don’t look back. Harbour gate lights swim in vision. I expect sirens, alarms, something. Only quiet follows—the kind that tells you the world just finished editing three people and kept the footage. I tie up, vomit over rail—purple coffee, rum, guilt. Hands shake too hard to hold hose so I sit on deck, let diesel air dry me. Every plank hums seventeen, louder than before, like amplifier gained three lives. I find Lace’s wrench left earlier, hurl it into dark. Clank, splash, silence. Inside, I write their names on the shit-list bulkhead: Deek, Lace, Fuse. Only Deek already had a line—now two. The pencil snaps. I scratch the names again with knife tip, dig into plywood. Blood from palm smears over them, silver mixing red. I’m still carving when the sun comes up, pale and ashamed. Phone vibrates—Peter. I let it. Text follows: COURT 9 AM. DON’T BOTHER. I laugh, sound like breaking Styrofoam. I open logbook, date it, write: Crew gone. Fault mine. No witness. Then I close the book—some sins you don’t log, you just carry. I start the engine, let it idle, feel the new vibration—three heartbeats short. I consider pointing the bow at open water and never turning. Instead I kill throttle, go below, pack a bag with grape gum, charts, and the snow-globe Sammi forgot. The court can take the boat, the house, the name—but the song stays, and it wants paying. I sit in the dark galley, hand on throttle, waiting for enough courage or too much fear. Outside, the livewell gurgles, fry multiplying under a light that shouldn’t burn this bright. I listen to the hum count coins I can’t return, and I know tonight I became the archive’s newest page—written in three lives, signed in silver.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is doing a great deal right, and some things imperfectly. Let me work through it carefully. **Strengths:** The chapter opens with a strong, economical hook—the note under the wiper, the crumpled chrome flakes "reproducing like foil bunnies." That image is genuinely good: domestic, slightly absurd, and unsettling in the right register. The chapter maintains the salt-stained, short-sentence voice established in the plan throughout, and it rarely lapses into adjective piles. The disappearance of Deek, Lace, and Fuse is handled with real craft. "He doesn't even look scared, just confused, then gone—boots left, laces smoking" is excellent—it echoes the plan's "boots left, still smoking" but earns it through the confusion detail. The underwater sequence where the fry write Sammi's silhouette is the chapter's strongest passage: it's strange, earned, and emotionally resonant without being sentimental. The fry letting Mara go because she touches Sammi's outline is a clean piece of alien logic that doesn't over-explain itself. The guilt mechanics work. Mara hands Deek the striker when she should speak—this is the "one unlikable choice per chapter" the plan demanded, and it's properly uncomfortable. She doesn't heroically refuse; she participates. The aftermath—vomiting, carving names with a knife, blood mixing silver—is appropriately bleak without being melodramatic. The ending paragraph is strong: "I listen to the hum count coins I can't return, and I know tonight I became the archive's newest page—written in three lives, signed in silver." This is the chapter's most overtly "poetic" line, and it mostly lands because "archive" has been established as the alien's apparent function, so the metaphor is earned rather than decorative. **Weaknesses:** The chapter introduces Lace and Fuse as new characters with almost no establishment. Lace gets "ex-diver with busted eardrums who speaks in whispers" and Fuse gets "once torched a cop car for fun and kept the melted badge." These are efficient but thin—they exist to be erased, and the reader knows it immediately. The plan called for Deek to plan the burning and for Mara to help, but the plan also said "crew wants to tow the livewell sphere to scientists for big payout; first mate Deek plans to burn filament with acetylene." The chapter deviates by introducing two new characters who weren't in the plan, which dilutes the impact of Deek's erasure specifically. The plan's note that "no one remembers him except her" is partially honored but muddied by the fact that Lace and Fuse also vanish—the "no one remembers Deek" element loses its specificity. The plan specified Mara "steals their wallets from galley, mails cash to their families, scuttles rest of fuel." This doesn't happen in the chapter—she vomits, carves names, and sits in the dark. This is a meaningful omission: the wallet-stealing was meant to be the "one unlikable choice" (petty theft with a guilty conscience), and its absence means the chapter's moral texture is slightly less complex than intended. "Shock-wave slams dinghy, flips us" is slightly clunky—the transition from fire to sphere to erasure happens very fast, and the pacing feels rushed in the middle section. The underwater sequence recovers this, but the actual moment of Deek's disappearance could use one more beat. "I laugh, sound like breaking Styrofoam" is a good simile. "Heart hammering seventeen" (the frequency) is clever shorthand. "Pale and ashamed" applied to the sun is a minor purple moment—the sun doesn't need to be ashamed; Mara's shame is already present. The dialogue is minimal (one note, one text), which suits the chapter's isolation theme. The plan called for Mara to steal wallets and mail cash—this would have been a more active, morally complex beat than what we get. The chapter ends on interiority rather than action, which is consistent with the voice but slightly less dynamic than the plan envisioned. The coherence is generally strong. The chapter follows logically from the established world. The "no one remembers him except her" element from the plan is present but not fully developed—we don't see anyone else failing to remember Deek, we just have Mara's grief. Overall: a strong chapter with genuine craft, some rushed middle-section pacing, a deviation from the plan's specific beats (wallet theft, solo Deek focus), and two thin new characters who exist only to be erased. The voice is consistent, the alien logic is coherent, and the emotional core lands. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 14 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 14 Followed Chapter Plan: 11 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The harbour is flat steel at 3 a.m., every light doubled in the water like the world’s been photocopied wrong. I sit on Wanjiku’s rail barefoot, engine idling, wake licking the pilings. The cut in my palm pulses seventeen, steady as a countdown I can’t read. Sammi’s gone—court order, police escort, her humming fading down the pier like a radio losing station. I drank the last beer, then the last rum; now I’m drinking cold coffee that tastes of bilge and copper. When the filament comes it doesn’t ask. A thread of blue drops from low cloud, slips between masts, finds my bow cleat, wraps once. No wind, no sound except my heart trying to learn the hum. The line thickens, lifts the bow six inches—gentle, impossible. Water peels away from the hull like skin off milk. I should cut the rope, throttle out, call someone. Instead I step forward, let it take my weight. The boat rises slow. The harbour falls away—diesel rainbows, floating beer cans, the dumpster where Deek’s boots sit sole-up and empty. Everything shrinks to toy, then to nothing. Air goes quiet, no gulls, no horns, just the low throb inside my bones. I pass the pier light at eye level, then above it, then the roofline of the cannery. The filament glows, not bright—moonlight through thin ice. At twenty feet up the boat stops. Water below forms a bowl, held by nothing. Inside the bowl fish swim upside-down, chrome as dimes. They school, make shapes: helix, trawler, Sammi’s profile small and precise. My throat locks. I whisper her name; the fish write it in cursive, then erase. A second thread descends, touches the deck beside my foot. It doesn’t burn. I feel pressure, invitation, debt. I place my bleeding palm against it. Silver skin splits, blood beads perfect spheres that float an inch off wood, mirror-bright. The filament drinks them, pulses once—red to blue to white. Inside my head a frequency opens, wider than sound. I see: Dad’s bar in Mombasa, fan clicking, warm beer smell. Gran counting beans, click click. Sammi born, purple, yelling until I hum. Peter signing papers, pen scratching like gull feet on roof. Deek’s boots empty, laces smoking. My own face in a chrome scale, younger, certain. The images stack, compress, become a weight I can’t shoulder. I understand nothing except the price: keep the record or be deleted from it. I offer what’s left—my voice. I sing the lullaby, cracked, off-key, every note a mistake I own. The threads shiver, braid together, form a rope no thicker than anchor line but luminous. It loops my wrist, not tight, a bracelet of cold light. Then slack. Choice, not chain. Below, the mirrored fish school faster, writing new shape: my trawler again, but smaller, and beside it a girl’s outline growing. They want the next generation, the echo. I shake my head. “Not her.” They pause, swirl, make the helix—life continuing. I taste metal, speak clear: “Take me. Archive me. Leave her noise alone.” The rope brightens, tests my weight. I step off the rail. Air holds. I dangle, barefoot, boat above, void below. Fear is a fish-hook in gut, but lighter than guilt. I close eyes, keep singing, same three notes Gran used, same five Sammi added, no more because that’s all we had. Light climbs my arm, shoulder, throat. It doesn’t hurt; it inventories. Every scar, every lie, every net dragged too low. I feel the count—moja, mbili, tatu—up to numbers I never learned. When it reaches my heart it pauses, listens to the skip where cochlea broke. Then continues, gentle, thorough. I think of Sammi in a bathtub inland, humming to tiles that don’t answer. I give the light that picture. It copies, stores, moves on. Time stretches, folds. I lose the edges of me—where boots end, where breath begins. I’m spread so thin I can see the curve of the planet, the rings above patient as sundials. They are not gods, not machines—just filing cabinets with appetite. And I am the latest folder, flapping open. A final pulse. The rope releases. I fall—not far, just enough for knees to buckle as deck meets feet. Boat bobs, water rushes back, fills the bowl it left. No splash, more like breath inhaled after long dive. The filament loosens from cleat, slides skyward, dimming to thread, to hair, to nothing. Sky stays black, but the hum inside my bones stops. Silence so complete I hear blood scraping vein walls. I sit hard, legs useless. The bracelet on my wrist has cooled, solidified—mirror strip flush to skin, no clasp, no seam. When I move it catches light that isn’t there and throws back images: Deek grinning, Lace whispering, Sammi at seven months fat-cheeked. A private cinema I never asked for. Engine waits. I key, she catches first turn—obedient now, like something apologised for. Tanks read full though I’ve burned miles. Gauges lie or don’t; I don’t argue. I steer toward harbour, through gap where orange tape still flaps, now shredded by wind I can feel again. Dock is empty, sodium lights fizzing normal. I tie lines, hands working without me. The livewell gurgles. Inside, chrome fry have doubled, schooling the helix nonstop. I scoop one in palm. It doesn’t flip, just lies there reflecting my eye bigger than boat. I release; it rejoins the wheel. Below, I open the log. Pen hovers. I write: Paid. Don’t know balance. Don’t know interest. Then I close the book, shove it deep. In bunk I lie booted, bracelet cold against cheek. Dreams come fast: water rising through floorboards not to drown but to remember. Every scale a page, every gill a line. I read myself aloud—voice steady, someone else’s. I wake before sun, tongue tasting of iron and grape. Engine ticks cooling. Outside, gulls cry normal, fighting over dumpster fries. The world looks copied wrong still, but copied—meaning it continues. I count heartbeats: moja, mbili, tatu—up to ten this time, then past, into numbers I invent. The bracelet keeps tempo, silent now, storage full. I brew coffee, drink it black, no rum. Hand trembles less. I tell the empty galley: “We sail at dawn. Don’t know where, but we sail.” The bracelet flashes once, like wink, like debt. I wink back, taste copper, keep breathing.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents some of the strongest work in the collection, executing the plan's core ambitions with considerable skill. Let me assess it carefully across dimensions. STRENGTHS: The sensory hierarchy (sound first, smell second, sight last) is maintained throughout. The chapter opens with sound—the engine idling, wake licking pilings—before moving to visual. The 17 Hz pulse in the palm is a smart callback that grounds the supernatural in the body. The prose is genuinely salt-stained without being performatively gritty. The flash-inventory sequence ("Dad's bar in Mombasa, fan clicking, warm beer smell...") is one of the chapter's best moments. It's compressed, earned, and avoids the trap of over-explaining. The images stack without commentary, trusting the reader. This is the plan's "archive" concept rendered viscerally rather than abstractly. The alien encounter stays true to the plan's directive: never named, never shown whole, motives opaque. "Just filing cabinets with appetite" is a genuinely good line—deflating the cosmic without being cynical, finding the bureaucratic horror in the sublime. This is the kind of unexpected angle that distinguishes good speculative fiction. Mara's voice is consistent with her profile: the Swahili counting (moja, mbili, tatu) appears naturally, the cochlear damage is woven in ("the skip where cochlea broke"), and her decision to offer herself instead of Sammi is consistent with her established motive structure. The "Not her" moment is earned because we've seen the custody stakes built across chapters. The bracelet as permanent mark—"mirror strip flush to skin, no clasp, no seam"—is a smart physical consequence that avoids the plan's warned-against "magic tech fix" while still giving Mara something changed and unresolved. The ending of the chapter ("I wink back, taste copper, keep breathing") is appropriately unresolved. It doesn't triumph or collapse; it continues. WEAKNESSES: Some metaphors strain. "Fear is a fish-hook in gut, but lighter than guilt" is trying hard and lands awkwardly—the comparison between fear and guilt via weight is a bit mechanical. "Water peels away from the hull like skin off milk" is vivid but slightly overwrought; "skin off milk" is an unusual enough image that it draws attention to itself in a way that doesn't quite pay off. The fish writing Sammi's name in cursive is a beautiful image, but "then erase" feels like a reach for poignancy that tips into the sentimental. The plan warned against "tidy metaphors" and this one is almost too neat. "I'm spread so thin I can see the curve of the planet" is the chapter's weakest moment—it's the kind of cosmic zoom-out that the plan explicitly wanted to avoid, and it briefly loses Mara's grounded interiority for something more generic. The dialogue is minimal (as planned for this chapter), but "Not her" and "Take me. Archive me. Leave her noise alone." work well—direct, in character, not over-written. The plan called for "one unlikable choice per chapter." Here it's arguably the self-offering (which is more heroic than unlikable) or the earlier theft of wallets (which happened in Chapter 6). This chapter is slightly softer on Mara's moral complexity than the plan intended. The Deek reference ("dumpster where Deek's boots sit sole-up and empty") is handled well—present but not belabored, consistent with the plan's note that only Mara remembers him. PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows its plan closely: filament wraps boat, lifts it, water peels from hull, mirror-fish record heartbeat, lullaby sung, mother's voice replayed (though this is compressed into the flash-inventory rather than a direct replay), self-offered instead of Sammi, wound seals chrome, boat drops, engines start first turn. The plan's "grief buckles her knees" is rendered as "A weight I can't shoulder" and the physical collapse on deck—close enough. The bracelet as chrome seal is a nice addition not in the plan but consistent with its spirit. OVERALL: This is accomplished literary speculative fiction. The prose is controlled, the character voice is consistent, the alien encounter is genuinely strange without being arbitrary. Minor overreaches in metaphor and one cosmic zoom-out prevent it from being flawless, but it's substantially above average work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The engine wakes before I do—throat-clear, then steady idle. I lift my head from the chart table, neck stiff, bracelet cold against skin. Coffee dregs look like oil in the bottom of the mug. Through the porthole dawn is colour of old pennies, sky flat, rings still up there—three coins left on the bar after last call. I brew new coffee, add nothing. The harbour master’s notice flaps on the piling: EVAC ZONE EXTENDED – CUSTODY TRANSFER SITE @ 0800. They’ve commandeered the community hall for legal swaps, kids handed like library books. Sammi’s name is on the list. My name is on another: delinquent parent, present-yourself-or-else. I stuff a duffel: charts, torch, grape gum, the snow-globe she forgot. The bracelet counts each item—pulse against bone, silent inventory. I zip, sling, step onto deck. Gulls watch, heads tilted, quiet for once. Maybe the hum taught them manners. Fuel gauge shows full—impossible, but I take gifts where they come. Lines come easy in my hands; the bow filament doesn’t return. Water is just water, slap and sigh. I cast off single-handed, no crew, no witnesses. Engine shifts to forward, Wanjiku leans, hungry for open. Past the breakwall I throttle slow, let her decide speed. The livewell gurgles astern—chrome fry still schooling helix, alive on nothing. I scoop one in palm; it flicks my lifeline, writes a tiny S. I tip it back, keep the S for later. Court time looms. I steer south-west, away from town, toward the blank blue where rules thin. Not running—just choosing the ground. The bracelet warms, approving, or maybe storing mileage. At four miles out I cut engine. Silence falls, huge. I plug the aux cable into the hydrophone Hector rigged, drop transducer. Speaker crackles—normal sea hiss, then underneath: faint heartbeat, seventeen, never left, only whispered. I key the mic, speak. “Trade accepted. Terms mine.” No echo, just heartbeat continuing. Good. Deals with gods-or-whatever should be one-sided and quiet. I open the throttle wide. Bow lifts, spray white as new paint. Speed shows twelve knots—impossible for this hull, but gauge climbs. I don’t argue. Wind tears tears from eyes I thought were dry. At eight miles I see them—chrome fingerlings ahead, jumping like skipped stones, drawing a line across the surface. I follow; the wheel turns easy, like someone else steers. The bracelet flashes sun, spells memories I can’t stop: Sammi at two smashing banana into her curls; Peter signing papers, pen final; Deek’s boots empty; Lace’s eyes white; Fuse grin gone. I speak aloud, let the wind take it. “You got the files. Keep them. Give her noise a room without walls.” Heartbeat thickens, adds layer—small voice underneath, humming five notes. Sammi’s timbre, but not her—echo, copy, safe. Relief punches knees; I sit hard on cockpit sole, let the boat run. Speed climbs fifteen. Hull creaks but holds. I laugh, sound like ripping canvas. “Fine, we fly then.” I rig the autopilot—bungee cord and prayer. Chart shows only blue, but the chrome fry keep jumping, breadcrumbs in daylight. I go below, fetch the snow-globe: plastic Indian Ocean, tiny dhow inside. I shake it, watch fake snow swirl. Sammi’s fingerprint smudge on glass—thumb width from last hold. I open the hatch, stand in wind, hurl it forward. Globe arcs, sun catching flurries, lands amid the schooling fry. They swallow it, globe and all—splash, gone. Water closes like it never happened. The heartbeat skips, adds a new rhythm—gentle, almost lullaby. I breathe, first full lung since the rings arrived. Ten miles. Land behind is memory. Fuel gauge still full. I kill throttle, let momentum glide. Engine coughs once, quiet. Silence again, but padded now, like room with curtains. I sit on deck, legs dangling over the side, boots skimming surface. Bracelet catches light, throws Sammi’s laugh onto the wave face. I laugh back, cracked, off-key. We converse that way—reflection and real—until sun climbs higher, turns sea to beaten metal. Course set south-west, nowhere specific. provisions for a week, maybe more if hunger learns manners. I open duffel, find Sammi’s grape gum pack—one piece left. I unwrap, fold into cheek. Sweet covers salt, briefly. I speak to horizon: “When you’re big enough, find her. Play the five notes. She’ll know.” Heartbeat fades to whisper, content or full. I don’t need reply. I start the engine again—she turns, catches, settles. I lash wheel, let her run. Then I go below, fetch pen, write in log what I never wrote before: *Paid. Balance unknown. Forward motion accepted as receipt.* I close book, stow it. Above deck wind smells of nothing but distance. I sit throttle-side, hand on metal warm from sun. Bracelet glints, counts heartbeats I no longer owe. Land behind sinks under earth’s curve; ahead, chrome fry leap, writing path only they read. I hum the five notes—wrong pitch, right intention. Engine keeps time. The ocean, finally, answers with ordinary waves, slap and hush, slap and hush, learning a new song one syllable at a time. We run like that all morning—boat, woman, echo—into blue that doesn’t end, into debt that feels like freedom, into song that needs no witness.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 8 is the culmination of an ambitious eco-gothic first-contact story, and it largely delivers on the tonal and thematic promises of the plan—though with some notable inconsistencies and a few moments where the writing strains against itself. **Strengths:** The chapter opens with genuine atmosphere.
