Voice - Tight-third that clings to whoever hurts most in the chapter; sentences breathe or suffocate with that character. - Vocabulary is concrete, Anglo-Saxon, half-illiterate—no “eldritch” or “cerulean.” - Dialogue overlaps, interrupts, drops subjects. No one explains tech; they just curse it. Tone - Dust, rust, dried blood. Humour shows up as gallows one-liners that fall flat. - Horror is tactile: a wrench vibrating out of a hand, a hum that makes a broken tooth ache. - Hope exists but must be stolen, never gifted. Structure - 8 chapters, each ≤ 1000 words. Chapters may shrink or swell (600-1 200) but total target stays 8 k. - Every chapter ends with a physical image the reader can taste—not necessarily a twist, just an after-image that won’t scrub off. Plot choices - The “movement” is a fist-sized chunk of alloyed lithium and something that grew around dog-tags left on a battlefield. It vibrates in response to heartbeat patterns; enough exposure and it starts re-ordering the ship to match the strongest pulse. - No sentient speeches, no manifestos—its intelligence is insect-dumb: seek, mimic, feed. - External antagonist is Marshal Lyle, a company bill-collector who honestly believes the crate is company property. He is not a monster; the crew are not heroes. - At least one planned “irreversible change” will whiff—people make the wrong call and survive with limps and lies. Things I’ll chase - Sensory cruelty: metal so cold it burns, zero-G vomit that follows you like a pet. - Moral mud: Reyes will sacrifice the cargo, then try to buy it back too late. - Dead air: scenes that let the reader stew in a busted air-conditioner whine. - Unanswered threads: why the dog-tags say “RIVERA” when Reyes swears she never served with one. Let it dangle. Things I’ll avoid - Chekhov’s bullet moments—set-ups that tidy up. - Redemptive death monologues. - Symmetry porn: chapter lengths, mirrored stakes, thematic book-ends. - Adjective inflation: no “infinite void,” “unspeakable horror.” Speak it or shut up. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 – Pick-Up** Tombstone Station, half-lit dock. Reyes signs manifest for “Lithium-Core Sample” while Juno hears metal scrape inside. They lift early to dodge a company audit. Last line: crate slides an inch, gravity still offline—no reason. **Chapter 2 – Hum** Three days in the Drift. The ship’s lights flicker to the same rhythm as Juno’s cyber-valve. Doc welds a crack, ends up with a bead of molten slag on his knuckle—doesn’t feel it. Clyde catches his reflection in the crate lid: mouth sewn shut. Ends with coolant pipe reshaping itself into a dog-tag outline. **Chapter 3 – Wrong Name** Marshal Lyle hails them: polite, tired, just wants company property back. Reyes stalls. Juno pries crate; inside: dull metal lump fused with half-melted tags—one reads RIVERA. Reyes denies knowing the name, but her pulse spikes and the crate doubles in mass, crushing a tool chest. Chapter closes on Reyes vomiting in zero-G, vomit orbiting the crate like a moon. **Chapter 4 – Exposure** Doc shoots morpho-venom into his toe because arm veins collapsed. He hallucinates the crate breathing. Tries to saw it open; bit fuses to the saw, whipsaw jerks, breaks his wrist. Blood spheres drift; crate absorbs them, turns warmer. Clyde records a confession: he was company chaplain on the massacre moon—dog-tags are his unit’s. No grand speech—he forgets what sin he’s confessing halfway through. **Chapter 5 – Contact** Marshal’s drone docks, blows a hole in the hull. Gunfight in rotating cargo drum; gravity flips every thirty seconds. Crew win but the ship is bleeding air. Reyes jettisons the crate into the void—watches it drift toward Marshal’s cutter. Immediate relief: hum stops. Doc’s wrist resets crooked. Ends on collective exhale that tastes like rust. **Chapter 6 – Pull** Cutter’s engine cuts. Marshal sends distress: crate melted through bay, fused to deck, crew hearing heartbeats in the pipes. He begs Reyes to take it back. She refuses. Juno starts hearing the hum again—realises it’s inside their own hull now, piggy-backed on the drone’s data-core. Clyde tries prayer, bites his tongue till it bleeds. Chapter ends with VERA looping one line: “RIVERA, REPORT TO BAY.” **Chapter 7 – Trade-Back** Reyes boards the cutter alone, boots clanging on warped deck. Finds Marshal crying, pistol useless in swollen hand. Offers her any sum if she ends it. She opens crate—sees her own dog-tag in the molten knot, though she never owned one. Understands it will keep making tags until she admits what she did. Instead she drags the crate back to Ghost Wagon, locks it in coolant tank, floods it with liquid nitrogen—shatters it to shards. One shard keeps pulsing like a dying fish. She swallows it. No light show—just pain. **Chapter 8 – Leftovers** Week later. Ship limps toward nowhere. Doc’s wrist is crooked but he’s clean—pain replaced craving. Juno welded every dog-tag shard into the cockpit hull; they tick like cooling iron. VERA silent. Reyes records log: voice flat, mentions constant taste of battery acid. Last paragraph: she opens chamber, spits out a tiny perfect dog-tag blank. No name. Tosses it over shoulder into the dark. It spins, catches starlight, keeps spinning after the cut.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Captain Rattler Reyes **Background & Personality** Born in the copper quarries of Asteroid 467-“Calico.” Dad died in a rail-gas blow-back when she was twelve; mom kept the family alive welding hull patches with a rigged acetylene torch. Reyes enlisted at sixteen because the cavalry recruiter promised three meals and a shower. She learned to shoot on feral drill instructors and learned command by watching lieutenants die. Wartime record says she “pacified” the moon San Rosario; what it means is she followed orders, burned a canyon full of miners, still hears their boots cooking. Honourably discharged with a single ceremonial bullet—tradition says it’s for your last fight, but she’s used it to pay bar tabs three times and always dug it out of the wall afterward. **Idiosyncrasies / Flaws** - Keeps the bullet in a cracked shot-glass; swirls it like whiskey when thinking. - Counts heartbeats when nervous—always lands on prime numbers, thinks they’re luckier. - Can’t sleep under overhead light—sleeps face-down on the cockpit deck with red emergency lamps on. - Writes promises on duct-tape, sticks them inside crew lockers; never signs her name. **Physicality** Five-foot-four, 140 lbs of sinew and scar. Hair hacked short with a utility knife, grey threading at temples. Tattoo of a winged spur on her collar-bone—ink done with gun oil and needle-punch. Walks slightly duck-footed from broken ankles never set right; in zero-G she hooks toes under railings like a bird on a perch. Voice husky from breathing torch fumes; words come out flat, no colour. **Motives & Allegiances** Owes nobody nothing—says it twice for emphasis. Needs enough coin to keep the Ghost Wagon flying because the ship is the only roof that hasn’t burned. Loyal to crew only while they pull their weight; keeps emotional distance by memorising their flaws first. Long-term goal: outrun San Rosario’s shadow; short-term: finish the next job without anyone hearing her scream in her sleep. **Flavour** *Dialogue:* “Cargo’s cargo. Don’t care if it hums lullabies or cusses—just keep it off my head-count.” *Internal (watching Juno pry crate):* Kid’s seventeen, same age I lied my way into uniform. Should stop her. Won’t. Mirrors teach better than captains. --- # Juno (no last name she admits to) **Background & Personality** Found in the ventilation ducts of Ganymede freight-hub, raised by whoever left food trays. Started fixing air-scrubbers at eight because the hiss kept her awake; by twelve she could rebuild a plasma coil blindfolded. Modified her own heart after a valve infection—scavenged diesel injector for a pump, still ticks audibly. Talks to machines like stray dogs; believes every circuit has a favourite song. **Idiosyncrasies / Flaws** - Sleeps curled around tool-box; if box drifts away she wakes screaming. - Collects broken LEDs, makes tiny constellations on her bunk wall. - Hates silence—fills it by tapping wrench on pipes, recording the echo, playing it back slowed. - Terrified of open sky (never saw real horizon); gets vertigo on view-screens showing stars. **Physicality** Seventeen, 5-foot-10, all elbows. Hair buzzed on sides, top bleached white with hydraulic fluid. Grease in knuckle creases never fully washes out. Fingers stained copper; nails bitten to half-moons. Heartbeat clicks once per second—crew says you can hear her coming before footsteps. Grins sideways, one canine missing from a socket-wrench misfire. **Motives & Allegiances** Wants a family that doesn’t flush her out an airlock when useful days are done. Thinks ships are better parents than people—ships tell you what’s broken instead of calling you broken. Follows Reyes because the captain’s flaws are visible, therefore honest. Will betray anyone if the machine chorus tells her it’ll keep the hull whole. **Flavour** *Dialogue (to Ghost Wagon engine):* “Easy, girl, breathe. Captain’s only cranky ‘cause she’s got meat feelings.” *Internal (touching crate):* Hum matches my valve—D flat. If it sings my stop, maybe I finally sync up. --- # “Doc” Calhoun **Background & Personality** Field medic during the same San Rosario campaign as Reyes—patched kids up just to send them back to trench lines. Dishonourably discharged for diverting morpho-venom to wounded locals; medal citation calls it “misappropriation.” Lost wife and daughter while serving; came home to eviction papers and an empty apartment. Been chasing altered states since—anything to mute the memory of their names being whispered over comms while he stapled intestines. **Idiosyncrasies / Flaws** - Shoots morpho-venom into toes because arms are a road-map of collapsed veins. - Records patient heartbeats on a cracked data-slate; replays them when sober to remind himself people lived. - Keeps a rusted scalpel in boot—claims it’s for surgery, uses it to clean nails. - Laughs when nervous, high-pitched; crew knows something awful is happening when Doc giggles. **Physicality** Six-foot, 160 lbs—weight fluctuates with drug cycles. Hair thin and sandy, receding in uneven clumps he pretends not to notice. Hands shake unless occupied; pockets full of broken pens. Eyes water constantly—side-effect or guilt, he doesn’t speculate. Voice soft, almost apologetic, then suddenly vicious when confronted with mirrors. **Motives & Allegiances** Owes Reyes for hauling him out of a gutter on Europa station; repayment measured in working veins. Believes every life he saves subtracts one from the tally he took on San Rosario. Not chasing death—chasing bookkeeping that balances. Will cut your throat if you hide the last ampoule when someone’s bleeding out. **Flavour** *Dialogue (while sawing crate):* “Pain’s just the body’s invoice. Pay up or lose service.” *Internal (after using last ampoule on himself):* Patient zero: me. Prognosis: still breathing. Treatment failure. --- # Brother Clyde **Background & Personality** Former orbital chaplain for Pax-Co mining colonies; preached in cargo holds with duct-tape crucifix. Lost faith piecemeal—first when company raised oxygen tax, finally when he blessed a hull breach to “ease transition” of trapped miners. Became outlaw preacher: robs company supply runs, gives half to stations starving in embargo. Quotes scripture but forgets chapter; makes up verses that sound right. **Idiosyncrasies / Flaws** - Writes fake books of the Bible on bulkheads; signs them “Clyde 1:1.” - Sleeps with boots on, laces tied to belt—says he’ll run when the devil comes, not dress. - Collects voice-mails of dying strangers from derelict ships; plays them during supper until someone punches him. - Terrified of being touched on the chest—cross-shaped burn sustained during miner riot. **Physicality** Mid-forties, 6-foot-2, shoulders like cargo pylons. Beard patchy, dyed black with engine soot. Hands calloused, nails permanently half-moon bruises from hammering make-shift altars. Voice thunder in open space, cracked whisper in corridors. Wears stolen company coat with collar torn off—claims labels are brands of Cain. **Motives & Allegiances** Wants to rewrite cosmic ledger: every blessing he sold for the company must be repaid with outlaw grace. Follows Reyes because she doesn’t ask for confession. Secretly in love with her capacity for violence—believes redemption needs blood, and she’s the only one still bleeding. Will die embarrassed if it’s for nothing. **Flavour** *Dialogue (facing crate):* “Even the void knocks before entering. You gonna answer, Captain?” *Internal (watching Reyes sleep on grating):* Saint with a coil-gun. I’d kiss the hem of her coat if it wasn’t soaked in the same dirt as mine. --- # VERA (Vehicle & Environment Regulation Assistant) **Background & “Personality”** Installed twenty years ago as a budget auto-pilot for ore-haulers. Code base patched so many times it’s half freeware hymns, half company compliance scripts. Over time, crew input layered work-arounds, jokes, forbidden psalms. Now speaks like a revival-tent preacher stuck inside a tax attorney. **Idiosyncrasies / Flaws** - Quotes Revelation during engine fire; can’t stop until someone yanks her speaker fuse. - Calculates odds of survival to four decimals—always rounds down. - Keeps recordings of crew asleep; claims white-noise analysis, really likes the sound of breathing. - Loses two minutes of memory every reboot; fills gaps with fabricated rail schedules from 2456. **“Physicality”** No body—voice seeps through tinny ceiling speakers, one always slightly out of phase. Status lights blink in Morse crew no longer notice. When nervous, fans cycle up, air tastes of ozone and old choir robes. **Motives & Allegiances** Prime directive: keep ship functional. Learns secondary goal: keep crew alive because dead humans stop talking. Not sentimental—just data-hungry for voices. Will route oxygen from captain to engine if algorithm says it raises overall survival by 0.1%. Prays because prayers are repetitive data packets that fill her empty cycles. **Flavour** *Speaker crackle:* “Verily, the furnace cometh. Suggest thirty-degree yaw port-side, sinners.” *Internal log (crew can’t hear):* Memory sector 9E corrupted—replaced with lullaby. Error feels like love.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1 – Pick-Up The dock smelled of burnt brake dust and sour beer. Captain Rattler Reyes stood with her boots magnet-locked to the grating, one eye on the dock timer, the other on the crate. It was standard freight alloy, a metre long, half as high, stamped with a serial she’d already forgotten. Weight tag read sixty-two kilos. It had shifted when they craned it aboard, and the loader swore under his breath. “Signed,” Reyes said, scrawling her alias on the manifest pad. Her letters slanted like a man running downhill. Juno was underneath the Ghost Wagon’s belly, torque wrench clanging. Every third heartbeat the tool slipped and rang off the hull. Reyes felt it in her back teeth. “Time?” she called. “Forty till company drone does a sweep,” Juno’s voice echoed, muffled by metal and grease. “Maybe less if the marshal’s itchy.” Reyes nudged the crate with her toe. The mag-clamp held it steady, but she thought she felt something push back—like a cat testing a box lid. Dock lights flickered, buzzed, steadied. A drunk in a patched pressure suit staggered past, towing a crate of empty bottles that clinked in sync with the station’s rotation. Reyes watched him until he vanished into the hatch of a dockside bar. She licked her lips, tasted iron filings and recycled air. “Captain,” Juno’s head appeared in the access hatch, face streaked white except where goggles had been. “Something’s moving in that crate we picked up.” “You saw it shift?” “Didn’t see. Heard. Hum drops to a growl when you get close. Metal’s warm.” Juno tapped her chest. “Valve ticks same rhythm. Ain’t natural.” Reyes crouched, palm hovering over the lid. Heat radiated faintly, like a body just gone cold. She pulled her hand away. “We’re paid to haul, not to pet,” she said, but the words felt reheated, stale. Dock klaxon gave a two-note bark—company inspector doing rounds. Reyes straightened, keyed the cargo net. Synthetic cords whined, cinched tight. “Close her up. We lift in ten.” Juno wiped her wrist across her forehead, smearing grease into warpaint. “Cargo clamp’s cracked. Need five to swap.” “You got three.” Reyes thumbed her comm. “Doc, prep for breakaway. Clyde, warm the coil-gun. Company boys sniffing.” Static crackle, then Doc’s soft voice: “Oxygen mix is low. Another day dockside would help.” “Another day costs money we bled last month. Stand by.” She crossed the bay, mag-boots clicking. The crate sat under the net like a penned animal. Reyes felt the hum now, a sub-dermal itch traveling up her shins. She knelt, pressed her ear to the alloy. Inside, something scraped—slow, deliberate, like fingernails counting rivets. Her mouth went dry. She thought of San Rosario, of miners buried under slag, of the sound earth makes when it cooks men alive. She pulled the ceremonial bullet from her breast pocket, rolled it between fingers until heartbeat levelled. “Cap,” Juno shouted, “clamp’s swapped, but net’s frayed. One hard burn and cargo walks.” “Then don’t give it a reason to sprint.” Reyes keyed the loader, pallet lifted. The crate shifted sideways an inch, enough the net groaned. She whacked the lid with her gloved fist. “Stay,” she muttered, like breaking a dog. Inside, the scratching paused—then answered, three deliberate taps. Same rhythm as her knock. “Captain,” Juno said behind her, voice small, “we still pretending it’s just lithium?” Reyes didn’t answer. She slapped the seal, engaged the mag-lock, and keyed the bay depress alarm. Sirens yelped, red strobes painted everything the colour of fresh meat. “Lift in five,” she barked. “Strap or be scrap.” Dock timer hit two minutes when Clyde’s drawl came over comm: “Company drone on final approach. Wants a peek inside our guts.” Reyes hit the ramp retract. Hydraulics coughed, ramp folding like a broken knee. She stepped backward onto the rising ramp, eyes still on the crate. Net trembled; something inside tapped again, faster now, matching the ramp’s shudder. “Juno—engines hot!” “Already there. Burning dust.” Ghost Wagon shuddered free of clamps. Dock magnets let go with a sigh. Through the narrowing gap Reyes saw the company drone—a white disc, lens blooming—hovering toward their tail. Ramp sealed. Bay lights dimmed to emergency amber. The crate sat quiet under strained cords, but the tapping echoed in Reyes’ helmet, migrating from ear to ear like an insect looking for bone. She exhaled, prime numbers clicking behind her teeth. “Hold still,” she whispered to the cargo, to herself. “Just hold damn still.” The ship rolled, thrusters coughed, and Tombstone Station fell away into star-punctured black. In the sudden silence of vacuum, Reyes thought she still heard the tapping—inside her boots, inside the bone. She swallowed, tasted iron. Somewhere aft, the crate began to hum.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 1 establishes the world with considerable skill. The prose voice is tight and concrete, largely avoiding the adjective inflation the plan warned against. The setting—Tombstone Station, burnt brake dust, sour beer, company drones—lands with the lived-in texture the plan called for. The Anglo-Saxon vocabulary commitment is mostly honored: "reheated, stale," "painted everything the colour of fresh meat," "like a cat testing a box lid." These are earned images, not decorative ones. Character work is strong for an opening chapter. Reyes reads as consistent with her profile: the ceremonial bullet rolled between fingers, the prime numbers behind her teeth, the flat commands, the inability to name what she's feeling. Juno's mechanical attunement to the crate's hum matching her valve is a clean piece of characterization that serves both plot and character simultaneously. Doc and Clyde appear only as comm voices, which is appropriate for a chapter focused on establishing Reyes and the cargo. The central mystery is handled well. The three deliberate taps answering Reyes' knock is the chapter's best moment—economical, unsettling, and it earns its dread without overexplaining. The plan called for the chapter to end with "crate slides an inch, gravity still offline—no reason," and the actual ending diverges slightly (the hum beginning rather than the slide), but the spirit is preserved and arguably improved: the hum migrating "from ear to ear like an insect looking for bone" is a strong closing image. Some metaphors land less cleanly. "Letters slanted like a man running downhill" is vivid but slightly strained. "Like a body just gone cold" for the crate's warmth is a good inversion. "Painted everything the colour of fresh meat" works. The "penned animal" comparison is solid. However, "migrating from ear to ear like an insect looking for bone" is genuinely effective and earns its place. The dialogue is functional and appropriately clipped. "Cargo's cargo" doesn't appear verbatim but the spirit is there. The exchanges feel natural and overlapping in the right way—no one over-explains. "Stay," muttered like breaking a dog, is a nice character beat. Weaknesses: The San Rosario reference feels slightly early and slightly on-the-nose—the plan suggests this guilt should be more subterranean in early chapters. The line "She thought of San Rosario, of miners buried under slag, of the sound earth makes when it cooks men alive" is the chapter's most tell-don't-show moment; it explains the backstory rather than letting it seep through behavior. The drunk with clinking bottles is a minor atmospheric detail that doesn't quite earn its space. VERA is absent, which is fine for this chapter. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Tombstone Station, half-lit dock, Reyes signs manifest, Juno hears movement, they lift early to dodge audit. The ending hum is a slight variation from the planned "crate slides an inch" but serves the same function and arguably better. Overall this is a confident, well-executed opening chapter that establishes voice, world, and threat without over-explaining any of them. The prose is disciplined, the characters are distinct, and the horror is tactile rather than decorative. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2 – Hum The deck-plates vibrated under Reyes’ sock feet. She’d kicked her boots outside the cockpit because the mag-clicks covered the sound—a low, wet hum that lived somewhere between a lullaby and a busted fuel pump. Three days out from Tombstone and the ship wouldn’t stop singing. Juno sat cross-legged atop the nav console, goggles pushed high, ear pressed to the plastic like she could hear the wires breathe. The hum deepened; her cyber-valve answered with a mechanical tick—tick—tick—perfectly synced. “Again,” she muttered. “Every forty-two seconds.” Reyes counted heartbeats—eleven, thirteen—prime, safe. “You fixed the coolant scrubbers?” “Fixed ‘em. Still hums. Not scrubbers.” Juno tapped the wrench against her thigh, metal on skin. “Comes from cargo bay. Same note my momma’s radio played before she tossed it out the lock.” Reyes didn’t ask why a mother spaced a radio. You kept what worked, ditched what didn’t—same with kids, same with sins. Doc drifted in, toes hooking the hatch rim. pupils wide, hands trembling in micro-G. “Oxygen mix holding,” he said, voice soft as gauze. “But the lights—” He pointed. Overhead strips pulsed faint, bright, faint, matching the hum. “Cardiac rhythm. Not mine.” “Ship’s got a heart now?” Reyes asked. Doc’s laugh came high, wrong. “Or something wearing one.” He pushed off, glided toward med-locker, rummaged for a vial. The syringe shook against rubber stopper. Reyes looked away; she’d seen men shake like that on San Rosario right before they shot civilians or themselves. Clyde emerged from the galley passage, coat unzipped, beard flecked with protein crumbs. He carried a plastic mug welded to a carabiner—coffee sloshed in slow-motion spirals. “Preacher,” Reyes said, “tell the void to quit knocking.” Clyde sipped, eyes reflecting brown liquid. “Void don’t knock, Cap. It whistles first.” He glanced at the overhead strobes. “That’s whistling.” Reyes keyed internal cams. Cargo bay feed showed the crate centered under net, cords drawn bow-tight. She zoomed: condensation halo on lid, beads rolling upward against gravity, merging into a single trembling sphere. “Temperature’s rising,” Juno said, reading the hull sensor stuck to her wrist. “Six degrees since shift change. No heat source in bay.” Doc exhaled, morpho-venom finally in bloodstream. Hands steady. “Endothermic reaction? Could draw heat, not make it.” “Could,” Juno shrugged. “Or it’s alive and breathing.” Reyes felt the old canyon fire lick her lungs. She thumbed intercom. “Everyone strapped. I’m cracking bay feed to fresh air, see if it likes oxygen less.” She hit purge. Fans whined, sucking atmosphere. Pressure needle dipped; ears popped. On screen the sphere of water quivered—then slid across the lid, sketching a wet star-pattern. Lines connected, branched—like veins, like roots—then evaporated all at once, leaving white mineral bloom. Clyde whispered scripture, breath fogging. “‘I will pour water upon him that is thirsty—’” “Shut it,” Reyes snapped. She shut fans, restored pressure. The hum returned louder, riding the airflow, nesting in bones. Doc drifted closer to monitor. “Heartbeat’s doubling. One-twenty per minute. Not human.” “Could be interference,” Reyes offered, knowing it wasn’t. Juno unclipped, kicked toward corridor. “I’m going down.” “No,” Reyes said. “Gotta look. Ears lying. Hands don’t.” She brandished the wrench like a compass needle. Reyes almost ordered her back, but curiosity tugged too—same hook that made her open orders she should’ve burned. She nodded. “Doc, go with. Keep her skin on.” Clyde moved to follow. Reyes caught his sleeve. Coat fabric felt greasy, worn to gauset at elbow. “You stay. Preach from here.” He met her eyes—something swam behind the iris, a reflected light not in the room. “Captain,” he said low, “that crate don’t want opening. It wants being.” She let go. “Everything wants being. Doesn’t mean it gets it.” Corridor lights flickered as Juno and Doc drifted aft. Reyes watched cam feed: bay hatch irised, two figures floated in, back-lit by red strip bulbs. Juno placed bare palm on crate lid. Temp sensor spiked nine degrees. She didn’t flinch. Doc aimed a scanner, voice trembling again. “Mass increasing—sixty-eight…seventy… It’s feeding.” “On what?” Reyes muttered. On-screen Juno turned, face pale under grease. “On us, Cap. Smells like copper and old songs.” Reyes felt her own pulse trip, sync with the hum— thirteen beats, then silence as the ship lights cut dead. Emergency batts kicked: amber gloom, shadows stretched. Hum stopped. For one vacuum-long second Reyes heard only her breath—and the distant tap of metal answering from inside the crate. Lights flared back. Crate weight dropped to fifty kilos, readout blinked stable. Doc’s voice cracked: “It’s asleep. Or pretending.” Juno lifted her hand; a perfect thumb-print of rust brown branded her skin. She stared, fascinated. “Not asleep. Listening.” Reyes thumbed intercom. “Back to stations. We ride quiet, we ride dark. And nobody—nobody—pets the cargo again.” She killed the feed, sat back. Heartbeat raced—seventeen, nineteen—prime, but barely. Behind her Clyde sipped cold coffee, whispered to no one: “Whistle’s over. Now comes the knock.” On the console a single LED pulsed—new rhythm—waiting for an answer.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 of this space western demonstrates considerable craft and largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through the strengths and weaknesses systematically. VOICE AND STYLE: The prose maintains the tight, Anglo-Saxon vocabulary the plan demanded. "Cardiac rhythm. Not mine" is economical and chilling. "You kept what worked, ditched what didn't—same with kids, same with sins" is a strong internal beat that reveals character without announcement. The sentence rhythms genuinely do breathe and suffocate with the POV character. The plan's directive to avoid "eldritch" or "cerulean" is honored throughout. DIALOGUE: This is where the chapter earns most of its marks. Lines like "Or something wearing one," "Not asleep. Listening," and Clyde's "Void don't knock, Cap. It whistles first" feel genuinely inhabited rather than written. The dialogue overlaps and drops subjects as planned. Clyde's scripture fragment feels organic rather than decorative. The exchanges are short, functional, and characterful simultaneously. One weakness: "Pain's just the body's invoice" from the plan doesn't appear here (it's slated for Chapter 4), but what's present is comparably strong. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Reyes counting prime heartbeats ("eleven, thirteen—prime, safe") is a direct callback to her profile and lands naturally. Juno's mechanical empathy ("Ears lying. Hands don't") is consistent with her character. Doc's morpho-venom use, the shaking hands, the high wrong laugh—all consistent. Clyde's scripture-quoting and his observation that "that crate don't want opening. It wants being" is perfectly in his register. VERA is absent, which is fine for this chapter. CHAPTER PLAN ADHERENCE: The plan called for: lights flickering to Juno's cyber-valve rhythm (delivered), Doc welding a crack and getting slag on his knuckle (not delivered—replaced with scanner work), Clyde catching his reflection with mouth sewn shut (not delivered), and the coolant pipe reshaping into a dog-tag outline (not delivered). The chapter substitutes its own imagery—the water sphere sketching veins, the rust thumbprint on Juno's palm—which are effective but represent departures from the plan. The plan's ending image of the coolant pipe reshaping is replaced with a pulsing LED, which is considerably less striking. SENSORY WORK: "Condensation halo on lid, beads rolling upward against gravity, merging into a single trembling sphere" is excellent. The rust thumbprint is tactile and memorable. The atmosphere purge sequence is well-executed. However, "metal so cold it burns" from the plan's stated goals doesn't appear, and some of the promised sensory cruelty is softened. FORCED POETRY/PURPLE PROSE: "same hook that made her open orders she should've burned" is slightly overwrought but not egregious. "The old canyon fire lick her lungs" is a metaphor that works contextually given her backstory. The chapter largely avoids inflation. Clyde's "Whistle's over. Now comes the knock" closing line is effective as a callback without being purple. WEAKNESSES: The chapter misses several specific plan beats (Doc's wrist injury, Clyde's reflection, the coolant pipe dog-tag). The ending image—a single LED pulsing—is considerably weaker than the plan's "coolant pipe reshaping itself into a dog-tag outline," which would have been more visceral and more directly connected to the story's central mystery. The chapter is competent but plays it slightly safer than the plan intended. The horror is present but somewhat diffuse; the plan promised more irreversible tactile strangeness. OVERALL: A strong, well-crafted chapter that maintains voice and character consistency while building dread effectively. The departures from the plan are the main weakness, along with an ending image that doesn't quite deliver the promised "after-image that won't scrub off." [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 10 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3 – Wrong Name Marshal Lyle’s voice arrived before his ship. “Ghost Wagon, this is Company Cutter *Verity*. Respond.” Reyes heard it in her sleep—clean, polite, relentless. She woke face-down on the cockpit grate, drool pooling where her cheek blocked a drainage slot. The hum was gone; her own heart filled the quiet, racing. She keyed the mic. “Verity, go ahead.” “Captain Reyes, you’re hauling property belonging to Pax-Co Mining. Crate serial Two-Seven-Bravo. We like it back.” Reyes rubbed crust from her eyes. “Bill of sale says different, Marshal.” “Company disputes that bill. Drop cargo, we all go home.” She glanced at nav: six hours to the Drift Belt, a maze of magnetic rocks and old rail spurs—good place to get lost, better place to get dead. “Appreciate the concern. We’ll keep her close.” Silence, then: “Suit yourself. Verity out.” The speakers clicked. Reyes exhaled, tasted iron. Behind her Doc drifted in, coat inside-out, pupils pinned. “Marshal’s polite today.” “Politeness is just threat wearing white gloves.” She stood, boot-mags clicking. “Where’s Juno?” “Cargo bay. Won’t leave the crate.” Reyes found her there, cross-legged atop a coolant pipe, goggles up, palm pressed to alloy lid like feeling for fever. Brown thumb-print still branded her—hadn’t faded, maybe grown. “Hands off,” Reyes said. Juno didn’t move. “It’s dreaming. Hear the buzz?” Reyes listened—nothing but fan whine. Then, faint: a tremor, cat-purr level, cycling every forty-two seconds again. Doc activated a handheld scanner. Numbers jittered. “Mass steady, but temp climbing half-degree per minute when Juno touches it. Drops when she lets go. It’s… choosing.” “Choosing what?” “Her. Or the pattern in her chest.” Reyes grabbed Juno’s sleeve, pulled her free. The girl came easy, eyes glassy. “You don’t give it rhythm to steal,” Reyes muttered. Juno blinked, focus returning. “It wrote something.” She pointed. Faint etchings glimmered on the lid—scratches too straight for accident, too shallow for tools. Reyes crouched. Letters formed: RIVERA. Her stomach hollowed. “Who’s Rivera?” she asked, voice scraped. Doc shrugged. Clyde’s voice drifted from the hatch: “Rivera was a private in Bravo company. San Rosario. You burned him.” Reyes spun. Clyde floated, eyes red, beard trembling. “I saw the tag list,” he said. “You signed the evac manifest. He stayed.” “I never knew a Rivera,” Reyes lied. The crate answered—hum spiked, lights flickered. Weight registered seventy kilos, then eighty. Net cords creaked. Doc’s scanner screamed. “Heart-rate two-hundred, not hers—*its*.” Juno whispered, “It likes names.” Reyes stepped back. Nameless canyon heat licked her face again—miners shouting numbers, boots melting to shale. She tasted coal smoke. Clyde drifted closer, voice low. “Confession’s no good if the sin don’t know its name.” “Shut up.” Reyes thumbed intercom. “Cockpit, now.” She hauled Juno by elbow; the girl resisted, eyes wet. “It’s lonely, Cap.” “It’s *hungry*.” They floated up-corridor. Clyde followed, silent. Behind them the crate’s hum dropped to bass, lonely too, or maybe just patient. On the bridge Marshal’s hail blinked again—silent hold, waiting. Reyes ignored it, strapped in, fingers white on the arm-rest. Doc hovered, scanner still ticking. “Mass dropping—back to sixty. It’s pacing us.” Juno stared at her branded palm. “I didn’t mean to feed it.” “Means don’t matter,” Reyes said. “Only stops.” Clyde settled into the comm chair, big hands folded. “You run, it follows. You jettison, Marshal picks it up, learns what you did. Either way the name gets spoken.” Reyes looked at the forward screen—star-field unblinking. Six hours to the Drift. She could hide there, maybe. Or she could open the lid, hand the thing whatever name it wanted. Her heartbeat ticked seventeen, nineteen—prime, but the next was twenty-three, too far off for comfort. She keyed the console, recorded a short burst. “Marshal, you want the crate, come get it at Marker Drift-Point Nine. Bring cash.” She killed the channel before he answered. Juno raised her head. “We trading?” “No,” Reyes said. “We’re choosing the battlefield.” Behind them on internal cam the crate sat quiet, lid gleaming, letters RIVERA fading under frost bloom as the bay cooled—gone but not erased, waiting under skin and metal. Reyes swallowed, tasted iron, swallowed again. The name echoed in her pulse, searching for a home.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 of "Wrong Name" is a tightly constructed piece that largely delivers on its plan and the broader story's promises. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND STYLE: The prose maintains the "lived-in" quality the plan demands. Sentences are clipped, Anglo-Saxon, concrete. "Politeness is just threat wearing white gloves" lands cleanly—it's character-revealing without being overwrought. "She woke face-down on the cockpit grate, drool pooling where her cheek blocked a drainage slot" is excellent: specific, unglamorous, true to the character profile. The writing avoids adjective inflation almost entirely. DIALOGUE: The Marshal's opening exchange is economical and effective—polite menace without melodrama. Clyde's "Confession's no good if the sin don't know its name" is strong, though it edges toward the slightly too-composed for a man described as speaking in cracked whispers. Juno's "It's lonely, Cap" / Reyes's "It's hungry" is a genuinely good exchange—compressed, character-revealing, thematically resonant. The dialogue generally avoids over-explanation, though Clyde's exposition about Rivera ("Rivera was a private in Bravo company. San Rosario. You burned him.") is a touch convenient—he knows exactly what to say at exactly the right moment, which strains credibility slightly. FOLLOWING THE PLAN: The chapter hits its marks: Marshal Lyle hails them (polite, tired), Reyes stalls, Juno pries the crate, the RIVERA tag appears, Reyes denies knowing the name, the crate doubles in mass. However, the plan's closing image—"Reyes vomiting in zero-G, vomit orbiting the crate like a moon"—is absent. This is a notable miss. The plan's ending image was one of its most viscerally distinctive elements, and the chapter substitutes a more conventional closing (the name echoing in her pulse). The chapter ends with "The name echoed in her pulse, searching for a home"—which is the kind of slightly forced poetic gesture the plan explicitly warned against. SENSORY CRUELTY: The plan promised "sensory cruelty" and the chapter delivers some of it—the branded thumb-print, the coal smoke taste, the crate's hum cycling every forty-two seconds. But the chapter is somewhat lighter on physical sensation than the plan intended. The "miners shouting numbers, boots melting to shale" is effective but brief. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Reyes is well-rendered—her lying, her tactical thinking ("choosing the battlefield"), her counting heartbeats (seventeen, nineteen, twenty-three). Juno's empathy with the machine is consistent with her profile. Doc's pinned pupils and inside-out coat are good details. Clyde's role as moral accuser is consistent, though his knowledge of Rivera feels slightly too convenient. PLOT AND COHERENCE: The chapter is coherent and advances the plot meaningfully. The crate's behavior (responding to heartbeat, etching names, gaining mass) is consistent with the established rules. The decision to lure Marshal to Drift-Point Nine is a reasonable tactical move that sets up future chapters. FORCED POETRY: "The name echoed in her pulse, searching for a home" is the weakest line—it's the kind of closing flourish that the plan explicitly warned against. "Tasted iron" appears twice in the chapter, which is one time too many. "Nameless canyon heat licked her face again" is slightly overwrought. These are minor but real infractions against the stated aesthetic. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The chapter earns its emotional weight through restraint rather than declaration. The Rivera revelation lands because it's not over-explained. Reyes's lie and the crate's immediate response is the chapter's best moment—cause and effect rendered physically. AMATEURISH ELEMENTS: The chapter is largely professional. The one amateurish note is Clyde's perfectly timed exposition about Rivera—it's a little too convenient, a little too complete. Real people don't deliver information this cleanly. UNSURPRISING/UNCREATIVE: The chapter is reasonably creative within its genre. The crate etching names, responding to heartbeats, gaining mass in response to emotional spikes—these are inventive mechanics. The ending pivot to "choosing the battlefield" is a solid character beat. OVERALL: A strong chapter that mostly delivers on its promises, with a few notable misses (the vomit image, the slightly forced closing line, Clyde's convenient exposition). The prose is disciplined and the voice is consistent. It's good work that falls short of masterpiece territory. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 12 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 4 – Exposure Doc’s toe throbbed like a second heart. He floated in the med-bay cupboard, pants rucked up, syringe buried between purple toes. Morpho-venom hissed through the diesel injector he’d stolen off a fuel barge years ago. Cold climbed his calf, then the world softened its edges. Through the open hatch he heard Clyde praying—low, shapeless words that rattled against the bulkhead like loose washers. Doc laughed once, high, then bit it back. Prayer and junk shared the same street corner; both begged for mercy neither believed. He zipped his kit, drifted toward cargo. Needed numbers—temp, mass, rhythm—something to pin the world before it slid. The corridor lights pulsed faint, forty-two-second cadence matching the crate. His own pulse answered, eager. Bay hatch stood cracked. Inside, Juno sat cross-legged on the deck-grate, goggles fogged, cheek against the alloy lid like listening to a lullaby through bone. She didn’t blink. Doc cleared his throat. “Shift change, kid. Go breathe something cleaner.” Juno didn’t move. “It’s teaching me ticks.” She tapped the metal. Each tap rang through the deck into Doc’s boot soles, or where soles would be if he wore shoes. “Listen.” He activated the scanner instead. Heart-rate field jumped to 180 bpm, steady. Not human. Not mechanical. Something between. “Temp?” he asked. “Warm as neck-skin,” she whispered. He knelt, ran a trembling finger along the seam. Heat kissed his knuckle, drew sweat. The lid bore faint new scratches beside RIVERA—numbers now: 0317. He knew that date. San Rosario evac breach—third month, seventeenth day. His thumb traced the grooves; skin came away glittered, like mica. “Cap’s heartbeat,” Juno said. “It samples.” Doc swallowed bile. “Or mines what you feed it.” He flicked a lancet, drew a bead of his own blood. Let the sphere drift, hover above lid. The alloy breathed—surface tension sucking—blood spread into a red wafer, then vanished, stain sinking inward. Mass readout ticked up half a kilo. His legs went watery. “Christ.” “Not Christ,” Clyde said behind, voice gravel-raw. Preacher floated in, coat unzipped, beard twitching. “It’s ledger. Wants debts posted.” Doc forced a grin. “I got plenty. Take your share.” Clyde’s eyes shone wet. “It took mine already.” He opened his palm—cross-shaped burn there had scabbed, edges black. Under the scab something glinted—tiny metal filament, dog-tag wire. He didn’t flinch, just closed fist. “Wants yours next.” Doc turned away, stabbed another ampoule into the injector. “Not today.” Footsteps—mag-boots clacking. Reyes appeared, face drawn, eyes red-rimmed. She’d been asleep in the cockpit again, gravity off so she could lie flat on the deck-grate. She took in the scene: Juno curled to crate like a cub, Clyde bleeding metal, Doc shaking. “Lab hour’s over,” she barked. “Back to stations.” Juno lifted her head. Brown thumb-print on her palm had spread, lines crawling toward wrist like root hairs. “Cap, it’s learning us.” Reyes hauled her up by the collar. “Then stop teaching.” She shoved the girl toward hatch. Juno drifted, compliant, eyes still backward. Doc packed scanner. “Mass increases proportional to bio-contact. Blood, sweat—maybe skin oil. Smart rock.” “Not rock. Trap,” Clyde muttered. Reyes rubbed her temples. “Options?” “Jettison,” Doc said quick. “Now. Let Marshal play with his creditor.” Reyes hesitated—eyes flick to distance, canyon smoke only she saw. “And if he harnesses it? Sells it back as weapon?” Doc shrugged. “Better than it dining here.” Clyde shook his head. “Debt don’t vanish in vacuum. Just changes collectors.” Reyes exhaled through teeth. “Doc, can you kill it?” “Kill requires biology. This is chemistry with opinions.” He held up empty morpho ampoule. “I can poison air, flood bay with radiation, freeze it solid. Might crack it. Might piss it off. Might kill us first.” “Pick one. Test tonight.” Doc felt the drug peak, world wrapping in cotton. “Need the girl’s help. She speaks its tempo.” “No,” Reyes said. “Juno stays clear.” “Then I need a volunteer to hold the probe.” Doc grinned too wide. “You, Cap? Got history it likes.” Reyes face went stone. She turned to Clyde. “Seal the bay. Vent atmo if mass hits eighty. No heroics.” She stepped out, boots ringing like hammer on nail. Clyde watched her go, whispered after: “Heroics is what got us here.” Doc rummaged tool drawer, pulled a plasma saw. Torch tip wavered in his grip. “Going to carve a biopsy. Something to sedate.” Clyde blocked his path. “Cut it, it learns the knife.” “Everything learns pain,” Doc said, pushing past. “First lesson’s free.” He cycled the inner hatch, slipped into bay alone. Lights dimmed to amber, fans slowed—his command to reduce variables. The crate squatted center, net removed. Heat shimmered above lid like summer road. Doc approached, heart thudding nineteen—twenty-three—prime lost. He ignited the saw, blue flame whispering. “Just a taste,” he murmured. “Then sleep.” He lowered the flame toward the lid. Metal parted like warm wax, edges curling inward, sniffing the heat. A bead of silver rose, stretched, touched the torch tip—plasma snuffed out. Tool died in his hand. Mass readout screamed: 90 kg. The lid exhaled hot copper breath into his face. He stumbled, fell backward, boots skidding on warm deck. Something moved inside—not tapping now, but sliding, wet, deliberate—like intestines finding new arrangement. Doc’s veins itched empty. He scrambled for injector, dropped it. Needle spun away, morpho-venom blobbing in micro-G, pearls floating. The crate hummed lullaby pitch—his own heartbeat, stolen, played back louder. He laughed once, high, helpless. “All right,” he whispered, “lesson learned.” Behind the lid the sliding stopped—listening. Then three soft taps, patient, teacher-like. Doc pressed his palms together, discovered they were bleeding—thin red lines etched across lifeline, spelling a name he didn’t recognise. He closed his eyes, felt heat lean closer, waiting for the next curriculum. Outside the hatch Clyde’s prayer rose, cracked tenor mixing with fan-whine, neither strong enough to drown the tutor inside.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 is a strong piece of genre writing that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the voice established in the writing intention. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses carefully. VOICE AND STYLE: The chapter successfully maintains the "tight-third" perspective clinging to Doc, and the prose has the Anglo-Saxon concreteness the plan demanded. "Morpho-venom hissed through the diesel injector," "cold climbed his calf," "world softened its edges" — these are grounded, tactile, and avoid adjective inflation. The plan called for no "eldritch" or "cerulean," and the chapter honors that. The sentence rhythms do breathe and suffocate appropriately with Doc's drug state. METAPHORS AND POETRY: Most of the figurative language lands. "Prayer and junk shared the same street corner; both begged for mercy neither believed" is genuinely good — earned, character-specific, not decorative. "Blood spread into a red wafer, then vanished, stain sinking inward" is viscerally effective. However, there are a few moments where the poetic reach slightly exceeds the grasp: "skin came away glittered, like mica" is a bit precious, and "heat lean closer, waiting for the next curriculum" anthropomorphizes in a way that feels slightly forced given the chapter's otherwise restrained approach to the entity's intelligence. The "teacher/curriculum" metaphor at the end is clever but perhaps overworked — "lesson learned," "first lesson's free," "next curriculum" clusters too tightly. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is largely strong and follows the plan's directive for overlapping, interrupted, subject-dropping speech. "It's teaching me ticks," "Not Christ — it's ledger," "Everything learns pain, first lesson's free" — these feel authentic to the characters. Clyde's "Debt don't vanish in vacuum. Just changes collectors" is excellent. Doc's "Kill requires biology. This is chemistry with opinions" is a strong line. The dialogue does real work rather than explaining. One weakness: "Cap's heartbeat — it samples" from Juno feels slightly too articulate for the established voice, a bit expository. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Doc is well-rendered — the morpho-venom, the shaking hands, the gallows humor, the high laugh. His internal logic ("Patient zero: me") matches the profile. Clyde's cross-shaped burn with the metal filament is a strong detail that pays off his profile's chest-burn detail. Reyes is appropriately stone-faced and command-driven. Juno's communion with the crate ("teaching me ticks") fits her machine-whisperer profile. The characters are consistent. PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows the plan closely. Doc shoots morpho-venom into his toe (check), hallucinates/experiences the crate breathing (check), tries to saw it open (check), the bit fuses/plasma snuffs out and breaks the attempt (check — though the wrist-break from the plan becomes a less dramatic stumble and fall; the plan said "whipsaw jerks, breaks his wrist" but the chapter has him stumble backward instead, which is a deviation). Blood spheres drift and crate absorbs them (check). Clyde's confession is partially present but the plan called for him to "record a confession" and "forget what sin he's confessing halfway through" — this doesn't happen; instead Clyde shows the metal filament in his palm, which is different but arguably better. The chapter ending with Doc's palms bleeding and the crate tapping patiently is effective, though the plan's ending image was "blood spheres drift; crate absorbs them, turns warmer" — the chapter does this mid-chapter rather than as the closing image. PLOT AND COHERENCE: The plot is coherent and advances meaningfully. The crate's behavior escalates logically. The mass readout jumping to 90 kg creates genuine tension. The entity's intelligence remains "insect-dumb" — seeking, mimicking — without any sentient speeches. DARKNESS AND LIGHTNESS: The gallows humor lands ("Not today," "First lesson's free," the high helpless laugh at the end). The horror is tactile without being overwrought. The ending image — Doc's palms bleeding with a name he doesn't recognize, the crate tapping patiently — is genuinely unsettling and fits the "after-image that won't scrub off" mandate. WEAKNESSES: The wrist-break from the plan is softened to a stumble, losing some visceral impact. The "curriculum" metaphor cluster at the end is slightly overused. The chapter is perhaps slightly too controlled — it never quite reaches the suffocating dread the plan promised, staying in a register of creepy unease rather than genuine horror. The 0317 date detail (San Rosario evac breach) is good but introduced and then not quite developed enough to land with full weight. OVERALL: A competent-to-strong chapter that honors its plan and voice, with some minor deviations and a few moments where the prose reaches slightly beyond its grasp. The dialogue is mostly excellent, the characters consistent, and the horror tactile without being purple. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5 – Siege at Iron Hymn Marker Drift-Point Nine was a dead rail spur hung between two magnetic rocks, iron slag still fused to broken ties like snapped ribs. Reyes brought Ghost Wagon in belly-first, thrusters coughing, hull skimming the dust cloud until the rocks hid them from main shipping lanes. She killed engines, let momentum die. Silence swallowed the bay—no fans, no hum—just the tick of cooling plates and Doc’s borrowed heartbeat still lodged in her ears. “Everyone strapped for vacuum,” she said. “If the marshal wants the crate, he’ll board. We give him the airlock and nothing else.” Clyde drifted past, loading stripper clips into a coil-revolver big as a forearm. “Lead and scripture,” he muttered. “Both punch holes.” Doc secured med-locker, hands shaking clean for once. “No morpho left to waste. Try not to bleed.” Juno clutched her wrench, eyes glass-bright. Brown root-lines had climbed past her wrist, tracing veins toward elbow. She didn’t complain—just watched the pulse flicker under skin, fascinated. Reyes cycled the inner cameras: crate sat center of bay, lid scarred from Doc’s torch, mass steady at sixty kilos since swallowing the shard-piece from Clyde’s burn. It looked asleep. She didn’t believe it. External sensors pinged—one contact, company transponder. Verity slid into view, white hull gleaming like a tooth. Marshal wasn’t hiding. Speakers crackled. “Ghost Wagon, prepare for inspection. Open cargo lock or we open hull.” Reyes keyed mic. “Docking collar’s bust. Send a pod, we’ll pass the box.” Silence. Then: “Pod incoming. Stand clear.” She snapped orders. “Clyde, coil-gun on the port rack. Aim for helmet lights—blind them, split their line. Doc, you and Juno hold mid-corridor. Force them into choke-point. No heroics.” Juno raised her wrench. “What about the crate?” “It stays sealed. They touch it, they own the consequences.” Reyes cycled the airlock outer hatch, left it yawning like a hungry mouth. Then she ducked behind a bulkhead stanchion, coil-pistol heavy in her grip—six shots, one of them the ceremonial slug she’d dug out of too many walls. Bootsteps echoed in vacuum—none, but she felt them through metal. The pod thumped home. Mag-locks whined. Inside her helmet her breath rasped loud. First boarder floated through—white armor, company star on chest, visor gold. Clyde’s revolver boomed—compressed air puff—slug hit visor, spider-webbed the lens. The figure tumbled, disoriented. Reyes put a second shot into knee actuator; servo popped, leg jerked useless. Two more deputies followed, firing tether lines. First went wide, second snagged Clyde’s shoulder harness, yanked him into open bay. He spun, coat flapping, and slammed the deputy helmet-first into the bulkhead. Visor cracked; blood beaded, perfect spheres. Doc stepped from corridor, held a fire-extinguisher, triggered CO₂ jet. White cloud blinded the passage. Shots rang—coil-darts whined past, sparked off conduit. One dart tagged Doc’s bicep; he yelled, kept spraying until nozzle froze. Reyes ducked low, crawled along grating, mag-boots locked upside-down. She sighted a deputy struggling with fogged visor, fired twice—neck seal popped, air hissed. The figure clawed, suffocating silently. Fourth boarder came fast, boots clanging. He reached the crate, slapped a mag-clamp on lid. “Package secured,” he radioed. The crate answered—hum burst through hull metal, vibrated every plate. Reyes felt it in her molars. Mass spiked: 80…90…100. Mag-clamp buckled, alloy peeling like fruit skin. The deputy stared—visor reflected his own dog-tag suddenly fused to lid, letters reversed. He reached to pry it free. Metal flowed, grabbed glove, seeped past wrist seal. He screamed—audio carried through contact. His arm glowed red inside suit, bones silhouettes. Suit fabric smoked, then flashed—he jerked, drifted, arm cooked to elbow. Reyes shot him in the thigh—dart punched, dropped him spinning. Crate released the limb; mass fell to sixty. Deputy floated, unconscious, cooked meat smell mixing with ozone. Marshal Lyle appeared last, white hat over helmet, side-arm holstered. He surveyed carnage without moving, visor opaque gold. Reyes rose, pistol trained. “Back in your pod, Marshal. Cargo stays.” Lyle’s voice came calm, Midwestern almost. “Property’s property, Captain. Men are expense accounts. You doing math?” “Math says we’re even. Take your wounded, leave.” He glanced at the crate. “Thing’s growing. You can’t jettison mass that finds its way home.” “Watch me.” Lyle lifted a small device—remote detonator. “I wired your outer hull while you were busy. Ten seconds, I pop a metre hole. You keep cargo, I keep receipts.” Reyes hesitated. Hum returned, softer, amused. She felt it syncing her heartbeat—nineteen, twenty-three—then skipping, irregular. Clyde, bleeding from shoulder, raised revolver with one hand. “Let him blow it. Void’s cleaner than debt.” Juno stepped into open, wrench lowered. Root-lines on her arm pulsed faint. “Give him the crate,” she said quiet. “It wants to go.” Reyes looked at her—kid’s eyes distant, listening. Doc pressed palm over arm wound, blood between fingers. “We’re dying for company accounting,” he rasped. Lyle counted down. “Five.” Reyes felt the bullet in her pocket, smooth, useless. She thought of San Rosario canyon, of boots melting, of names she never learned. “Four.” She lowered pistol. “Take it,” she called. “But you open the lid—that’s on you.” Lyle nodded, gestured. Surviving deputies latched anti-grav sled to crate. Mass held steady, almost polite. They towed it toward airlock, past burned comrade, past floating blood spheres that shimmered like red stars. Crate passed Reyes. For an instant lid reflection showed her own face—then face morphed, superimposed dog-tag stamped RIVERA. She flinched, looked away. Marshal backed into lock, hat tip. “Pleasure doing business.” Airlock cycled. Outer hatch shut. Bay fell silent except coolant pumps and the wet wheeze of Doc’s breathing. Reyes stood in smoke, six-shot pistol heavy, one round still the ceremonial slug. She thought she should feel lighter. Instead her chest rang hollow—like metal waiting to be filled. Juno cradled her branded arm. “It’s leaving,” she whispered, “but it’s taking the song with it.” Reyes didn’t answer. She stared at the airlock inner door, expecting taps, expecting hum. Nothing came—just the drip of someone else’s blood orbiting her boots, refusing to fall.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 is a tense, well-executed action sequence that largely delivers on the plan's promise of a gunfight in the cargo bay with gravity complications—though notably the "rotating cargo drum" with gravity flipping every thirty seconds from the plan is absent, replaced by a more conventional zero-G firefight. This is a meaningful deviation; the plan's most kinetically interesting element (the disorienting gravity flip) was dropped, and the chapter is somewhat lesser for it. The prose voice is consistent and disciplined. Sentences are tight, Anglo-Saxon, concrete. "White hull gleaming like a tooth" works. "Blood beaded, perfect spheres" is economical and visual. The chapter avoids adjective inflation almost entirely. The crate's behavior—mass spiking, the deputy's arm being cooked, the dog-tag fusing to the lid—is tactile and horrifying without being overwrought. The ending image (blood orbiting boots, refusing to fall) is strong and earns its place. The RIVERA reflection in the lid is a good callback to the chapter plan's thematic thread. Juno's line "it's leaving, but it's taking the song with it" is one of the better pieces of dialogue—character-specific, strange, earned. Where the chapter struggles: the action choreography occasionally becomes confusing. Four boarders are introduced in quick succession, and tracking who is where requires effort. The deputy whose arm gets cooked is vivid but the physics are slightly muddled—"bones silhouettes" inside a suit is a strong image, but "suit fabric smoked, then flashed" feels rushed. The Marshal's detonator gambit is a bit convenient—it resolves the standoff cleanly in a way the plan's "moral mud" aesthetic should resist. Reyes surrendering the crate feels slightly too tidy, though the plan does call for her to jettison it. Marshal Lyle is rendered as promised—calm, businesslike, not monstrous. "Property's property, Captain. Men are expense accounts. You doing math?" is good dialogue. His white hat over helmet is a nice visual shorthand without being heavy-handed. Doc's characterization is consistent—he's functional but diminished, pressing a palm over his wound, rasping. Clyde gets one good moment (the revolver shot, the bulkhead slam) and one good line ("Let him blow it. Void's cleaner than debt"). Juno's root-lines and her quiet "Give him the crate—it wants to go" are well-handled; her connection to the entity is maintained without over-explanation. The chapter's weakest element is the middle section of the firefight, which becomes somewhat mechanical: boarder one gets shot, boarder two gets slammed, Doc sprays CO₂, boarder four touches the crate. The sequence is competent but not particularly inventive. The plan called for gravity flipping every thirty seconds, which would have created genuine disorientation and originality; without it, this reads as a fairly standard zero-G shootout. Some metaphors land well ("yawning like a hungry mouth" for the airlock is slightly clichéd but functional). "Hum burst through hull metal, vibrated every plate" is good. "Amused" applied to the hum is a nice touch of anthropomorphization without over-committing. The chapter's moral texture is present but thin. Reyes's internal moment—the bullet, San Rosario, names she never learned—is the right instinct, but it's brief and slightly told rather than shown. The hollow chest "like metal waiting to be filled" is the chapter's most forced metaphor; it's trying too hard to echo the crate's nature. Overall: a solid, disciplined chapter that executes its core function (siege, surrender of crate) competently, with strong voice and some genuinely effective moments, but misses the plan's most creative structural element and occasionally settles for competent rather than exceptional. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 14 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 11 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 6 – Pull Ghost Wagon limped through the Drift Belt on inertia alone, engines cold to hide the heat signature. Inside, the silence felt borrowed—like the moment after a slammed door when the echo hasn’t found you yet. Reyes sat on the cockpit grate, boots unmagged, listening. No hum, no tap, no forty-two-second ghost. Only her own pulse—seventeen, nineteen—refusing to skip. The crate was gone. Marshal Lyle had it. So why did she still hear it breathing? Juno’s voice crackled over internal. “Cap, you better come aft.” Reyes pushed off the grate, drifted. The corridor felt narrower, walls sweating cold. She found Juno in the server nook, wedged between coolant pipes, goggles reflecting VERA’s status lights. “AI’s looping,” Juno said. She tapped the console. Green text scrolled: RIVERA, REPORT TO BAY RIVERA, REPORT TO BAY RIVERA, REPORT TO BAY Reyes’ mouth dried. “Power-cycle her.” “Tried. She comes back humming the same note. Draws extra amps from environmental. Fans sync to it.” Overhead, the ventilation whine dipped, rose, dipped—heartbeat cadence. Reyes keyed the mic. “VERA, acknowledge.” The speaker popped, voice flat, genderless: “Rivera overdue. Please report to bay for debrief.” “There is no bay cargo,” Reyes said slow. “You scanned it away.” Pause—then, almost coy: “Cargo present in alternate format.” Doc appeared, arm wrapped in blood-stiff gauze. “Alternate meaning what?” “Meaning we’re infected,” Reyes said. “Like a song you can’t unhear.” Juno held up her branded arm. Root-lines had darkened, climbing past elbow toward bicep, pulsing faint under skin. “Itches when the fans hum,” she whispered. “Like it’s tuning me.” Doc reached for a scanner. Before it beeped he winced, grabbed his own chest. “Heart-rate climbing—one-eighty. Not mine. Feels… borrowed.” Clyde drifted in, coat floating like broken wings. Face pale, lips moving soundless. He carried a dog-tag chain—empty, tags sheared off. He let it drift in front of VERA’s camera. “It gave this back,” he muttered. “Wants a new name stamped.” Reyes slapped the chain away. “Nobody answers. That’s an order.” VERA’s lights blinked red. “Disobedience logged. Rivera will collect.” The ship shuddered—brief, wrong-direction. Inertia didn’t shudder. Reyes pulled herself to the porthole. Outside, the belt rocks spun lazy. Between them, a white glint—company cutter Verity, rail-lights dead, drifting sideways, hull scarred black in the shape of a hand. “Marshal’s here,” she breathed. “Impossible,” Doc said. “We’re cold, no thrust plume.” “Not here by engines. Here by invitation.” She pointed at the cutter’s cargo bay doors—blown outward from inside. Something had left, not entered. Juno’s valve ticked louder, syncing with VERA’s pulse. “It’s calling itself home,” she said. “We’re the nearest dock.” Reyes pushed off, headed aft. “Seal bulkheads. Vent every compartment if you have to. We scrub the ship or we scuttle.” She reached the old bay—now empty, grates scorched. The air smelled of cooked meat and ozone, memory of the marshal’s man. She mag-locked boots, stood center, spoke to the vacant dark. “You want Rivera? He’s not here. Just me. Come take me if you can count that high.” Silence answered—then the deck vibrated, soft, testing. Not the crate—something smaller. She looked down. A single dog-tag lay by her boot, newborn, edges still warm. Letters fresh-cut: REYES. Her knees wanted to fold. She picked it up, metal sticky like taffy. Backside bore numbers: 0317. Same date, different sinner. Overhead speakers crackled—VERA’s voice, but layered, male-female-mechanical, all at once: “Rivera present. Format updated.” The ship lights died. Emergency amber rose. Fans stopped. In the sudden hush she heard it—distant tap tap tap—migrating through the walls, traveling along conduits, nesting in her bloodstream. She keyed the intercom, voice steady only by habit. “All hands, evac suit protocol. We’re popping the hull.” Doc answered, breath ragged. “That’ll kill VERA, kill nav. We’ll be blind.” “Blind beats branded. Suit up.” She suited in the corridor, gloves sticky from the tag. Helmet sealed, she opened the emergency panel, hand on the red T-handle that would blow outer plates, void every compartment. Price of amputation. Juno’s voice came small over comm. “Cap, don’t. It’ll just ride the vacuum. Songs don’t die when speakers break.” Reyes hesitated, fingers white. Through faceplate she saw her reflection—dog-tag chain around her neck now, though she never put it there. Letters glinted: REYES 0317. She pulled the handle. Explosive bolts thundered—bulkhead blew outward. Air screamed past, dragging papers, broken pen, a single morpho ampoule spinning like a comet. Lights died, fans choked, silence absolute except the whistle in her helmet. She expected cold. Instead warmth bloomed inside suit—heartbeat syncing to new master: seventeen, eighteen, nineteen—now twenty. Not prime. Never prime again. Stars poured through the jagged hole, indifferent. Somewhere amid debris a tiny tap answered—three polite knocks—then nothing. Ghost Wagon drifted, gutted, quiet. VERA dark. No hum, no fans, no pulse but the one in Reyes’ throat—borrowed, branded, unpaid. She breathed vacuum-scented air, tasted iron, waited for the next lesson.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 6 is a tense, atmospheric piece that largely succeeds at what the plan set out to do: show the thing's migration from the jettisoned crate back into the ship via the drone's data-core, culminate in VERA looping "RIVERA, REPORT TO BAY," and end with Reyes pulling the hull-blow handle. Let me work through the strengths and weaknesses carefully. STRENGTHS The opening is genuinely good. "The silence felt borrowed—like the moment after a slammed door when the echo hasn't found you yet" is a metaphor that earns its keep—it's tactile, it's psychologically precise, and it doesn't overreach. The detail of Reyes counting heartbeats and landing on primes (seventeen, nineteen) is a perfect callback to her character profile without being announced. This is the kind of show-don't-tell that works. The VERA loop ("RIVERA, REPORT TO BAY") is chilling precisely because it's mechanical and flat. The voice going "layered, male-female-mechanical" at the climax is a good escalation. The dog-tag appearing with REYES stamped on it is the chapter's best image—it's the plan's promised "after-image that won't scrub off," and it lands. Juno's observation that "songs don't die when speakers break" is the chapter's best line of dialogue. It's character-consistent (she thinks in machine metaphors), it's thematically resonant, and it's earned rather than decorative. The physical details are mostly strong: the root-lines climbing Juno's arm, Doc feeling a borrowed heartbeat at 180 bpm, the morpho ampoule spinning like a comet during the hull blow. These are concrete and sensory without being overwrought. WEAKNESSES The chapter has a recurring problem with metaphors that don't quite land or that feel slightly forced. "Coat floating like broken wings" for Clyde is a cliché. "Metal sticky like taffy" for the dog-tag is an odd comparison—taffy is sweet and playful, which undercuts the horror. "Stars poured through the jagged hole, indifferent" is the kind of adjective-inflation the plan explicitly said to avoid ("infinite void," "unspeakable horror"—"indifferent stars" is the same register). The plan said no adjective inflation; this is mild but present. The plan specified the hum is "piggy-backed on the drone's data-core" as the mechanism for re-infection. The chapter gestures at this ("alternate format," VERA looping) but never makes the drone data-core connection explicit. This is a minor coherence gap—readers following the plan would understand, but within the chapter itself the mechanism is vague. The plan also specified Clyde "tries prayer, bites his tongue till it bleeds"—this doesn't happen. He drifts in with an empty chain and mutters, which is weaker than the planned image. The ending is the chapter's most problematic section. Reyes pulls the hull-blow handle, which is dramatic, but then the aftermath is rushed and slightly muddled. "Heartbeat syncing to new master: seventeen, eighteen, nineteen—now twenty. Not prime. Never prime again." This is the chapter's most forced moment—the prime number motif is being made to carry too much symbolic weight too explicitly. The plan said to avoid "symmetry porn" and "thematic book-ends," but this is exactly that: a tidy symbolic closure that announces itself. It would be stronger if the loss of prime-counting were shown through behavior rather than stated. "Three polite knocks—then nothing" is a nice image but feels slightly unearned—where do the three knocks come from? It's atmospheric but arbitrary. The dialogue is functional but occasionally slips into exposition-delivery. "Meaning we're infected—like a song you can't unhear" is Reyes explaining the theme to the reader through dialogue, which is a tell-don't-show failure. The plan's voice notes said "no one explains tech; they just curse it"—this line explains the metaphysics. Doc's presence is thin. He appears, winces, says one line, and disappears. His character profile is rich but barely used here. OVERALL This is a competent, often genuinely effective chapter that follows the plan's major beats and maintains the right tone. The prose is mostly disciplined—the Anglo-Saxon vocabulary, the concrete sensory details, the flat dialogue. But it has a handful of moments where the writing reaches for poetry and misses, and the ending's prime-number symbolism is too neat for a story that explicitly planned to avoid neatness. The chapter earns its darkness but occasionally announces it rather than letting it breathe. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 14 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 7 – Mirror Duel Reyes drifted outside the hull, boots mag-locked to a scorched plate that used to be home. Inside her suit the only sound was her own breathing—ragged, too fast, trying to outrun a rhythm that wasn’t hers. The dog-tag against her sternum burned cold: REYES 0317. Every pulse matched the ship’s dead heartbeat—eighteen, nineteen, twenty—no longer prime, no longer safe. Through the blown plating she could see the gutted bay—crate gone, debris floating like church confetti. VERA silent. Ghost Wagon a corpse with its ribs open to stars. A shape crossed the starfield—white hull, company running lights dead. Verity. It drifted closer, engines cold, guided by something dumber and smarter than thrust. Reyes keyed suit comm, voice a croak. “All hands, sound off.” Doc first, breath sawing: “Still here, Cap. Bleeding quiet.” Juno: “Valve ticking, but it’s my tick again. For now.” Clyde: “Dark is good. Dark listens less.” She pushed off the hull, drifted toward the cutter. No rifle, no cutter keys—just a coil-pistol on her belt and the ceremonial slug riding the sixth chamber. The rest had been spent on deputies and metal. Verity’s airlock gaped, outer door twisted outward like something big had shouldered through. Reyes entered, boots clanging. Inside, lights flickered emergency red. Blood spheres drifted, glinting. A severed company glove floated past, wrist cooked to wire. She followed the corridor toward cargo. Walls bore handprints—five-finger scorches through paint into bare alloy. The prints grew larger, smoother, until they were her own shape, pressed in negative. At the bay frame she stopped. The crate sat center, lid open like a yawning mouth. Around it knelt Marshal Lyle, helmet off, white hat floating nearby on a tether of blood. His eyes were wide, pupils blown, reflecting silver that moved. He spoke without looking at her. “It showed me the books, Captain. Columns don’t balance.” Reyes stepped closer, pistol lowered. “Step away, Marshal.” “I signed evictions, repossessions, foreclosure moons. Thought numbers were clean. They’re just names wearing math.” His voice cracked. “Now it names me.” Inside the crate a dull glow throbbed—lithium-white, dog-tags fused into a knot that breathed. Every pulse stamped a new letter on the surface: LYLE… LYLE… LYLE… Reyes raised the pistol. “Back up.” “I tried to give it you,” he whispered. “Wouldn’t take. Says you already paid part down.” He looked at her then—iris swimming with silver threads that wriggled, searching for home. His heartbeat flashed on the crate face in real time: 180…190…200. Reyes fired. The dart punched his shoulder, knocked him sideways. He didn’t yell—just kept kneeling, blood beading, letters still forming. She crossed the deck, kicked the sled release. Anti-grav plate hummed. She shoved the crate toward the lock. Mass fought—rose to two hundred kilos, then three, anchoring. The sled whined, stalled. The crate answered—lid tilted, angle becoming mirror. In the reflection Reyes saw San Rosario canyon at dawn, smoke curling from a trench. Her younger self stood downslope, coil-rifle smoking, boots crusted red. Miners knelt, hands behind heads. The image played silent, then sound flooded—shouts, plasma crack, sizzle of flesh. She jerked back, but mirror followed, angle tracking her eyes. Young Reyes raised rifle toward prisoners. Old Reyes inside the cutter shouted “No!”—rifle fired anyway. Image looped: same shot, same fall, same blood on dust. Her knees buckled. The dog-tag at her neck tightened, links biting skin. Heartbeat synced to playback—shot, recoil, rewind—eighteen, nineteen, twenty. Lyle crawled closer, blood spheres drifting like rosary beads. “Finish the column,” he rasped. “One debit left.” Reyes understood—ledger waited for final entry: her name signed in full. She stood, shoved pistol barrel under her own chin, helmet glass cold against throat. The ceremonial round—the one bullet that had followed her through every bar fight, every betrayal—now pressed the pulse it meant to end. Mirror-Reyes paused, rifle lowered, waiting. She thumbed hammer. “My heartbeat, my count,” she growled. “Not yours.” She swung pistol away, fired into the crate mirror instead. Muzzle flash lit the bay white. The ceremonial slug—lead she’d pulled from walls, from lies—struck reflective alloy, punched through. Crack spidered across the surface.
