Voice Intention says “sentences short and visual” – already sounds like AI impersonating 1980s Leonard. Human alternative: let the prose get loopy when Jay is concussed, let it run on when she’s manic, let it fragment when her eardrum bursts. ## B. Stakes Plan gives her a cause (save kids) AND money AND justice. That’s three safety nets. Human might choose one dirty motive: Jay just wants the cash in the safe, the kids are collateral guilt. Reader doesn’t know for three chapters if she’ll sacrifice them. ## C. Failure beats Every gadget works. No mention of partial success. Human version: nail-gun jams after second nail, she has to finish the guy by stabbing with a toddler’s safety scissor – loses a tooth in the process. ## D. Sensory honesty LLM forgets smell. A real house after electrocuted bowel release smells like copper pennies and cheap chlorine; it makes you gag while you cheer. ## E. Ending Tuition-paid, sunrise walk, cute wave – a Hallmark card sprinkled with blood. Human might end on Jay vomiting behind the ambulance, bonds fluttering into the gutter, cop asking “Who’s your lawyer?” and she can’t remember her own fake name. # Brainstorm – how to de-machine the story 1. Write one chapter drunk, revise sober Literally dictate it with slurred speech; capture ums, curses, half-finished jokes. Trim later. 2. Break the grid Let Ch 3 be 300 words of nothing but sound design: breathing, floorboard creak, kid humming in wardrobe. Let Ch 6 balloon to 2 000 words of real-time panic. 3. Messy morality Maybe Jay’s first explosive accidentally blinds the rookie (Lyle) who was trying to quit the crew and pay his daughter’s chemo. She hears him whimper about little Emma while she’s taping his eyes shut. That blood never washes off. 4. Unreliable injuries She thinks her eardrum bursts – actually it’s adrenaline + panic. Vargas’s gun was blanks. She killed two men for phantom reasons. Discover it later, leave it ambiguous. 5. Cut the catch-phrase Or let Marco shout it at school the next day, get suspended. Jay cringes; she hates catch-phrases. 6. Missing kid Lily hides so well nobody finds her until the final paragraph – Jay’s victory lap is hollow, keeps hearing giggles inside the walls. 7. Format vandalism Insert a real NextDoor thread between chapters: neighbours arguing whether the explosions were fireworks or gender-reveal parties. Caps-lock racism, emoji outrage, someone selling essential oils. 8. Money problem The duffel is mostly dyed bait bills; ink pack explodes on her in the ambulance. Tuition dream gone; she’s just a killer with purple hands. 9. Time leak Story starts at 9:17 p.m., ends 9:41 p.m. subjective time – Jay’s watch is smashed, she’s lost temporal grip. Chapters are numbered by pulse rate, not clock. 10. Political friction Jay is Black; the ex-cop intruders use cop jargon to terrorise her. Aftermath news calls her “gang-related suspect.” White mum and dad get tearful TV interview. Reader feels the real villain is the culture that trained both cops and criminals. 11. Language dirt Let dialogue repeat, overlap, step on itself: “Get down—” “I’m—” “Shut up shut up—” Transcribe inaccurately: “gonna” becomes “gon,” punctuation evaporates mid-sentence. 12. Imperfect research Jay’s bomb recipe almost works – she misremembers the ratio, burns her own calf. Google search history later shows “how to treat chemical burn fast” and “can you die from glitter in bloodstream.” # Take-away The plan is a solid scaffold, but every rung is sanded pin-smooth. To make it breathe, we need splinters: failures, sensory overload, moral mildew, structural asymmetry, and at least one moment that refuses to satisfy. Give the reader the win they came for, but leave the taste of copper foil in their mouth so they can’t quite whistle cheerfully afterwards.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This is a one-night, sweat-stained pulp reckoning—exactly 8 000 words, no moral safety net, no tidy TED-talk ending. - **Voice**: First-person present, Jay’s inner monologue: half-art-student, half-bomb-tech, 100 % exhausted. Sentences breathe, stumble, repeat when she’s rattled; they tighten into staccato when she’s hunting. Metaphors arrive raw (“the air tastes like battery acid and cheap vanilla plug-in”) then vanish—she doesn’t have time to be poetic. - **Violence**: Household, improvisational, ugly. Nail-gun misfires, she finishes with kid’s left-handed safety scissors. Blood smells like pennies and chlorine; she throws up twice and keeps moving. Kids live, but Lily’s night-light is forever ruined for her. - **Stakes**: Save the kids (because she couldn’t save her little brother in a similar break-in), grab enough cash to disappear, survive the cops who’ll assume she’s the threat. Victory costs her fingerprints, half her hearing, and any illusion that she’s one of the good guys. - **Structure**: 8 chapters ≈ 1 k each. No chapter cliff-hanger is a guaranteed win; some end mid-explosion, one ends with her hyperventilating in a cupboard. Time-stamps warp—her watch shatters Ch 3; after that we’re on pulse and sirens. - **Politics & class**: Ex-cop intruders wear badges like backstage passes; Jay is Black, broke, and already on the local PD’s “nuisance list.” News footage will call her an “unknown female suspect.” Reader feels the double standard without lecture. - **Catch-phrase**: She never says it. A dying intruder wheezes, “We picked… wrong… bitch—” Jay answers, “I’m not the bitch, I’m the babysitter,” more to herself than to him. Line is half-baked, half-desperate—no trailer moment. - **End**: Morning. House is a crime scene. Cash in the safe turns out dye-packed; she limps away with $37 and a burner phone. Tuition dream dies, but the kids wave from social-worker car. Last image: Jay buying off-brand bleach at 7-Eleven, blood still crackling in her hair. - **Avoid**: clever Rube-Goldberg kills, perfect bombs, cop high-five, Reddit-friendly one-liners, sequel bait. - **Aim for**: one messy win, sensory overload, reader finishes feeling the cops might still shoot her next. # Chapter Planning ## CHAPTER 1 – 9:17 pm “Volcano” Jay sets off a baking-soda volcano for Marco and Lily, films it on cracked iPhone. Parents leave in a hurry, Dad mutters “keep doors locked, lot of nuts out there.” Jay pockets the leftover vinegar—habit. House is smart-wired; she disables interior cams so kids can dance dumb. Unknown to her, exterior cam shows fake patrol cruiser idling. Chapter ends when doorbell rings, she peeks, sees badge glare, heart drops. ## CHAPTER 2 – 9:41 pm “Badge in the Teeth” Four intruders force entry—ex-cops, windbreakers, real warrant paper (forged). Leader Vargas zip-ties Jay first; she’s “the help.” They demand safe location; she genuinely doesn’t know. Tech guy Larson kills power; smart-house voice glitches, creepy. Kids hustled upstairs. Jay palms box-cutter by slicing her own palm—blood makes handle sticky. Chapter ends with Vargas back-handing her, taste of iron. ## CHAPTER 3 – 10:05 pm “Garage Chemistry” She wriggles free, wedged between recycling bins. Grabs cordless nail-gun (jammed first try), pool shock, half-bottle of vodka. Builds a quick-and-dirty chloramine smoke bomb in kid’s lunch thermos; no timer—she’ll have to yank the rag manually. Accidental inhale burns her throat; eyes stream. First partial success: Larson chases, thermos pops, he staggers blind, nails miss his head but shred his bicep. Chapter ends gun-tossed, she dives under minivan, watch shatters—time unspools. ## CHAPTER 4 – 10:?? pm “Pink Pool Light” Dixon drags her out, minivan door slams her ribs. Jay yanks garden hose, loops it round fence post; van rolls, knocks Dixon into pool. She kicks inverter in after him—lights pop, water steams pink. Not sure if he’s dead; she’s shaking too hard to check. Vargas realizes she’s mil-tech; racial slur slips. Jay’s inner flash: brother’s casket, military fold flag. Chapter ends Vargas firing into pool water—Dixon finished off, Jay puking behind barbecue. ## CHAPTER 5 – 11:30 pm “Wine Moms & Bearer Bonds” Smoke still thick. Vargas cracks hidden safe behind wine rack—reveals bearer bonds + encrypted USB. He brags parents launder cartel cash; kids are loose ends. Jay sees USB as ticket out—immunity, maybe. Bargain fails; Vargas shoots rookie Lyle (panic, racial spite). Blood splatter on Jay’s sneakers; she pockets USB. Sets off second bomb—vinegar & baking-soda pressure in wine bottle—shrapnel of glass and merlot. Chapter ends her sprinting upstairs to kids, calf sliced, leaving bloody footprints. ## CHAPTER 6 – 12:04 am “Lego Caldera” Playroom floor becomes minefield—scattered Lego, Jay barefoot, pain equals awake. Lily sobs; Marco live-streams on cracked phone (battery 4 %). Jay rigs final trap: nail-gun tied to door-handle, trip-line of sparkly jump-rope. Dixon (still alive, half-scalded) bursts in, gun wavers. Line fires—nails hit his collarbone, not fatal. He grabs Lily. Jay drops weapon, hands up. Chapter ends on her whisper: “Take me. Let the girl pee first,” stalling, mind blank, real fear. ## CHAPTER 7 – 12:?? am “I’m the Babysitter” Marco hits smart-speaker: “Play Baby Shark max volume.” Feedback squeal, Dixon flinches. Jay shoulder-charges, they crash into dollhouse; wood splinters. Scissors finish—she feels cartilage pop, wet wheeze. Vargas crawls in, leg shredded by earlier bottle. Mutual exhaustion—guns empty. He spits threat; she answers, smashes his radio, hammers nail through USB into his palm—symbolic evidence. He rasps the half-phrase, dies. Chapter ends Jay convulsing, ears ring, silence like cotton. ## CHAPTER 8 – 5:47 am “Bleach & Blue Sky” Real cops arrive, treat her as suspect first. Detective Ramirez (Latina, tired eyes) clocks the USB, switches tone. Parents pulled from gala in cuffs, perform grief for news cam. bonds in safe triggered dye-pack—ruined. EMT wants hospital; Jay signs out AMA. She limps to 7-Eleven, buys off-brand bleach, chugs chocolate milk, vomits neon. Kids wave from social-worker car; Lily mouths “thank you” window fog. Jay’s final thought: tuition gone, fingerprints scarred, sunrise too bright. She walks on, barefoot, milk mustache pink with someone else’s blood.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Jay Munroe – “the wrong babysitter” **Background**: Grew up in Fayetteville, NC, raised by an over-worked supply-sergeant mom after dad split. Little brother Andre died at 12 during a home-invasion robbery while Jay (17) was meant to be watching him; she froze, hid in closet. Military became escape hatch + self-punishment. Four years Air-Force EOD, one tour Kandahar. Dishonourable discharge after punching Lt-Col who tried to cover-up a drone strike that cooked a school. Took the fall, lost GI Bill. Now 24, shares mouldy duplex, delivers food on e-bike, majors in studio art at commuter college because clay doesn’t ask questions. **Personality / ticks**: Stays awake tracing the ceiling crack until she hears the house settle; carries kiddie sticker-prizes in pocket for instant smiles; counts exits everywhere; absent-mindedly braids extension cord when nervous; mouth-breathes when anxious – slight whistle; draws stick-bomb diagrams on diner napkins then orders pancakes “for the smell.” **Flaws**: Guilt junkie – volunteers for danger to balance cosmic ledger. Terrible with money (paypal overdraft -$37). Won’t accept help; would rather super-glue split lip than admit pain. Sleeps in sports bra because wired bras “feel like tourniquets.” **Physicality**: 5-6, lean-shouldered, natural hair twisted into short free-form locs she dyes purple at tips. Hands scarred (frost-bit from disposing ordnance in winter field). Left thumbnail black from dropped palette knife. Carries herself toes-first, like she’s testing ice. When still, she grips her own wrist, anchoring. **Motive / allegiance**: Keep these kids breathing; grab enough untraceable cash to clear Mom’s mortgage and vanish; never freeze again. **Voice samples**: Internal: *“Count the heartbeats, Jay. One-Mississippi two—Andre’s ghost is still in the closet, still twelve, still waiting for me to open the door.”* Spoken, wiping blood: *“Chocolate milk fixes most things. If it don’t, we graduate to det-cord.”* --- # Marco Rojas – 8-year-old co-protagonist **Background**: Parents launder cartel money through import/export front; raised by nannies since infancy. Gifted, bored, already figured out parental lie matrix. Builds Lego bots that bully other toys; live-streams because attention is currency. Thinks “police” are just another gang with better lights. **Ticks / flaws**: Repeats last word of every sentence under breath when scared; hides snacks inside couch for “siege days”; trusts adults zero percent but latches onto competence – decides Jay is “approved.” **Physicality**: Skinny, ears stick out, perpetual bed-head. Wears astronaut PJs two sizes small. Carries tablet like oxygen tank. **Motive**: Stay alive; protect little sister; maybe go viral doing it. **Voice**: *“If they shoot us the views will crash—bad optics, Jay.”* *“I turned off geolocation. Dad says that keeps the tax man away.”* --- # Lily Rojas – 6, emotional core **Background**: Still believes parents commute to “paper factory.” Sleeps with LED unicorn that cycles pink-blue. Selective mute when strangers shout; communicates via eyebrow semaphore. **Ticks**: Collects smooth pebbles, labels them with day’s mood in crayon. Holds breath when lying (learned from Mom). **Physicality**: Round cheeks, hair too fine to stay in braids. Bare feet slapping like sticky notes. **Motive**: Keep unicorn glowing; believes Jay is literal superhero once she sees purple hair in torch beam. **Voice** (whisper): *“Rock says today is ‘brave.’ I’m giving it to you.”* --- # Vargas – crew leader, ex-narcotics detective, 44 **Background**: 18 years LAPD, forced out after Internal Affairs tied him to missing evidence. Runs “recovery” squad that retrieves dirty money for 20 %. Grew up in Boyle Heights, joined Army then PD to escape gang life; became a more legal gang. Divorced, sends insulin money to diabetic daughter he’s not allowed to see. **Personality**: Calculating, but racial resentment leaks when stressed. Quotes department policy while breaking fingers. Wears aftershave that smells like high-school locker room – citrus over mildew. **Flaws**: Needs final big score to pay daughter’s surgery; ego cannot accept being out-thought by “some babysitter.” Old knee injury – stairs hurt, pride hurts more. **Physicality**: 6-1, linebacker gone soft, grey buzz-cut, moustache over-trimmed. Carries custom 1911 with Punisher skull grip (gift from rookie year). **Motive**: Bonds in safe = $2 M = daughter’s life + beach exile. **Voice**: *“We’re not robbers, Miss Munroe. We’re repossession agents with a court-signed invite.”* Internal: *“If I crawl out of here legless at least the VA will finally take my calls.”* --- # Dixon – crew muscle, 38 **Background**: Former Sheriff’s deputy, fired for steroid trafficking. Took up bouncing, then debt-collection. Gym rat, believes in “protein and payback.” Raised on survivalist podcasts; hoards freeze-dried beef in van. **Ticks**: Sniffs ammonia before action (reminds him of football). Calls women “ma’am” even while strangling. Keeps count of “confirmed” kills like batting average. **Flaws**: Panics when plan deviates; water-phobic since nearly drowned as kid. Needs Vargas for legitimacy. **Physicality**: 6-5, 260 lbs, neck disappears into skull. Tattoo of Virgin Mary crying dollar signs. **Motive**: Prove useful enough to stay in the money circle; secretly wants podcast sponsorship about “tactical lifestyle.” **Voice**: *“Ma’am, stop wiggling, you’ll bruise worse.”* Internal: *“One more body, I hit double digits—podcast ads pay per corpse, right?”* --- # Larson – tech / surveillance, 31 **Background**: Communications intel in National Guard, discharged for hacking superior’s bank. Makes living jamming cams for stash-house robberies. Lives in Koreatown studio with wall of retro game consoles. **Personality**: Talks in bandwidth metaphors, hates loud noises, chews sunflower seeds to keep hands busy. **Flaws**: Convinced he’s smartest in room; underestimates analogue threats (like scissors). Asthmatic – smoke bombs are kryptonite. **Physicality**: Thin, pale, hoodie reeks of vape juice. Carries tablet + foldable keyboard like teddy bear. **Motive**: Wants payout to produce indie VR shooter; sees tonight as “data collection.” **Voice**: *“Signal’s clean, she’s dark—wait, why’s she baking soda trending?”* Internal: *“If I lose fingers I can’t code. Fingers > friends.”* --- # Detective Ramirez – 48, homicide, integrity on life-support **Background**: 25 years watching rich criminals walk. Grew up in same township as Jay’s mom; recognizes the name. Keeps antacids in every pocket. **Personality**: Listens more than speaks; files away tremors in voices. Knows media will spin this as “gang-related home invasion.” **Motive**: Use USB to nail cartel lawyers, maybe city council; protect Jay from Internal Affairs meat-grinder. **Voice**: *“You’re bleeding on my evidence, kid. Try to aim left.”* Internal: *“If I screw this collar the brass will bury me under the new precinct parking lot.”*

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# CHAPTER 1 – 9:17 pm “Volcano” The baking soda smells like the cheap kind, the five-pound bag Mom used to keep to soak up oil spots on the driveway. I pour too fast and the volcano—store-bought plastic, painted neon orange—spews a froth that looks like dishwasher puke. Lily claps anyway. Marco narrates into his cracked tablet, voice piping with that influencer cadence he’s too young to know he’s copying. “Observe the exothermic reaction,” he says, then under his breath, “exothermic.” Repeats it like a prayer. I wipe my hands on the dish towel tucked into my cut-offs and check the time. 9:17. Parents are late leaving. Of course. The air conditioner gave up an hour ago, so every window’s wedged open, letting in August like a dog that won’t stop humping your leg. Sweat runs between my shoulder blades, following the channel an IED vest carved there years ago. I shift, try to breathe. Mr. Rojas clops down the stairs reeking of aftershave, Mrs. Rojas behind him in sequins that catch the recessed lights like fish hooks. They look right through me, same way drill sergeants look through trainees who’ve already washed out. “Remember,” Mrs. Rojas says, squeezing Lily’s cheeks, “if anyone asks, we’re at the Marriott charity gala.” She says it like she’s practiced. I file it away with the other weird: new security cameras last week, Mr. Rojas whisper-arguing on the patio about bearer something, a panel in the wine cellar I noticed when I fetched apple juice—flush to the wall, no handle. Not my business. My business is $18 an hour, keep the kids alive, finish an art degree I can’t pay for. Mr. Rojas taps his Rolex. “Back by two. Doors stay locked.” He says it to Marco, not me, like the eight-year-old’s head of security. They leave. I listen for the deadbolt but it never clicks. Engine fades. House exhales expensive emptiness. Lily tugs my shirt. “Jay, can we make it explode again?” “Bedtime in twenty. One more eruption, then teeth, pajamas, unicorn lights.” Marco already has the vinegar bottle. “Larger nozzle yields greater plume,” he narrates. I wonder if he even knows he’s talking out loud. While he measures, I slip the leftover vinegar into a sports bottle, cap it. Vinegar plus pool shock equals chloramine gas if you’re desperate. I’m not there yet, but I like options. We set the volcano on the kitchen island. I drip food coloring—blood-blue—into the crater. Lily counts down from five, voice high and sure. On one I dump soda. The foam rockets up, splatters the pendant lights, drips like alien icing. Lily squeals so hard she snorts. Marco spins the tablet, catching every angle. For a second the kitchen smells like elementary school science and not like dread. Twenty minutes later pajamas are on, teeth half-brushed (I pretend not to see Lily swallow toothpaste). We do the hallway light-switch relay: on-off-on until Lily’s unicorn night-light blooms pink-blue-pink. She needs it; the LED costs more than my weekly groceries. Marco claims he’s too old, but he still hugs the doorframe until the cycle starts. I leave their door ajar exactly two inches—the width of my fist. Back in the kitchen I wipe blue foam off marble counters. My cut-offs ride up; scar tissue on the back of my thigh grabs like Velcro. I ignore it, rinse dishes, stack them quiet. The smart fridge hums louder than the AC. On its screen the security app shows four cameras: front walk, side gate, pool, garage. All green. I swipe to close it but pause. A fifth thumbnail flickers, new to me: wine-cam, interior. It’s offline, gray static. I tap. Nothing. Maybe the vinegar steam fried it. Maybe. I pour flat Dr Pepper, stand at the sink, stare through window into cul-de-sac. Streetlight halos the fake Victorian mailbox. Beyond it, idling at the curb, is a white Ford Explorer, spotlight mounted, no light bar. My pulse taps once, hard. Could be neighborhood patrol, could be anyone. I count to thirty. Driver door opens. Man steps out: polo, cargo pants, badge clipped at belt. He doesn’t close the door fully, cop habit—keep noise down. Three more shapes exit. No radios squawk. They move like a stack, one hand on the next guy’s back. Military spacing. My stomach drops into my shoes. I kill the pendant lights, kitchen now lit only by fridge. Blue foam glows radioactive. I back-pedal to the island, unscrew the sports bottle cap, sniff: vinegar, sharp, honest. Pool shock is in garage on child-locked shelf. Ten steps too far. I need time. Doorbell rings—two short, one long. Polite, urgent. I don’t move. My phone buzzes: unknown number. I let it roll to voicemail. The app shows front-porch cam: badge glint, guy holds it high so lens catches gold. He smiles like customer service. I exhale through teeth. Options: pretend not home (kids upstairs), open door (maybe legit), grab knife (escalate), grab kids (expose them). I choose stall. I step into view of door glass, keep chain on. “Yeah?” “Evening, ma’am. We have a warrant to search the premises. Stolen artifacts. Need you to open up.” He lifts paper, too far to read. His voice is smooth asphalt—no cracks. “Kids asleep. Come back tomorrow.” My voice cracks like cheap plywood. “Judge says now.” Still smiling. Behind him the others fan, one toward side gate, one crouching at garage keypad. My ribs squeeze. “I’ll call the homeowners.” I reach to close door. His boot jams, chain snapping taut. “Open the door, Miss. We’re coming in.” His eyes flick past me, counting valuables. I see the count. I slam shoulder into wood, chain holds, but barely. Boot retreats. I flip deadbolt, sprint. Heart in throat, I take stairs three at a time. Lily’s unicorn glow leaks under door. I scoop both kids, fingers over mouths before screams form. “It’s a game,” I lie, voice tremor betraying. “Super-spy hide.” Marco’s eyes know better. He clutches tablet like shield. Downstairs: splinter—door gives. Footsteps fan across marble. Male voices, calm, coordinated. I set kids in master walk-in, yank hanging shelves to make cave. “Stay, no sound.” I press vinegar bottle into Marco’s hand. “If anyone opens door except me, squeeze and run.” He nods, eight going on eighty. I pull door, darkness swallowing them. House alarm finally bleeps—entry delay. I never had the code. Thirty seconds and it’ll scream. I need those seconds. I descend hallway quiet, blood ticking in ears. At linen closet I snag towels—wrapping, gagging, muffling. My cut-off pocket holds box-cutter from volcano kit. I flick blade: one inch of desperate. Downstairs the leader speaks soft: “Sweep, locate safe, control the babysitter.” I whisper to myself, to Andre, to whoever listens: “Not tonight.” The alarm hits—shrill, relentless. I use the noise, slide down banister, land in shadow. One guy under foyer chandelier, silhouette gold. I pad behind, towel over mouth, box-cutter ready. The blade shakes like a tuning fork. I breathe once, taste vinegar, and move.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This opening chapter demonstrates considerable craft and largely succeeds at establishing the pulp revenge tale's voice and stakes. Let me examine it systematically. **Voice and Style**: The first-person present tense works well. Jay's voice is distinctive—the EOD background bleeds into her observational habits (counting exits, noting the panel with no handle, pocketing vinegar "out of habit"). The prose breathes with her anxiety without becoming overwrought. Sentences do tighten and loosen appropriately. The line "August like a dog that won't stop humping your leg" is vivid and character-appropriate without being pretentious. Most metaphors land: "sequins that catch the recessed lights like fish hooks" is sharp; "smooth asphalt—no cracks" for Vargas's voice works. **Where the poetry misfires**: "scar tissue on the back of my thigh grabs like Velcro" is functional but slightly clinical. "Blue foam glows radioactive" is fine. Occasionally the metaphors feel slightly assembled rather than organic—"heart in throat" is a cliché that slips through. The "blood ticking in ears" is competent but not fresh. These are minor complaints; the prose doesn't tip into purple territory. **Character Consistency**: Jay is well-rendered against her profile. The vinegar-pocketing habit, the exit-counting, the EOD tactical thinking, the guilt-ledger motivation (protecting these kids because of Andre)—all present and organic. The Andre whisper at the end ("to Andre, to whoever listens") is earned rather than forced. Marco's "exothermic" repetition under his breath is a nice character tick. Lily's unicorn light detail is deployed correctly. **Plot and Plan Adherence**: The chapter follows its plan closely—volcano experiment, parents leaving with suspicious dialogue, Jay noticing the exterior cam with the idling vehicle, doorbell, badge glint, forced entry beginning. The chapter ends exactly where planned: with Jay moving toward confrontation as the alarm sounds. The plan called for the chapter to end "when doorbell rings, she peeks, sees badge glare, heart drops"—the chapter extends slightly beyond this into the initial confrontation setup, which actually improves pacing. **Dialogue**: The dialogue is sparse but functional. "If anyone asks, we're at the Marriott charity gala" is a nice piece of exposition-as-character-reveal. The leader's "We're coming in" is appropriately menacing without being theatrical. Jay's "Not tonight" whisper is a bit of a cliché but fits the pulp register. The dialogue doesn't overstay its welcome. **Show vs. Tell**: Mostly showing. The parents' suspicious behavior is revealed through action and dialogue rather than Jay explaining it. The class dynamics (parents looking through her "same way drill sergeants look through trainees who've already washed out") are shown through comparison rather than stated. The one weak moment: "My stomach drops into my shoes" is telling-adjacent cliché. **Creativity**: The setup is familiar (home invasion, babysitter as unexpected threat) but the execution has genuine texture. The smart-house detail, the wine-cam going offline, the bearer bonds hint—these are competent genre moves. The EOD background as the source of Jay's improvisational chemistry knowledge is well-integrated. Nothing here is startlingly original, but it's executed with enough specificity to feel fresh. **Emotional Engagement**: The chapter succeeds at building dread. The transition from domestic warmth (Lily snorting at the volcano) to threat is well-paced. The unicorn light detail and the "two inches—the width of my fist" precision both work emotionally. The Andre reference lands without being heavy-handed. **Darkness**: The darkness is earned—the threat feels real, the children feel vulnerable, Jay's competence feels hard-won rather than convenient. **Minor weaknesses**: "Heart in throat" and "stomach drops into my shoes" are clichés that a writer of this caliber should have caught. The ending action (sliding down banister, towel over mouth, box-cutter ready) is slightly rushed—the chapter accelerates into action mode a bit abruptly after careful scene-building. The "blade shakes like a tuning fork" is a nice specific image that partially redeems the rushed ending. Overall this is a strong opening chapter that establishes voice, character, and stakes effectively while following its plan faithfully. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# CHAPTER 2 – 9:41 pm “Badge in the Teeth” The alarm drills through the walls, through my teeth, straight into the hinge of my jaw. I land barefoot behind the guy in the foyer—polo stretched tight across traps, cargo pants bloused over combat boots like he’s still on patrol. He’s scanning the balcony rail, gun pointed high. My own pulse is louder than the siren. I loop the bath-towel around his neck, cross ends, yank. Fabric cinches, his head snaps back. The gun barks once into plaster before I kick the wrist; steel skitters under the hall table. He claws backward, nails scraping my forearms. I haul harder, ride his weight until knees buckle. Box-cutter shakes in my right hand—I can’t find an angle that isn’t murder. I’m not there yet. I knee the back of his leg and he folds, gasping, fish-on-deck eyes rolling white. “Clear east!” someone yells—kitchen direction. Boots drum. I ditch the towel, snatch the fallen pistol—a Glock, magazine flush, safety off. My thumb knows the drill; my stomach doesn’t. I back-pedal behind the curved staircase as a second shadow spills across marble. “Larson, status?” the shadow barks. Not panic—irritation. These men expected an empty vault, not resistance. The guy on the floor wheezes, clawing at his throat. I left him alive—mistake or mercy, too late to know. I duck low, hug the wall toward the dining room. Crystal glasses from some corporate dinner still sit rim-up on linen; the chandelier trembles with every alarm pulse. I need the breaker, kill the noise, think. Map of house unfurls in my head: garage sub-panel, right past the laundry. I’d seen it while hunting for detergent to scrub volcano goo. A figure slides through the kitchen arch—slim, hoodie, tablet glow on his face. Tech guy. He taps, cursing. “Cam’s looping, power’s hard—get the girl, she’ll know the safe code.” Girl. Me. My photo must be on their phones. I taste metal. I retreat down the hall, bare feet silent on runner rug. The leader appears at the front door, silhouette against porch light, badge lifted like it still means something. “Ma’am,” he calls, voice smooth, “you’re interfering with a lawful search. Come out, we talk. Kids stay safe.” Kids. He said it like a bargaining chip. Alarm cuts—he’s found the panel. Sudden silence rings harder than noise. I slip into the half-bath, lock the flimsy knob, breathe. Mirror shows me wild: purple locs frizzed, cheek smeared blue from volcano, eyes too white. I rack the slide—round already chambered. Count: four men, maybe five. I have six bullets and no plan. My phone vibrates in my pocket—Marco texting: “they on stairs?” Thumb shaking, I answer: “stay dark. unicorn off.” I add three hammer emojis because words feel stupid. I crack the door. Leader’s voice drifts closer: “Sweep upstairs. Babysitter’s military—probably EOD. Don’t give her time to cook.” Footsteps ascend. My stomach caves. I can’t be floor below while they reach kids. I step out, gun high. “Down here, assholes.” Voice cracks like thin ice. Leader turns—forties, moustache trimmed sharp as a threat. Polo, slacks, badge on belt next to holster. He raises empty palms. “Easy. We’re on the same side.” “Put the gun on the floor.” My aim wobbles; I clench tighter, bones humming. He smiles small, unclips pistol reverse-style, sets it slow. “See? Talk.” I want to believe him the way kids want night-lights—anything to shrink the dark. But Andre’s closet ghost whispers: *they said “police” then too.* I inch backward toward staircase, need visual on upper hall. Leader follows, calm, like herding a skittish horse. “Name’s Vargas. We need a safe in the wine cellar. You open it, we’re gone. Kids sleep through.” “I don’t know any safe.” “Parents didn’t mention? Figures.” He clicks tongue. “Rich folks always leave the help in the dark.” I hate how that stings—help. I’m almost past the stairs. Sudden thud above—door kicked. Lily’s cry pierces drywall. My heart vaults. I pivot, sprint steps two at a time. Vargas shouts “Dixon—hold!” Behind me metal clicks—magazine seated. I don’t turn. Half-landing, shoulder to plaster, I swing gun upward. Dixon fills the upper hall—mountain in tac-vest. He’s got Marco by pajama collar, Lily tucked under other arm like groceries. Both kids silent, eyes moon-wide. “Drop it, sis.” His voice gravel rolled in syrup. Gun barrel kisses Marco’s temple. Skin dimples. The tablet in Marco’s hand drops, clatters, screen spider-webs. Time dilates to heartbeat. I see choices like chalk lines: shoot—maybe hit, maybe kids; surrender—safe code I don’t have; stall—pray for miracle. Sweat slips down my spine, pools at waistband. Vargas appears behind me, breath even. “Lower the weapon. We talk downstairs. Nobody dies.” I picture Andre’s casket, flag folded, Mom refusing tears because base protocol. I lower. Barrel trembles toward hardwood. “Good,” Vargas says. “Zip her.” Dixon releases Marco—kid scrambles to Lily. They back into wall. Zip-tie appears in Vargas hand like magic; he loops my wrists, ratchets tight—plastic bites skin. He lifts the Glock, pockets it smooth. “Now,” he says, guiding me down, “about that safe.” I catch Marco’s eyes—he’s counting, lips moving. Eight years old and already planning. I try to smile, fail. My blood pulses against plastic. One thought loops: *Options, chemistry, time. They fucked with the wrong babysitter—eventually.*
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 of this pulp revenge tale is a tense, kinetic piece that largely succeeds at what it sets out to do, though with some notable unevenness. STRENGTHS: The action choreography is generally competent and grounded. The writer avoids the trap of making Jay superhuman—she's reactive, scared, improvising. The detail of her thumb knowing the Glock drill while her stomach doesn't is a clean, efficient character beat. The zip-tie surrender feels earned rather than contrived. Character consistency is strong. Jay's EOD background informs her tactical thinking without being over-explained. Marco's silent counting at the end is a nice touch consistent with his profile. Vargas's "help" taunt lands as both tactically manipulative and revealing of his character. The Andre ghost reference is brief and functional rather than overwrought. The chapter plan called for: four intruders forcing entry, zip-tying Jay, demanding safe location, Larson killing power, kids hustled upstairs, Jay palming box-cutter by slicing palm, and ending with Vargas back-handing her. The actual chapter diverges significantly—Jay takes much more active resistance early (bath-towel chokehold, grabbing a gun), the box-cutter palm-slice doesn't happen, and the chapter ends with zip-ties rather than a back-hand. This is a meaningful deviation from the plan, though the spirit is preserved. WEAKNESSES: The opening action—Jay choking a guy with a bath towel before the chapter has established how she got there—is disorienting. The plan described a more passive entry where she's zip-tied first; instead she's immediately fighting, which undermines the "they have the upper hand" tension the chapter needs to establish. Some metaphors are strained: "Crystal glasses from some corporate dinner still sit rim-up on linen" feels inserted for atmosphere rather than earned. "Time dilates to heartbeat" is a cliché dressed up as poetry. "Badge lifted like it still means something" is the kind of line that sounds sharp but is actually a bit obvious for this genre. The dialogue is functional but thin. Vargas's lines are serviceable villain-speak without much texture. "We're on the same side" and "Nobody dies" are stock. The "help" taunt is the only line with real bite. The final line—"They fucked with the wrong babysitter—eventually"—is the model inserting the prompt's catchphrase awkwardly as an internal thought. The writing plan explicitly said she never says it; having it appear as an italicized internal monologue feels like the model couldn't resist, and the "—eventually" qualifier makes it worse, not better. The chapter is coherent and moves well, but it's more of a competent genre exercise than something genuinely surprising. The racial dynamics (Vargas's "help" comment) are present but underdeveloped. The promised "forged warrant paper" from the plan never appears. The smart-house voice glitch (described as "creepy" in the plan) is absent. Purple prose is mostly avoided, though there are occasional lapses ("Crystal glasses from some corporate dinner," "badge lifted like it still means something"). The piece is generally showing rather than telling, with a few exceptions ("I taste metal" is a bit stock). Overall: a solid, readable chapter that executes the core tension competently but misses some of the more distinctive elements from the plan and occasionally reaches for poetry that doesn't quite land. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 12 Weak Dialogue: 12 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 10 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 12 Characters Consistent with Profile: 14 Followed Chapter Plan: 9 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# CHAPTER 3 – 10:05 pm “Garage Chemistry” Zip-ties taste like copper when you chew them—plastic coating flakes off, gritty between molars. Vargas shoved me down the service stairs, through laundry, into garage. He killed the lights but the garage-door windows spill moon-stripes over concrete. My wrists burn, tie tighter than handcuffs ever were. He loops the slack around a support post, knots again, leaves me kneeling like a broken lawn ornament. “Think about the combination,” he says, voice calm-dad. “We’ll start with the kids if you stall.” He says it like reminding me to buy milk. Then he’s gone, door clicking shut. Engine of the house hums back to life—power restored, cameras rebooting. I hear him upstairs, muffled: “Keep them quiet, Dixon.” Quiet means alive. I cling to that. I spit plastic grit. My shoulders throb, arms dead behind post. Inventory: fingers still work, thumb half-numb. I need friction, not blade—blade’s upstairs in Dixon’s neck if I’m lucky. Garage smells of gasoline, fertilizer bags Dad Rojas never got around to spreading, and pool shock—calcium hypochlorite in ripped paper sack. Vinegar from my sports bottle dripped down my leg when they marched me; jeans damp, sharp smell rising. Chemistry 101: acid plus hypochlorite equals chlorine gas. I’m in a basement box with two ingredients and no mask. Perfect. First: freedom. I scoot butt forward, arch hips, try to thread tied wrists beneath boots. Hamstrings protest, scar tissue on thigh stretches like hot wire. Almost. I roll onto side, curl, knees to forehead—yoga class I dropped because I couldn’t afford mat rental. Hands pass under feet, scrape concrete. Pain flashes white but they’re in front now, still bound, but movable. I shuffle-worm to workbench, scan. Cordless nail-gun—Ryobi, yellow plastic faded. Magazine loaded—1-1/4 inch brads, maybe twelve left. Battery indicator: one bar. I bump the nose against post, safety presses, nothing—needs trigger pull AND nose depression. My thumb can’t reach. I wedge the gun between post and floor, nose up, slip tie over trigger guard. Angle has to be perfect or I nail my own femur. I press downward with bound wrists. Click—thunk. A brad spits, ricochets. Second try: tie catches trigger again. Click—brad punches through plastic, grazes skin, stops shy of artery. Zip-tie pops. Blood rushes back into palms like broken dam, pins-and-needles fireworks. I don’t wait for celebration. I load the nail-gun under my arm, snatch half-full vinegar bottle, scoop maybe 200 grams of pool shock into empty Gatorade container using kid’s beach shovel. Mix, cap loosely—pressure needs time. I tear a strip from fertilizer bag, stuff as wick. Chlorine starts hissing immediately, caustic apple bite in throat. I cough, eyes stream. Need to move fast or I gas myself first. Door upstairs opens. Footsteps. I kill garage lights, crouch behind ride-on mower. Larson—tech guy—descends, phone flashlight sweeping. He’s humming, some 8-bit tune. He heads to electrical panel, muttering about “loop the feed, kill the cloud backup.” His hoodie smells of vape juice, cherry chemical. He passes within two feet of me, thumb dancing tablet screen. I rise behind him, nail-gun to his calf, nose pressed deep into denim. Pull—thunk-thunk-thunk. Three brads vanish into muscle. He howls, collapses, tablet skittering. I swing gun barrel across his temple—plastic cracks, he goes loose. Not dead, breathing wet. Good. I need him quiet. Fumes build—my eyes burn, lungs scrape. I drag him by collar into laundry nook, shut door, towel across gap. Wipe tears on sleeve, taste chlorine like I’ve bitten a battery. I grab a shop rag, douse in vinegar, tie over mouth—makeshift filter, better than nothing. Inventory again: nail-gun (maybe six brads left), chloramine bomb (swelling, Gatorade sides bowing), keys hanging on pegboard labeled “boat” (no boat visible), zip-tie gun, and a box of sparklers left from July. Sparklers burn 1 600 Celsius—great for thermite if I had aluminum powder. I don’t. I pocket four anyway; fuses maybe. Upstairs: Dixon’s voice, irritated. “Larson, what’s the hold—kill that alarm feed.” No answer. He will come looking. I need height, choke point. Stairwell is narrow, carpeted, turns back on itself. Perfect funnel. I climb silently, press back against wall at hinge point. I can hear Vargas questioning Lily—soft, coaxing. My jaw ticks. I set the Gatorade bomb on third step, wick strip dangling over lip. I balance a kids’ wooden puzzle box atop it—precarious. When door pushes, box falls, jostles cap, gas releases point-blank. I’ll have seconds. I retreat into hallway shadow, nail-gun ready. My palms slick with sweat, stinging where plastic sliced. I breathe through vinegar rag, count heartbeats—Andre’s old trick to slow panic. One-Mississippi— Door opens. Heavy foot. Dixon descends two steps, stops. “Larson?” He sees the green bottle, frowns. He nudges puzzle box with boot. It teeters, drops. Cap pops—hiss like angry snake. Yellow-green cloud billows. Dixon coughs once, eyes widen. He backpedals, crashes into wall, clawing face. I step out, aim nail-gun at his thigh, squeeze—click. Battery dead. I toss it, useless. Dixon swings blind, fist the size of a catcher’s mitt. I duck, drive shoulder into his gut. We tumble. He’s twice me, coughing but fighting. Elbow catches my cheek—stars. I grab sparkler, strike against stair rail—flare, white blinding. I jab the burning wire into his forearm. He roars, flings me off. I roll, grab fallen tablet, whip edge across his nose—crunch. Blood spatters my rag. He swings, connects with my ribs. Air whooshes out. I collapse against banister. He staggers back into cloud, hacking, eyes streaming. I crawl upstairs, lungs burning. Behind me, Vargas shouts, “Dixon, report!” I don’t answer. I’m leaking air and time. Marco peeks from master bedroom, eyes huge. I wave him inside, shut door, drag dresser across. My side feels like broken pottery. I taste iron, chlorine, and somewhere under it, the vinegar of my own fear. The kids stare, waiting for superhero. All they get is me—bleeding, wheezing, purple hair matted to scalp. I manage a grin that probably looks like a snarl. “Still breathing?” I whisper. They nod. “Good. Game’s not over.” I fish sparkler stub from pocket, ember dead but magnesium warm. Options spin—chemistry, chaos, cost. My hands shake so hard the sparkler taps floor like a drummer counting next song. Outside, Vargas’s voice cuts through chemical haze: “You wanna play chemist? Fine. I turn the house into an oven. Last chance, babysitter.” I close my eyes. Andre’s closet smell—dust, cedar, our shared fear—floods back. I open them. Lily offers me her smooth pebble, crayon-labeled “brave.” I pocket it, stand. Ribs protest. I taste blood, smile real this time. “Coming,” I croak toward the door. “Keep your badge on.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 of "Garage Chemistry" is a tightly constructed action sequence that largely delivers on its plan and demonstrates genuine craft in several areas, though it has notable weaknesses worth examining critically. STRENGTHS: The chapter opens with an immediately grounding sensory detail—"Zip-ties taste like copper when you chew them"—that establishes Jay's tactile, survival-mode consciousness without feeling forced. This is the kind of specific, earned detail that distinguishes competent pulp from generic action writing. The escape sequence (threading bound wrists under boots, using the nail-gun against the post) is mechanically plausible and rendered with enough procedural specificity to feel real rather than convenient. The chemistry improvisation is handled well. The chloramine bomb construction is grounded in actual chemistry (acid + hypochlorite = chlorine gas), and Jay's self-awareness that she might gas herself first adds genuine tension. The plan has costs—her eyes stream, her lungs burn—which prevents the sequence from feeling like a Rube Goldberg fantasy. The writing plan explicitly warned against "clever Rube-Goldberg kills," and the chapter mostly honors this: the nail-gun battery dies at the critical moment, the sparkler improvisation is desperate rather than elegant, and Dixon doesn't go down cleanly. Jay's voice is consistent with her profile: the EOD background informs her chemical thinking, the guilt about Andre surfaces naturally ("Andre's old trick to slow panic"), and the class/race dynamics are embedded rather than lectured. The line "All they get is me—bleeding, wheezing, purple hair matted to scalp" is economical and effective. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Larson is disabled (nails in calf, blow to temple), the chloramine bomb deploys, the nail-gun jams/dies, Dixon is partially incapacitated, and the chapter ends with Jay in the bedroom with the kids, Vargas threatening from outside. The watch-shattering (time unspooling) is referenced implicitly through the chapter's lack of timestamp, consistent with the plan. WEAKNESSES: The metaphors are mostly functional but occasionally strain. "Pain flashes white but they're in front now" is fine. "Blood rushes back into palms like broken dam, pins-and-needles fireworks" mixes metaphors awkwardly—a broken dam and fireworks don't cohere. "My side feels like broken pottery" is generic. These aren't catastrophic failures, but they represent moments where the writing reaches for poetry and lands on cliché. The dialogue is sparse, which suits the chapter, but what exists is functional rather than distinctive. Vargas's "You wanna play chemist? Fine. I turn the house into an oven" is serviceable villain-speak but not particularly sharp. Jay's closing "Coming. Keep your badge on" is trying to be a cool line and almost works, but "keep your badge on" is a bit opaque as a taunt. The Larson takedown feels slightly too easy—he walks within two feet of her hiding spot, she nails his calf and clubs him, and he's neutralized. The plan called for Larson chasing her and the thermos popping to blind him, but instead he's dispatched in the garage before the bomb even deploys. This is a deviation from the chapter plan, though not a damaging one narratively. The Dixon fight is appropriately messy and costly (she takes an elbow to the cheek, a rib shot), but the sparkler-as-weapon feels slightly convenient given she just pocketed them. The sequence where she whips a tablet across his nose is viscerally satisfying. The Lily pebble moment at the end ("brave") is emotionally effective and consistent with the character profile. It earns its sentiment because it's brief and the chapter has made Jay bleed for it. The chapter is coherent and maintains internal logic throughout. The pacing is strong—it doesn't linger or over-explain. The class/race dynamics are present but not foregrounded in this chapter (Vargas's slur is planned for Chapter 4), which is appropriate. OVERALL: A competent, often impressive pulp chapter that delivers on its core promises. The craft is real, the voice is consistent, and the action has genuine cost. The weaknesses are real but not severe—some mixed metaphors, one slightly too-easy takedown, functional rather than exceptional dialogue. This is solid genre work with genuine literary ambition that mostly lands. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Model Output:
# CHAPTER 4 – 10:?? pm “Pink Pool Light” Chlorine haze drifts upstairs like mustard fog. My eyes stream, skin stings; every breath tastes like I’m swallowing pennies dipped in bleach. I wedge a towel across the bedroom threshold, buy seconds. Lily coughs into Marco’s pajama sleeve. I kneel, pull their heads to my chest, form a pocket of cleaner air. It’s theater, but theater matters when you’re eight and six. Footfalls pound the hall. Vargas barks orders, voice calm-sheriff, but an edge wobbles—he’s lost a soldier. Dixon hacks below, sounding like a garbage disposal with silverware inside. Good. Maybe cracked ribs punctured lung. Maybe he drowns in his own spit. I’m not counting bodies; I’m counting minutes until the kids are collateral again. “Marco, tablet,” I rasp. Screen’s cracked but alive. I swipe to security app—cameras rebooted. Garage cam shows Larson sprawled, face fish-belly, chest rising. Pool cam shows Dixon on hands and knees, vomiting neon onto stone. One enemy mobile upstairs—Vargas. Maybe two; I never saw the fourth guy clearly. I kill the app, open timer, set ninety seconds. “When it buzzes, you move,” I tell Marco. “Down the back stairs, through pantry, into garage. Hide inside that red kayak. Do not come out unless you hear me say ‘chocolate milk.’ Got it?” He repeats, “Chocolate milk,” eyes wide, absorbing code. I kiss Lily’s forehead—sweat-salt and kiddo shampoo. “Brave rock’s in my pocket,” I whisper. She nods, lip trembling. I stand. Ribs stab; something grinds, not quite broken. The Glock I lifted off Vargas earlier sits on dresser—his backup piece, tiny .380 with pink polymer grip. Six rounds. I tuck it rear waist, ignore the irony of stealing a cop’s cute throw-down. Nail-gun’s dead, but I still have sparkler stubs, one full M-80 from New-Year drawer, and the pool. Water conducts, hides, drowns. Plan: draw Vargas rear of house, separate him from kids, drop him quiet, double back. I move dresser inch by inch, crack door. Hall empty, lights off. Smart-house sensors flick scones alive as I pass—damn things announcing me. I whisper “override—sleep mode,” forgotten command from owner manual. Lights die. Thank you, geek past life. I pad toward rear balcony overlooking pool. Plate-glass slider reflects me: purple locs frizzed, cheek swelling, sports bra gray with sweat and someone else blood. I look like the monster parents warn about. Good. Below, Dixon stumbles outside, wiping mouth. He carries a long pole—pool skimmer. Weapon of opportunity. He limps to filter panel, flips breaker. Pump roars, waterfall feature gushes. He’s clearing chlorine mist, giving himself space to breathe. Smart. I need him dumber. I slide door, humid slap hits. He spins, eyes red slits. “Bitch—” cough “—you gassed me.” Raises skimmer like spear. I vault railing, drop eight feet onto patio table. Impact shocks through ribs; I collapse, gasp. Dixon swings pole, aluminum whistles. I roll; tabletop shatters. Splinters bite thigh. I scramble toward pool edge. He follows, massive, dripping snot and rage. I feint left, spin, grab pool vacuum hose—thick black coil. He lunges; I whip hose, crack across knees. He stumbles, crashes into water. Surface blooms around him, chlorine pink under LED lights. He thrashes—can’t swim, remember? I jump, land on his back, force him under. He bucks, elbow meets my cracked rib—white flash. I hold, knees between shoulder blades, hand forcing head. Bubbles burp. One-Mississippi, two— Gunshot cracks. Tile explodes near my knee. Vargas appears balcony above, pistol raised. “Let him up. Now.” I shove off, roll to side. Dixon surfaces hacking, spits pink. I crawl toward filter shed, reach inside breaker box. Live 220 hums. I yank plastic conduit, rip two wires free, spark pop. I toss ends into pool—water fizzes. Dixon’s eyes widen. He tries to climb out; I kick ladder away. He clings to edge, muscles quivering. “Electricity’s off, big man,” I lie through teeth. He doesn’t know, can’t see sparks in daylight bulb glow. Fear does the rest. He freezes, half floating, afraid to move. Vargas descends stairs slow, gun steady. “Enough stunts. Code to safe. Or I shoot the girl first—make you watch.” I believe him. Badge doesn’t erase sociopath. I raise hands. “Safe’s biometric—needs both parents. I can’t open it.” “Then you’re useless.” Finger tightens. Timer in pocket buzzes—ninety seconds. Marco should be moving kids now. I need Vargas facing front, not stairwell. I step between barrel and house. “You want useful? Let me shut the pump. Chlorine cloud hits neighborhood, someone calls hazmat. You’ll be on every channel by dawn.” His jaw works. He needs quiet, in-and-out narrative. “Thirty seconds,” he says. I move to filter console, hands visible. I flip breakers—pump dies, waterfall stops. Night goes still except cicadas and Dixon’s ragged breathing. I unscrew pressure valve—water jets, arcs across patio, soaks Vargas shoes. He curses, steps back, gun dips. That inch is enough. I draw the pink .380 left-handed, no warning. Pop-pop—two quick, center mass. First round punches sternum, second catches shoulder as he twists. He staggers, fires wild—round whines off stone. I dive behind grill, land hard, ribs screaming. He collapses sitting, back against pergola post, blood bubbling through shirt. Glock clatters. I kick it away, keep sights on him. Dixon still clings to pool edge, afraid of phantom current. Vargas presses wound, coughs pink foam. “You’re done,” he wheezes. “Almost,” I say. I snatch sparkler, strike patio stone—flare hisses. I toss it into pool skimmer basket soaked in chlorine tabs. White spark meets chemical—small whoomph, basket erupts, plastic melts, fire licks across surface. Not lethal, just chaos, smoke, heat. Dixon yelps, shoves off wall, swims through flames to far ladder. He climbs, soaked, sizzling, terror real. I back toward house. Vargas tries to lift gun hand—shaking, useless. “Safe… bonds… you don’t know what you—” cough. “Know enough,” I answer. “You picked the wrong paycheck.” I leave him leaking, lungs whistling. Dixon rolls on patio, slapping embers off shirt. I don’t finish them. I need them alive enough to keep cops curious, keep spotlight off me. I jog inside, dripping pool water, gun hot, heart hotter. Upstairs hallway: empty. Smart-house sensors flick as I pass, painting me ghost-white every three steps. I whisper “garage,” push through pantry door, descend narrow back stairs. Chlorine smell fades, replaced by gasoline and cold concrete. In corner: red kayak overturned. I flip it—empty. My stomach free-falls. Then soft giggle behind workbench. Marco peeks, finger to lips. Lily crawls out, unicorn night-light clutched—its glow now off, she killed it herself. Smart kid. I kneel, hug them both, feel bones vibrate. “Chocolate milk,” I whisper. They exhale. Overhead: distant sirens, finally. Some neighbor smelled cordite or chlorine or both. Cavalry coming—maybe to save us, maybe to shoot me. Doesn’t matter. We’re breathing. We’re not done. I look at my hands—pruned, bleeding, glittered with magnesium ash. I smell like every bad decision I ever made. I smile anyway. “Round one to the babysitter,” I mutter. “Round two starts now.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter delivers a kinetic, sweat-soaked action sequence that largely succeeds on its own pulpy terms. Let me break down what works and what doesn't. STRENGTHS: The voice is consistent and well-maintained. Jay's internal monologue has the right texture—clipped, tactical, occasionally dark-humorous ("pink polymer grip... the irony of stealing a cop's cute throw-down"). The prose breathes in the right places and tightens during action. The sensory detail is generally earned rather than decorative: "sweat-salt and kiddo shampoo," "chlorine pink under LED lights," the 220-volt bluff. These feel like a character who processes the world through her body and her training. The chapter plan is followed reasonably well. Dixon goes into the pool, the pink pool light appears, Vargas fires into the pool area, Jay vomits is notably absent (the plan called for her puking behind the barbecue), and the racial slur from Vargas is present but handled with restraint. The inner flash to her brother's casket and the military flag—a key emotional beat from the plan—is entirely missing, which is a significant omission. That was supposed to be the chapter's emotional core, the thing that makes Vargas's racial slur land with weight. Without it, the chapter is all mechanics and no soul. The kids' characterization is solid. Marco's competence (moving to the kayak, the fake-out with the workbench) and Lily killing her own night-light are good character moments consistent with their profiles. The "chocolate milk" code word is a nice callback. WEAKNESSES: The missing emotional beat (brother's casket, military flag) is the chapter's biggest failure. The plan explicitly called for this as the pivot point when Vargas's racial slur slips—it's what separates this from generic action and gives Jay's violence moral and psychological weight. Instead, Vargas's slur is barely registered, and Jay just keeps moving. The chapter becomes competent action choreography without the grief underneath. Some of the action choreography strains credibility. Jay jumps off an eight-foot balcony onto a patio table, collapses, then immediately engages Dixon in hand-to-hand combat. The physics are pulp-acceptable, but the sequence of events—ripping live wires from a breaker box and tossing them into a pool, then lying about it being "off"—is muddled. If she ripped live wires and tossed them in, Dixon would be dead. The bluff only works if she didn't actually electrify the pool, but the text says she did ("water fizzes"). This is a logical inconsistency that breaks immersion. The ending line—"Round one to the babysitter. Round two starts now."—is exactly the kind of trailer-moment one-liner the writing plan explicitly said to avoid. It's the weakest moment in the chapter, a Reddit-friendly quip that undercuts the exhausted, morally compromised tone the rest of the piece works to establish. The dialogue is functional but thin. Vargas's threats are generic villain-speak ("Or I shoot the girl first—make you watch"). The plan called for him to quote department policy while being monstrous; instead he's just a stock heavy. His characterization as a complex figure—the daughter, the insulin money, the ego that can't accept being outthought—is entirely absent here. Some metaphors land ("voice calm-sheriff, but an edge wobbles"), some don't quite ("Dixon hacks below, sounding like a garbage disposal with silverware inside"—this is vivid but slightly too cute). The "theater, but theater matters" line is a bit self-conscious. The chapter is coherent and the plot moves forward effectively. The action is creative in places (the pool vacuum hose, the sparkler into the chlorine basket). But the emotional depth promised by the plan is largely absent, making this a well-executed but somewhat hollow action sequence. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 10 Emotionally Engaging: 10 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 12 Weak Dialogue: 13 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 10 Characters Consistent with Profile: 10 Followed Chapter Plan: 10 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# CHAPTER 5 – 11:30 pm “Wine Moms & Bearer Bonds” The sirens swell, then cut—someone waved them off or they hit the gate. I don’t wait to find out. I herd the kids across oil-stained concrete, past the kayak tomb they outgrew in ten minutes. My ribs bark every inhale; chlorine burns the back of my throat like I’ve been gargling bleach and regret. “Where now?” Marco whispers, tablet gone, eyes huge without its glow. “Safe room that isn’t safe,” I say. Panel in the wine cellar—parents’ secret, my unknown. If bonds buy silence, I need them before Vargas bleeds out upstairs and the city takes over. Lily clutches my shirt tail. “Bad mans still here?” “Some. We’re shorter.” I try a smile; it feels counterfeit. I crack the stairwell door. Hall empty, lights dimmed to “away” mode—automatic when owners vanish. Good; sensors won’t broadcast us. I scoot them into the pantry, pull the dumb-waiter hatch. Cavity is two-foot square, shelves removed earlier by Dad to smuggle cases of Barolo. Tight fit, but kids are bendable. “Elevator ride,” I whisper. “No talking, no unicorn light.” Marco helps Lily inside, folds himself after. I lower the rope; pulley creaks. They descend into darkness toward kitchen scullery, one floor down. I’ll meet them via stairs once I’ve swept. Separation twists my gut, but three footprints are louder than one. I close hatch, breathe once, then jog toward front entry. Need USB, need leverage, need to know why these cops-turn-robbers want this house more than life. Bootprints marble my memory—Vargas leaking on patio, Dixon rolling in sparkler fire. I pass half-bath: mirror shattered, towel noose abandoned. My face stares back—wild purple frizz, cheek eggplant-purple, lower lip split and shining. I look like a comic-panel avenger drawn by a drunk intern. I look alive. Upstairs balcony door stands open; night air carries pool chlorine and something metallic—blood, maybe mine. I step outside. Vargas slumps where I left him, polo soaked crimson, breath wet whistle. His eyes track me, glassy but sharp. “Help…” he rasps, “lung… collapsing.” I crouch, keep his gun out of reach. “Combination to wine safe?” He coughs pink spray. “Biometric… parents only.” “USB—what’s on it?” “Ledgers… routes… you sell it, you vanish.” He wheezes laugh. “You… need me alive to broker.” I almost pistol-whip him for thinking I’m that brand of stupid. Instead I pat his pockets—car keys, slim wallet, second magazine. No phone. I toss keys over balcony into koi pond—plop, gone. “You’re alive,” I say. “Make it count.” I leave him praying to a god he probably extorted. Inside, I take main stairs two at a time, hug railing to avoid creak center. Master bedroom smells of Mrs. Rojas’ vanilla diffuser and panic-sweat. King bed pristine—no one slept here tonight. I pull the shoe-safe in closet—empty except Rolex boxes. I smash one—cheap decoy. Real safe behind mirror, I remember. I shove dresser aside, reveal steel plate flush to wall, no handle, just scanner slot. LED red. I press my thumb—red stays. Not my print, not my money. I consider the .380—six rounds won’t drill hardened steel. I consider C4 I don’t have. I consider parents, somewhere toasting charity with cartel cash. A groan drifts from hallway—Dixon, maybe. I backtrack, follow sound to guest bath. Door ajar, light on. Dixon hunches over toilet, vomiting pool water. His arm bears a blackened burn swirl where sparkler bit. He sees me in mirror, eyes red slits. “You… cooked me,” he spits. “Stay down, live longer.” I raise gun. He lunges anyway, shoulder low. I sidestep, butt-stroke his temple—he crashes into tub, porcelain cracks. Water sprays from faucet he snapped. He doesn’t rise. I zip-tie his ankles with a belt, take his pocket knife, spare mag that doesn’t fit my toy gun. I leave faucet running—noise cover. I descend toward kitchen, heart hammering ribs like loose nail. Timer in head says five minutes since sirens died—SWAT will stage, detectives will wake. I need evidence, hostage, something. Wine cellar door sits under back stairs, mahogany, temperature controlled. I crack it—cool air rolls out, smells of oak and guilt. I descend spiral into low red light, rows of bottles like artillery. At far wall: brushed-steel panel, LED green now—someone opened recently. I approach, listen. Inside: faint beep, digital. I press ear—nothing. I thumb the recessed latch. Panel sighs open, reveals narrow room, LED strips illuminating a freestanding safe taller than me. Biometric pad blinks. Around its feet: open briefcase stuffed with bearer bonds—cream paper, ornate scroll, denominations $100k each. I flip one band: unsigned, negotiable as cash in any offshore haven. Another item: thumb-drive, black, unbranded. USB—Jay jackpot. I pocket it. Bonds won’t fit pockets; I stuff a single band into sports bra—maybe fifty k—enough for vanish juice if I live. I photograph safe interior with cracked tablet—timestamp proof. Then I hear the rack of a slide behind me. “Hands up, turn slow.” Voice calm, younger. I obey. Rookie—Lyle, the one Vargas shot upstairs—stands swaying, left shoulder bandaged with torn polo, Glock trembling. Blood soaked down to belt. He’s pale, lips gray, but resolve holds. “I’m not the enemy,” I say soft. “You… shot Vargas.” “No, you did—he shot you first, remember?” I step sideways, keep bonds between us. His brow furrows, memory slippery through blood loss. “I… I called ambulance. They said stay put.” “Good advice. Put gun down before you faint.” He wavers. I see the moment decision tilts—he lowers muzzle an inch. I lunge, grab briefcase, swing hard. Corner catches his wounded shoulder—he screams, gun clatters. I kick it under shelving. He crumples, crying. “I just wanted insulin money… my kid sister…” I kneel, press towel to his wound. “Hold that. Stay conscious.” He grips my wrist, surprisingly strong. “USB… they’ll kill you for it.” “They already tried.” Sirens again—closer. Radio squawks upstairs: “LAPD, come out with hands up.” I leave Lyle breathing, pocket USB, snatch one more bond band, then kill cellar lights. I jog up spiral, heart sprinting. At pantry I tap dumb-waiter rope three times—pre-arranged. Pulley creaks; small bodies slide up. I haul them out, both shaky but unharmed. “Field trip’s over,” I whisper. “We surrender pretty.” Marco frowns. “But we win?” “We’re breathing. That’s the W.” Front door battering ram booms. Wood splinters. I raise hands, grip Lily’s fingers tight, place the .380 on floor, kick it toward hallway. Red dots of rifles skate across walls like laser mosquitoes. I shout, voice raw, “Two kids, one adult—coming out!” We step into light—hands high, eyes blinking chlorine, blood, and glitter. Bonds scratch inside my bra, USB burns against thigh. Evidence or death warrant—depends who’s counting. As boots thunder toward us, I catch my reflection in fractured mirror: purple hair singed, cheek swelling, eyes ancient. I look like every survivor who ever crawled out of a rich man’s war. I look free. For now.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 of this pulp revenge tale is a competent, energetic piece of genre fiction that largely delivers on its plan while showing some notable strengths and weaknesses. STRENGTHS: The chapter maintains Jay's voice reasonably well—the clipped, tactical interiority feels authentic to an EOD veteran. Lines like "I look like a comic-panel avenger drawn by a drunk intern" land with the right self-deprecating humor, and "rows of bottles like artillery" is a metaphor that earns its place given Jay's background. The pacing is genuinely propulsive; the chapter moves through multiple locations without losing spatial coherence, which is difficult to execute. The Lyle scene is the chapter's most interesting moment—a wounded rookie who wanted "insulin money" for his kid sister mirrors Jay's own desperation and complicates the moral landscape without being heavy-handed. This is the kind of nuance the plan called for and the execution delivers it. Marco's line "But we win?" / "We're breathing. That's the W" is a good exchange—economical, character-consistent, and emotionally resonant without being sentimental. WEAKNESSES: The chapter deviates from its plan in significant ways. The plan called for: Vargas cracking the safe and bragging about cartel money, Jay seeing the USB as a ticket out, a bargain failing, Vargas shooting Lyle (panic, racial spite), blood splatter on Jay's sneakers, a wine-bottle bomb (vinegar and baking soda), and Jay sprinting upstairs with a sliced calf. Instead, the chapter restructures considerably—Lyle is already wounded when Jay finds him, the wine-bottle bomb doesn't happen, and the racial dynamics the plan emphasized are largely absent. The plan's note about "racial spite" and the class/race politics being felt without lecture is almost entirely missing here. The metaphors are inconsistent. "Ribs bark every inhale" is fine. "Chlorine burns the back of my throat like I've been gargling bleach and regret" is the kind of forced, reaching simile the writing plan explicitly warned against—"regret" is doing no work there beyond signaling that this is Literary Writing. "Blood, maybe mine" is good. "Eyes ancient" at the end is a cliché. "Evidence or death warrant—depends who's counting" is a bit too neat, too trailer-ready for what the plan wanted. The dialogue is functional but occasionally flat. Vargas's "Help… lung… collapsing" / "Biometric… parents only" exchange is efficient but doesn't reveal character. Dixon's "You… cooked me" is weak—it's the kind of line that sounds tough without actually being tough. The chapter tells us Jay "looks free" in the final beat, which is a tell-don't-show problem—the image of her in the mirror with "eyes ancient" is already doing that work, and the explicit statement undercuts it. The Lyle character, while interesting, appears somewhat out of nowhere in terms of his survival—the plan had Vargas shooting him, but here he's bandaged and mobile, which creates a minor coherence issue with prior chapters. The ending with hands raised and laser dots is competent but predictable—it's exactly the image you'd expect, and the plan's ambition for something messier and less resolved isn't quite achieved. OVERALL: A solid, readable chapter that maintains momentum and voice, but falls short of the plan's more ambitious racial/class politics, omits the wine-bottle bomb entirely, and occasionally reaches for poetry that doesn't land. The Lyle scene elevates it above average pulp. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 8 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 13 Followed Chapter Plan: 9 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# CHAPTER 6 – 12:04 am “Lego Caldera” Boots fill the foyer like falling bricks, rifles up, voices overlapping: “Hands—kids—clear left—weapon there—” Red dots skate across my sternum. I keep my arms high, fingers spread so wide they cramp. Lily presses her face into my thigh; Marco holds her hand like he’s the last plug in a dam. “Down on knees—slow!” a helmeted cop barks. I obey, ribs screaming. Carpet fibers bite bare knees. A female officer sweeps Lily up; she shrieks, legs bicycling air. Another scoops Marco. They disappear behind ballistic shields, slippers flopping. I want to call something comforting, but words clog behind my teeth. Cop behind me zip-ties my wrists again—plastic teeth click, tighter than Vargas, tighter than doubt. He pats me down, finds the bond wad inside my bra, pulls it like a magician’s scarf. Cream bearer papers flutter to floor, numbers bleeding under boot prints. “Well, well,” he mutters, “Christmas bonus.” I spit dust. “Evidence—don’t mix.” He ignores me, reads rights like grocery list. My ears ring; the Miranda feels optional tonight. Detective Ramirez pushes through, ponytail graying, eyes sharp. She signals cuffs off. “She’s the sitter,” she tells uniforms. “We need her walking.” She helps me stand, grip surprisingly gentle. “You talk, you stay free—deal?” I nod, throat chlorine-raw. “Kids safe?” “Paramedics assess. You—hospital or statement?” “Statement while I can still stand.” I glance at stairs. No Dixon, no Vargas. “Two suspects down—wine cellar, pool patio. One maybe dead.” She lifts brow. “You confess?” “Self-defense inventory. Check their weapons, badges—fake.” She signals two officers, points directions. They move, rifles still high. We step into formal living room—white couches, baby-grand no one plays. Crime-scene LEDs already bloom. I sit, hands still bound in front like praying. Ramirez offers water; I swig, rinse metallic spit. She starts recorder. “Name?” “Jay Munroe.” “Address?” “None you’ll find.” I meet her stare. “Check my discharge papers—Air-Force EOD. Then check USB in my pocket—leads to bigger rats than me.” She slips on gloves, extracts thumb-drive. “Parents?” “Laundering. Safe full of bearer bonds. Rojas couple set tonight up—insurance scam meets cartel cleanup.” I lay it out quick, facts like cards: home invasion crew, fake warrant, my chemistry set, kids as leverage. I leave out only the fifty grand I stuffed in my sock—tuition dream dies hard. Uniform returns, pale. “Detective—wine cellar suspect alive, needs bus. Patio guy—collapsed lung, critical. Third, minor GSW, stable.” Ramirez exhales through teeth. “Weapons?” “Glock, unregistered. Serials filed. Badges—props.” She looks at me, calculating. “You held four armed men with vinegar and pool shock?” “And a nail-gun with one bar battery,” I add. “Don’t buy Ryobi.” A laugh hiccups out her before she can stop it. She rubs eyes. “Christ, Jay. Media will eat you alive.” I shrug. “Better than feeding them my corpse.” Paramedics wheel past hallway—Lyle on gurney, oxygen mask fogging. He lifts hand, tries to point at me. I nod once: debt acknowledged. Ramirez follows my gaze. “He’ll flip—cartel, routes, parent conspiracy. DA will want you pristine. Stay reachable.” “Got a duplex in Van Nuys—mold and good intentions.” She smiles thin. “Go to hospital. I’ll send escort.” She signals officer to cut ties. Plastic snaps free; blood floods palms like fizzy soda, painful and bright. Uniform drives me to ambulance. I refuse stretcher, sit on tailgate. Med tech cleans facial cuts, tapes rib wrap. I wince, count flashes—each one a receipt I’m still here. Across yard news van arrives, satellite dish blooming like poison mushroom. I turn away, but camera swivels. I feel red light hunt my skin. In fifteen minutes I’ll be “unknown female suspect” on loop. I pull hoodie up—salty, torn, but camouflage. Paramedic asks, “Pain one to ten?” “Eight when I breathe, three when I don’t.” He offers morphine; I wave off—need brain clear. Instead I sip oxygen mask, let cool flood lungs, wash chlorine ghosts. Kids appear under cop escort—small feet in borrowed blankets. Lily breaks free, runs, slams into my legs. Pain stars, but I kneel, hug her tight. She presses wet rock into my palm—same “brave” pebble, now warm. Marco stands back, eyes adult-heavy. “They said we might go to foster.” “Maybe,” I admit. “But you’ll go breathing. That’s win.” He nods, accepts math no kid should calculate. Ramirez approaches. “Lawyers en-route. Don’t sign anything without me.” She hands card, adds quietly, “USB copying. You’re golden if you stay clean.” I tuck card into sock beside cash. “Clean is relative—ask the pool.” She almost smiles, walks off barking orders. Ambulance shifts into gear. I wave kids off—another officer will ride with them to hospital for checks. Lily’s gaze drills me until door shuts. I feel the hole open inside—separation like sudden amputation. I tell myself breathing equals win, but win feels hollow. Cop escort drives me back inside for photos. I stand on blood-spotted marble while techs swab under nails, bag my vinegar rag, confiscate leftover sparklers. They photograph bruises like modern art. I keep face blank, practice looking victim not vigilante. Victim gets sympathy; vigilante gets cells. When they release me, dawn is a suggestion in the east. House smells like pool, gunpowder, and rich-people panic. Bonds dry on foyer table—evidence now, not future. I pocket one corner scrap anyway, microscopic seed of escape. I walk out past koi pond—Dad’s Rolex key fob glints underwater where I tossed it. I leave it. Some prices aren’t worth paying. News drone buzzes overhead. I flip hoodie up, middle finger low, keep moving. Street glows peach. My ribs ache, eyes burn, ears ring one-note hymn. But I’m vertical, kids breathing, USB singing in detective server.
