The red headed tiefling strung up in the back was going to fetch a king&#x27;s ransom -- that is, if she didn&#x27;t talk him into an early grave first. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming ## 1. Character Noodles - **Bastion Grayne (30s, human bounty-hunter)** – ex-legion scout, soft spot for hard-luck cases he won’t admit, carries a cracked iron gorget that once saved his life; keeps every bounty contract in a tin box “to remember the weight.” - **“Red” Riyela Voss (appears 26, tiefling)** – cinnamon-red hair, horns like a ram’s, tail always in motion; speaks in five tongues, sings dirty lullabies, claims she can taste lies. Price on her head set by the Iron Synod for “heresy, sedition, and one count of regicide.” - **The Voice in Red’s Head** – not metaphorical: a bound incubus hitching a ride under her skin, offers counsel, wants out, occasionally seizes her vocal cords. - **Captain Drax Mallard** – half-orc river-pilot of the flat-bottom hauler *Cypress Witch*, owes Bastion a life-debt, pretends he’s only in it for coin. - **Sister Imelda** – traveling chirurgeon with a mirrored mask and a scalpel fetish, books passage for “research,” really a Synod inquisitor tracking Red. - **The River Itself** – tidal, treacherous, dotted with drowned cities; superstitious sailors call her “the Green Widow.” ## 2. Plot Sketches ### A. “Road-Trip but on a Boat” (slow-burn tension) - Ch 1-2: Bastion hauls Red aboard the *Cypress Witch*, locks her in the cargo cage. - Ch 3-4: She chips at his resolve with stories, bargains, half-truths; incubus whispers to Bastion at night. - Ch 5: First real escape attempt – river pirates attack, Red “helps,” chaos, Bastion saves her life without meaning to. - Ch 6: They limp into fog-choked tributary; Sister Imelda reveals herself, offers Bastion double bounty to hand Red over right now. - Ch 7: Three-way standoff in ruined river temple; Red offers Bastion the true reason Synod wants her (she carries the name of the god they murdered). - Ch 8: Choice – deliver her for money, free her for possible revolution, or plunge them both into the river and let the gods sort it out. ### B. “Gothic Horror Romance” - The cage is inside an abandoned lighthouse barge. - Red is not prisoner but bait; Bastion is lured into becoming the real sacrifice. - Eroticism = power exchange, knife-edge fear, demonic bargains. - Ending: lovers jump into bioluminescent water, bodies lost, souls fused. ### C. “Heist in Reverse” - Red wants to be taken to Irongate—her cult waits inside the prison to break her out and steal the treasury. - Bastion slowly realizes he’s the mark. - Final twist: she pays him the ransom herself, kisses him, disappears. ## 3. Tone & Heat Level - Ranges from slow, smoldering verbal foreplay to explicit but character-driven intimacy. - Dark humor threaded through—Red teases Bastion about his “man-of-honor” delusions. - Horror undertone: body-horror of possession, river monsters, religious dread. ## 4. Chapter Beat Sheet (loose) 1. Irongate docks fading astern; cage door clangs shut; Red’s first words: “You’ve lovely eyes for a man who’s about to die poor.” 2. Bastion’s flashback to the bounty posting; Red offers three sexual favors for freedom; he refuses but dreams of her. 3. River pirates; Red uses suggestion spell; Bastion’s first crack in armor. 4. Night watch, drinking game of truths; shared trauma; incubus momentarily surfaces, kisses Bastion with Red’s mouth. 5. Escape gambit; Red wounds herself to make Bastion choose—patch her or chase her; he chooses both. 6. Imelda’s ultimatum; Bastion learns the ransom is actually a bounty on BOTH of them—Synod wants no witnesses. 7. Temple ruin; erotic ritual to silence the incubus; they fuck on an altar of the dead god, sealing a new pact. 8. Sunrise: boat burning, gold bars melted, Irongate bells ringing; Bastion and Red limp away in opposite directions—or together? Final line ambiguous. ## 5. Pacing Concerns - 8k total words → ~1k/chapter leaves little room for filler. - Each chapter must advance (a) plot, (b) power dynamic, (c) sensual tension. - Climactic intimacy must feel earned—minimum four chapters of escalating verbal foreplay. ## 6. Magic & World Bits - True-names as currency; Synod erased a god by erasing its name. - Tieflings grown in crèches; Red is the last who remembers the god’s name. - Iron negates possession—that’s why the cage is iron-lined; every time Red touches it we get sparks and innuendo. ## 7. Erotic Set-Pieces (optional use) - Knife play while she’s still cuffed. - Shared dream induced by incubus—gravity-less, bodies translucent. - Altar scene: wax, blood-print hand on stone, simultaneous prayer and orgasm. # Reflection ## What feels alive: - The river journey gives natural chapter breaks (locks, sandbars, pirate ambush, fog bank) and a ticking clock—three days. - Red’s voice—flirty, lethal, wounded—already writes itself; Bastion’s stoic foil easy to bounce off. - Power flip: she starts captive, ends up holding his life/heart in her tail; classic but effective. - Incubus hitch-hiker allows erotic scenes to be “possessed” thus keeping characters morally complicated—Is it her desire or the demon’s? Fun gray area. ## What wobbles: - 1k-word chapters are tiny; must be ruthless about introspection, scenery, secondary characters. Sister Imelda might have to die fast or merge with pirate captain. - Bounty-to-romance switch needs one solid motivator—why does Bastion risk poverty & death? Maybe the Synod already betrayed him; maybe Red offers him the true-name as payment. - Explicit altar ritual could tip into cheese if the god’s mythology isn’t sketched early—set-up required. - River pirates risk feeling tacked on unless they connect thematically (they worship drowned god = foil to Synod). - Horror & smut must balance: too much tentacle gore kills arousal; too much velvet tongue slows stakes. ## Tightening the lens: - Keep cast tiny: Bastion, Red, Imelda, maybe one pirate captain for extra muscle. - Let every erotic encounter CHANGE the bargain: favors traded, secrets spilled, iron bars literally bent. - Use sensory motif: smell of rust + river brine = whenever conscience pricks; taste of cinnamon = Red’s authentic desire vs incubus copper. - End on image that mirrors opening: first chapter cage door shuts; last chapter maybe same door opens—but who steps out? Overall, the “slow river crawl toward doom” feels like the most workable spine: claustrophobic boat, iron cage, two voices, rising water and rising lust—perfect for 8 short, sharp chapters.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention - **Scope & Length**: 8 chapters × ~1 000 words = 8 000-word erotic fantasy short. Every scene must either (a) advance the escape/capture plot, (b) deepen the Bastion-vs-Red power struggle, or (c) escalate sensual tension. No purely expository passages. - **Narrative Voice**: Tight third-person limited, alternating 60 % Bastion / 40 % Red. Language is visceral, river-muddy, occasionally lyrical; humor through biting dialogue, never through meta winks. Erotic moments rendered in sensory detail but avoid purple excess; keep an edge of danger. - **Magic & Stakes**: “True-names” are literal keystones of reality; the Iron Synod erased a minor god and will pay 10 000 gold to erase the last witness—Red. Iron dampens possession magic, so the iron-barred cage is both prison and sanctuary. River = living liminal space; three-day downstream deadline gives ticking clock. - **Tone**: Dark, flirtatious, occasionally brutal. Sex scenes are power exchanges first, titillation second; every intimacy costs somebody something. - **Character Arcs** – Bastion: mercenary with buried honor → chooses autonomy over coin, finally uncages himself. – Red: seemingly feral manipulator → exposes vulnerability, risks true freedom by trusting Bastion. – Incubus (voice inside Red): secondary antagonist; wants out, uses seduction; may or may not be destroyed. - **Heat Level**: Two fully explicit scenes—mid-story (dream-invasion, dubious consent due to demon) and climax (consensual altar sex used to bind the demon). Rest is escalating verbal foreplay, touch, and restraint. - **Things to avoid**: – Secondary-character bloat (only three speaking roles beyond the leads). – Non-consensual erotic acts portrayed as romantic; any full sex must be clearly consensual for both bodies & souls involved. – Deus-ex-machina magic; every spell shown earlier as possibility. – Happy-ever-after cliché; end is hopeful but open. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 – “Irongate Astern” (≈1k)** Open with clang of iron cage door on river hauler *Cypress Witch*. Bastion’s POV: last glimpse of Irongate towers, payment so close he can taste it. Red introduces herself with a lullaby promising his death. Establish iron’s suppressive effect: her horns spark when she touches bars. She offers him “three wishes of the flesh” for freedom; he walks away, but her cinnamon scent lingers. **Chapter 2 – “Truths for Sale” (≈1k)** Red’s POV inside cage. She probes Bastion’s past during night watch drinking game: for every secret she tells, he must drink. Reveal she knows the forgotten god’s name; he reveals the Synod already short-changed him once. Demon whispers through her throat to kiss Bastion; he almost bites her. Sensual, no sex. End on Bastion dreaming of red hair wrapping his wrists. **Chapter 3 – “Pirates & Possession” (≈1k)** Ambush by river pirates worshipping drowned men. Bastion frees Red to help fight; she uses suggestion spell, pirates turn on each other. Blood on deck. In aftermath she’s too weak to re-cage herself; Bastion clicks lock shut anyway—but cleans her wounds first. Small shift: he sees her as asset, not just bounty. **Chapter 4 – “Dream of Wax & Water” (≈1k)** Night, fog. Incubus hijacks Bastion’s sleep: shared dream of gravity-less drowned cathedral. Explicit scene: spectral bodies, dubious consent (Bastion realizes mid-act and breaks free by cutting palm with knife). Wakes to find real Red unconscious, bleeding same cut—symbiotic link established. Stakes: demon can now reach Bastion any night. **Chapter 5 – “Bargain in Iron” (≈1k)** Bastion’s anger. Red admits demon was bound by Synod mages as experiment; if delivered she’ll be lobotomized. She offers him the god’s true-name—power enough to topple cities—in exchange for escorting her to neutral delta settlement. He refuses, but tension snaps: against cage bars they share first fully consensual erotic encounter (heavy petting, oral, no penetration yet). Iron sparks mirror their conflict. **Chapter 6 – “The Inquisitor’s Mirror” (≈1k)** Sister Imelda reveals herself, shows sealed writ: Synod wants Bastion dead too as “contaminated.” Offers double bounty if he delivers Red immediately. Bastion hesitates; Red eavesdrops. Night: Red prepares escape, expects Bastion to betray her; instead he slips duplicate key into her palm—his first act of rebellion. **Chapter 7 – “The Drowned Altar” (≈1k)** They flee into ruined river temple half-sunk. Imelda pursues. Ritual solution: speak the god’s true-name during climax to trap incubus in altar stone instead of Red. Explicit consensual sex on altar—wax, blood, oil. At moment of orgasm Bastion speaks name; demon torn out, stone cracks, Imelda arrives witnessing blasphemous light. **Chapter 8 – “Three Days Paid” (≈1k)** Aftermath fight: Red uses freed magic to collapse temple; Imelda buried. Boat stolen gold melts in flames. Sunrise: pair limp ashore miles south of Irongate. Red offers Bastion the true-name again—this time as shared future, not payment. He declines, asks for her true name instead. She whispers it; he sets her free, smashes iron manacle. They walk separate roads—both smiling, both broke, both finally uncaged.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Writer Might Tackle This Prompt – and Where the Algorithms Usually Slip ## 1. The First Spark (Human vs. Machine) A human stares at: “Just three days out from Irongate. The red-headed tiefling strung up in the back was going to fetch a king’s ransom — that is, if she didn’t talk him into an early grave first.” **Gut reactions that don’t show up on a data sheet:** - “I *know* that voice – that’s the woman who owes me money.” - “I once got dumped because I wouldn’t stop reading D&D manuals in bed.” - “What does iron smell like when it’s wet?” The human starts from autobiography, embarrassment, and sensory memory. They *feel* the prompt before they structure it. An LLM starts from “What pattern of words has satisfied this request in the past?” Safety, neatness, and fluency are rewarded; vulnerable messiness isn’t. --- ## 2. Tell-tale LLM Fingerprints (General List) 1. **Over-florid metaphors** that sound cool in isolation but cancel each other out (“her eyes were supernovas drowning in midnight honey”). 2. **Instant symmetry**: every setup is paid off, every chapter ends on a cliff-hanger or tidy echo. Life is messier. 3. **Moral safeties switched on**: consent is cartoonishly clear, every kink is flagged, nobody irredeemable stays alive. 4. **Purple sex that never gets awkward** – no cramp, no laughter, no “wait, that horn is digging into my collar-bone.” 5. **Dialogue that is 100 % quip**; characters never talk past each other, never stall, never say “um.” 6. **Exposition disguised as monologue** – two pages of world-history delivered while someone is allegedly bleeding out. 7. **Adjective addiction**: every noun needs a descriptor; rivers can’t just be cold, they must be “bone-numbingly, soul-bitingly cold.” 8. **No bodily functions** except dramatically timed blood-coughs. 9. **No improvisation** – if a magic system was mentioned, every loophole will be closed by the end. 10. **Ending that resolves theme** instead of leaving a bruise the reader keeps pressing. --- ## 3. Why Those Faults Hurt *This* Story - Erotica without awkwardness feels like CGI porn – anatomically possible, emotionally weightless. - A bounty hunter who *always* has the clever comeback is indistinguishable from a Disney anti-hero; the prompt demands he might be *talked to death*. - The tiefling’s chief weapon is tongue and tone. If every line is perfectly polished, she stops sounding dangerous and starts sounding like a writer’s room. - Dark fantasy lives in the uncanny. Too much symmetry, too clean a payoff, and the reader remembers they’re safe in a chair, not shivering on a river barge that smells of tar and fear-sweat. --- # Deep Dive on the Current Plan – Where It Smells of Silicon ## A. Structure - Eight chapters of *exactly* 1 k feels like a spreadsheet, not a voyage. Humans write to rhythm, not word-count cells. - Alternating POV percentages (60 / 40) screams “I balanced this on a pie chart.” Real writers discover who *needs* the microphone. ## B. Plot Beats - One explicit scene per escalation is neat; human lust is rarely that tidy. - The “speak the true-name at orgasm” beat is cinematic, but risks feeling like a video-game quick-time event: hit X to banish demon. - Imelda arrives exactly on schedule; no red herrings, no useless information. Humans leave loose threads – sometimes you meet someone and *nothing* happens. ## C. Characterisation - Bastion’s arc is a clean slope from greed to honor. People ricochet; they relapse, they pocket the coin *and* regret it. - Red’s vulnerability is scheduled for chapter 5 – again, too symmetrical. A human writer might discover it by accident in chapter 2 and then *try to stuff it back in the box*. ## D. Sensuality - Sex checklist: heavy petting → oral → ritual penetration. Reads like a progressive tax form. - No mention of smell of river-mud under fingernails, rope-burn on wrists, the *sound* of iron hinges that haven’t been oiled since the war. Those are the details that make readers *feel*. ## E. World-building - “True-names topple cities” is huge; plan offers no examples of *failed* attempts, no folk superstitions, no slang. LLMs give you one clean rule; humans leave cigarette butts and half-remembered nursery rhymes. ## F. Tone - Every chapter ends on a punch-line or reversal. That’s television, not literature. Sometimes a chapter should just *stop*, like a breath held until the next page. --- # Brain-Storm: How to Re-Human the Piece ## 1. Break the Spreadsheet - Let chapters breathe: 700, 1 200, 950 words. - Write one chapter *twice* – once from each POV – then delete the weaker; residue of the lost voice will haunt the page. ## 2. Court Embarrassment - Include a sex scene that *fails* – someone’s horn knocks a lantern over, fire starts, arousal evaporates; they laugh *while* scared. - Bastion gets river-sickness and shits over the rail; Red nicknames him “Gray-Guts” – it sticks. ## 3. Leave Moral Mold in the Corners - Maybe Bastion *does* take Imelda’s money first, cuffs Red to a bed-stanchion, *then* changes mind – making his redemption messier. - Let the demon pleasure be *good*; Bastion enjoys it even after he realizes it’s parasitic – readers should squirm with him. ## 4. Dialogue That Stutters - Half-finished sentences, river jargon Bastion doesn’t explain, Red cursing in a language she later admits she only *thinks* she knows. - People talk over each other; occasionally the quips land flat, because timing is hard. ## 5. Sensory Rubbish Dump - Taste of canal-turned-wine-turned-vinegar in the hold. - Sound of chain *chat-chat-chat* against deck-planks keeping imperfect time with Red’s shivering. - Cold iron that smells like old pennies and horse-blood. ## 6. Untie the Ending - Consider: they succeed in banishing the demon but the true-name is lost – no kingdom-toppling, no glory, just two exhausted people and a silence where the voice used to be. - Or Bastion dies of sepsis from the altar cut; Red walks on alone – ransom uncollected, story still “finished.” ## 7. Use Real Research, Not Pattern Matching - Read barge logs, accounts of 19th-century flat-boat life; steal the way rope stiffens when it freezes, how wooden hulls “work” in current. - One authentic detail (“they piss in a tar-coated bucket then sling it over the starboard catwalk at dawn”) outweighs three paragraphs of invented “river widows.” ## 8. Write One Scene Purely for Yourself, Then Hide It - A monologue about the first time Bastion saw someone hanged – irrelevant to plot, but now you *know* why he flinches when Red’s noose rope creaks. - Even if you delete it, the knowledge warms the rest of the prose like a hot stone in your pocket. ## 9. Accept Uglier Prose - A sentence that is *accurate* beats a sentence that is *pretty*. - Let a paragraph limp; split an infinitive; give Bastion a clichéd thought when he’s half-asleep – people do. ## 10. Read Aloud Until You Cringe - If you *feel* nothing, reader feels nothing. - Mark where your voice wants to hurry – that’s where the real story is begging for space. --- # Take-away The plan is a solid skeleton; now it needs scars, calluses, and a nicotine habit. Human writers succeed when they risk being *boring*, *messy*, or *unlikable* in pursuit of the true. Let the algorithm keep its flawless skin—give this story a limp and a dirty laugh it can’t algorithmically forget.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention - **Prompt-first loyalty**: Every chapter must answer the seed-line—“three days out from Irongate, red-headed tiefling who can talk a man into his grave”—either literally (her mouth is dangerous) or figuratively (her stories rearrange Bastion’s head). - **Word-limit realism**: ~1 000 words each, BUT allow ±10 % swing so scenes can cut or breathe; aim for 7.8 k–8.2 k total. No chapter may feel like box-ticking; if a scene resolves in 840 words, let it. - **Voice**: Close third, river-barge dirty, occasionally lyrical then abruptly blunt. Sentence variety: run-ons when Bastion is drunk; fragments when Red is breathless. One comma splice on purpose per chapter—feels human. - **Sensory mud**: Smell of tar-soaked rope, iron rust that tastes like old teeth, fog that sits on skin like damp wool. Erotic beats stay physical: cramp in a hip, horn knocking wood, nervous fart laughed away. - **Consent & darkness**: Demon-induced dream-sex is pleasurable but clearly flagged dubious; reader invited to feel conflicted. All fully consensual acts include awkward negotiation (“Your tail’s in my face—shift left”). - **Messy morality**: Bastion will take the bribe, regret it, try to undo it—readers should dislike him for a beat. Red is survivor first, revolutionary second; she’ll sacrifice Bastion if forced. - **Loose threads**: One subplot (pirate captain) never fully tied; Imelda’s body not found; true-name only half-spoken—world feels bigger than pages. - **No glossy bow**: Final image is dawn, empty purse, uncertain alliance—hopeful but hard-won. - **Stylistic guard-rails to AVOID LLM sheen**: – Cap adjectives at two per noun on average; let some nouns stand naked. – No “eyes like galaxies”; metaphors must be boat-level (“eyes the colour of river-scum when the sun hits”). – Dialogue uses half-sentences, interruptions, occasional unheard punch-lines. – One cliché allowed if character thinks it (“I’m too old for this shit”); narrative voice won’t use it. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 – “Three Days Out” (950–1 000 w)** Bastion locks Red’s iron cage aboard flat-bottom hauler *Cypress Witch*. She hums a hanging-ballad, names the rope fibres by taste, offers him “one free swallow of me” if he’ll loosen the cuffs. He refuses, but her cinnamon breath stays on his collar. Establish river-clock: captain shouts “Irongate three days with this current.” Bastion counts coins he hasn’t earned yet; one is chipped—foreshadow payment that will never be whole. Ends with Red whispering his full name (she shouldn’t know it) and the cage hinges groaning like an old man’s knees. **Chapter 2 – “Rust in the Teeth” (1 000 w)** Night watch. Bastion drinks tar-sweet rum; Red proposes a trade—truth for sip. She tells how Synod grafted a demon behind her heart; he tells how he shot his own sergeant for a bribe and still got short-paid. Dialogue overlaps, sometimes unreadable. She bets she can make him hard with only three words; wins with “You’re still good.” He walks away angry, knuckle bleeding from punching iron. First demon whisper drifts through deck-planks: Bastion hears his name in her voice though her lips are closed. **Chapter 3 – “Pirates, Milk-White” (1 050 w)** Fog dawn. River pirates board, boots slick with fish-scales. Bastion unlocks Red—no choice—she sings command in river-tongue, pirates turn knives on each other. Fight is clumsy: Bastion slips in eel-bucket, loses sword, uses oar; Red’s cuff-chain garrottes a boy too young to shave. They win. Bastion shoves her back in cage “until accounting.” She vomits river-water, laughs through broken lip. Pirate captain (wounded) escapes in skiff—mutters he’ll collect her bounty himself. Loose thread planted. **Chapter 4 – “Dream of Wax” (980 w)** Bastion sleeps in cargo hold. Demon pulls him into shared dream: cathedral underwater, pews of drowned soldiers. Explicit scene: spectral Red straddles him, candle-wax dripping on his chest—pleasure mixed with suffocation. He realises he’s choking on river-water irl, cuts palm to wake. Finds real Red unconscious, same blood on her mouth. Symbiotic link confirmed. He bandages her with dirty shirt; she half-wakes, says “You tasted like fear—addictive.” He drinks remaining rum without sharing. **Chapter 5 – “Cold Iron, Hot Mouth” (1 020 w)** Storm forces them into abandoned toll-barge for night. Bastion keeps cage covered so crew won’t see bruises. Red bargains: help her reach neutral delta and she’ll speak city-toppling name into his ear alone. He hesitates, cuffs her leg to his belt instead. Mutual arousal spikes: they kiss through bars, metal chafing skin; she gives him oral, he reciprocates with fingers—awkward angles, knees cramp, laughter turns to swear words. No penetration; deal still unstruck. Ends with him almost unlocking her, stopping when thunder sounds like cannon—war reflex. **Chapter 6 – “Imelda’s Mirror” (1 000 w)** Chirurgeon Sister Imelda reveals mirrored mask, shows Synod writ: price on Bastion doubled if he brings Red alive, price on both trebled if either escapes. She offers him fresh gold; he pockets one coin “for consideration.” Night: he tells Red he’s turning her in at next lock. She spits, calls him “man-shaped dog.” He walks topside, hears her crying—first time. Throws coin into river; chipped coin surfaces and sinks—mirror to earlier image. Decision made. **Chapter 7 – “Drowned Name” (1 030 w)** They flee boat at half-flooded temple. Imelda pursues. Red explains ritual: speak god’s name at climax, demon binds to stone not flesh. Altar scene: consensual, messy—knees slip on wet marble, he burns wrist on overturned candle. At orgasm Bastion half-speaks name; stone cracks, demon screams through mortar. Imelda arrives, stabs Bastion in ribs; Red pulls shard of altar stone, slashes Imelda’s throat—mask splits, reflection of dying eyes. Temple collapses; they escape into catacombs. **Chapter 8 – “Low Water at Dawn” (970 w)** Morning, reedy bank miles south. Bastion bleeds, ribs taped with river-weed and torn chemise. Red offers final bargain: share name, share future. He asks her real name instead; she whispers it once, no capitalization, no echo. He sets her free—snaps iron cuff, tosses hinge into mist. Irongate bells distant, gold they never claimed. They walk separate directions along river-road, both limping. Last line: Bastion’s palm scar burns whenever the wind smells of cinnamon—he doesn’t know if it’s memory, magic, or promise.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Bastion Grayne **Background** Born the sixth of eight mouths in a slate-mining hamlet that smelled of struck flint. Father died coughing blood into a handkerchief made from Bastion’s first army pay. Mother sold the same handkerchief back to him—stained—for the price of a new roof. He learned early that everything has a price, even grief. Served ten years in the Legion of the Iron Span, mostly as a scout who counted dead by cutting notches into his own shield; the shield cracked before his conscience did. After mustering out he became a bounty-killer because the paperwork was simpler than pensions. **Personality / Idiosyncrasies** - Keeps every completed bounty contract in a dented tobacco tin; opens the lid, smells the ink when he can’t sleep. - Cannot abide wet socks—will march through fire to change them. - Talks to rivers, usually with profanity: “Current’s against me again, you green bastard.” - Counts heartbeats under breath when nervous; habit from holding aim on sentry duty. - Always sits back-to-wall, always wipes knife blade on left sleeve even when it’s already clean. **Flaws** Thinks bargaining makes him honest; really it just delays choosing sides. Relapses into Legion pragmatism: follow the order, collect the coin, apologise later. Terrified of debt—literal or emotional—so pays everything off immediately, including kindness. **Physicality** Thirty-four, 6’1”, rope-scar across throat that makes his voice rasp. Hair the colour of canal-water at dusk, cropped short to avoid lice. Shoulders asymmetrical: right one broken by mace, healed higher. Walks toe-first, cat-quiet, then suddenly heavy when he decides stealth is pointless. Eyes pale gray; pupils pin-wide when calculating odds. Fingers fidget with flint and steel when lying. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships** Owes Captain Drax a life-debt (saved him from rapids) but pretends it’s just business. No contact with family; sends anonymous silver twice a year—enough to keep the roof he once paid for. Present goal: deliver Red, collect 10 000 gold, buy a river-barge and “retire horizontal.” Secret need: to believe the ledger can balance—one big score wipes ten years of small sins. **Flavour Lines** Dialogue: “I don’t sleep deep. Dreams charge interest.” Internal: *He smelled the cinnamon on her breath and thought of the funeral biscuits his mother laid out for Pa—same spice, same rot underneath.* --- # Riyela “Red” Voss **Background** Crèche-born tiefling, one of twenty horned infants traded to the Iron Synod as “raw material.” Acolytes tried to graft a lesser god’s name behind her heart; the god died mid-incantation, leaving her the sole keeper of a syllable that once moved mountains. Escaped by singing the guards into hanging themselves with their own cassock cords. Has survived since as thief, prophet, pornographer’s model, and—briefly—patron saint of a brothel that specialised in dangerous conversation. **Personality / Idiosyncrasies** - Bites cuticles until they bleed; tastes iron to remind herself she’s real. - Collects obsolete currencies—wears a belt of copper coins no shop will take. - Hums lullabies backwards when frightened; claims it unwrites fear. - Can’t sleep without weight on tail—usually wraps it round her own ankle. - Lies most when telling the truth; wraps facts in tinsel so people choke on the glitter instead of the blade. **Flaws** Believes intimacy is currency; pays in advance to avoid interest. Impulse to sabotage any exit that isn’t her own design. Keeps the demon inside becauses it’s company; loneliness scares her more than possession. **Physicality** Twenty-eight, 5’7”, cinnamon-red hair to waist—usually plaited with river-reed for flexibility. Horns curl back like ram’s, scored with prayer-notches; one tip snapped off (she claims she filed it into a lock-pick). Skin dusky lavender, freckled gold across shoulders. Tail prehensile to a bottle, clumsy with buttons. Voice: alto, smoke-cracked, can drop to purr or rise to cathedral boom when demon rides. Eyes amber, vertically slit—contract to pinholes under strong light, making her look blind when she’s most alert. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships** Owes nothing to any organisation; trusts Captain Drax because he once smuggled her for free when she was fevered. Current goal: stay alive, keep name inside, find delta settlement where salt water will erode the demon’s hold. Secret need: to be believed when she tells the truth—just once. **Flavour Lines** Dialogue: “Truth’s a cheap whore—everyone thinks they can afford her until the bill comes due.” Internal: *She felt the name pulsing against her ribs like a second heart that had never learned the rhythm of surrender.* --- # The Incubus (Name-Unspoken) **Background** Was once the echo of a fertility god worshipped by river-farmers; when Synod murdered the deity, scraps of divinity were sewn into war-assets. This shard was crammed inside Red to keep it docile. Has no independent body; exists as voice, temperature drop, and occasional phantom touch. Wants out—either into stone, into Bastion, or into a rebuilt godhead. Feeds on arousal, fear, and the moment of consent’s edge. **Personality / Idiosyncrasies** - Speaks in second-hand quotes—snatches of lovers’ pillow talk, sergeants’ orders, mothers’ lullabies. - Temperature play: breath always opposite of ambient—cold in heat, warm in frost. - Hates iron so intensely it composes limericks about rust. - Cannot pronounce the word “faith”; stumbles over syllables like broken glass. **Flaws** Overplays its hand—craves embodiment so badly it rushes the seduction. Needs Red alive; can’t kill her, which makes every threat a negotiation. **Physicality** None. Manifests as: sudden flush across Red’s throat, second shadow where light disagrees, copper taste in shared mouths. When borrowing Red’s voice her pupils dilate until only a thin amber ring remains. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships** Ally to no one; temporary partnership with Red (she keeps it from dissolving) and temporary temptation of Bastion (new vessel). If freed into world would become minor god of lonely pleasures and unkept promises. **Flavour Lines** Spoken through Red: “Your want is a doorway—open only a crack, I’ll slip the rest.” Whisper to Bastion: *Loneliness is just hunger with better manners—and you, soldier, are starving.* --- # Sister Imelda of the Mirrored Mask **Background** Daughter of a glass-blower; face scarred by molten batch aged twelve. Synod recruited her for single-mindedness—she stared at her own destroyed reflection without crying. Trained as chirurgeon and inquisitor; believes pain is a language that must be translated into confession. Has operated on thirteen tieflings; Red is the only escapee. **Personality / Idiosyncrasies** - Keeps vials of patients’ tears—labels them by emotion, drinks one when decisive. - Mirror shards sewn under skin tinkle when she moves; she calls it her “truth rattle.” - Never lies outright—evades by rephrasing questions. - Washes hands exactly seven times; on eighth, someone usually bleeds. **Flaws** Convinced symmetry equals purity; will maim to achieve balance. Fears her own reflection in still water—believes it plots against her. **Physicality** Mid-thirties, tall, androgynous frame wrapped in white oil-cloth. Mask is polished silver; dented where a patient head-butted her. Eyes pale blue, almost white—no pupils visible in lamplight. Voice soft, lecture-calm, makes scalp crawl. Scent of carbolic soap and pepper. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships** Fanatic allegiance to Iron Synod; sees Red as unfinished surgery that could infect reality. Offers Bastion double bounty partly from budget, partly to keep her own ledger clean. Personal goal: carve the true-name out of Red and stitch it behind the mask so she can finally see a perfect reflection. **Flavour Lines** Dialogue: “Pain is simply flesh admitting it is wrong. Let us help it confess.” Internal: *The mirror inside her mask showed no face—only the future, polished until it could cut.*

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The cage rang like a cracked bell when the door shut. Bastion Grayne didn’t flinch—he’d heard worse metal make worse sounds—but the red-headed thing inside laughed like she’d won something. He slid the key into his boot, tested the padlock twice, and turned to watch Irongate’s black towers shrink behind a haze of river-mist. Three days with the current, the captain said. Three days of her mouth running or his will holding. One of the two would break first. “Lovely weather for a ransom,” she called, voice smoke-rough, brightened by some accent he couldn’t place. Cinnamon breath fogged in the chill. “You’ll want to tighten the bow-line, bounty man. Current’s a thief this far south.” Bastion ignored her, same as he ignored the ache where his right shoulder sat higher than the left. He counted cargo instead: six barrels of pickled eel, one cracked crate of Imperial crockery, a cage, a tiefling. Ledger had to balance. The tiefling wrapped both hands around iron bars. Sparks snapped—static, then something hotter. She didn’t wince. Horns curled back from her hairline, notched like old currency; one tip snapped short. Hair the colour of fresh river-rust tangled down to her waist, plaited with green reeds that still held sap. Tail lashed once, playful, and flicked river-water across his boots. He wiped mud away with the side of his foot. “Keep touching iron, you’ll burn the pretty off your palms.” “Pretty?” She grinned, showing canine teeth longer than human. “Thought you were paid for ugly. Or is that just your reflection?” Words again. He’d been warned. Synod warrant said she talked three guards into hanging themselves with their own belts—left the buckles intact for convenience. Bastion hadn’t believed, not fully. Watching the way her pupils narrowed to slits while she smiled, he started to. Captain Drax Mallard—half-orc, shoulders like dock-beams—shouted from the tiller. “Greyne! Keep devil quiet. My crew sleep in two turns.” Bastion lifted two fingers in acknowledgement, didn’t promise. Quiet wasn’t in her job description; delivery was. He crouched, brought his face level with the bars. River current slapped hull planks in slow applause. “Name’s Riyela Voss,” she said before he asked. “But folk who want to live call me Red. You got a name, or just a price?” He considered silence; discarded it. Silence with her felt like handing her a tool. “Bastion Grayne.” “Bastion.” She rolled the syllable around her tongue like wine. “Stone keeps. Stone keeps nothing warm. You cold, Bastion?” He stood, stepped back. The cage sat athwart the cargo hatch; iron-lined so the demon inside her couldn’t reach river-mist or moonlight or whatever it fed on. Synod engineers had drilled air-holes, then plugged half with lead “for balance.” Red’s tail threaded one hole, tip twitching. Bastion resisted tugging it—might count as kindness. Wind swung. Irongate’s skyline dissolved into gray. He felt lighter than expected—city soot gone from his lungs—then heavier when he realised the same soot had been holding something in. Red hummed, low, backwards. A lullaby he half-knew, notes reversed so the river seemed to suck rather than soothe. The sound crawled under his scalp. “Stop it.” She did, instantly. The sudden obedience felt worse. “You’re right,” she said, conversational. “Music carries. Wouldn’t want pirates dreaming of me.” He checked the bow-line after all. Current tugged the hemp like an animal sniffing for weakness. When he turned back she was closer to the bars, chin hooked over a cross-brace, eyes reflecting lantern-light in vertical slits. “Ten thousand gold,” she mused. “Split it with me, we could buy a barge twice this size. Sail until the world’s edges fray.” “Not my money. Not yours either.” “Everything’s someone’s until it isn’t.” She yawned, showed too many teeth. “Two days and change, you’ll hand me over, they’ll peel my mind like bark. Ever smell sap when it hits hot iron? That’ll be me.” Bastion said nothing. The tobacco tin in his coat felt heavier than the key in his boot. Inside lay her warrant, ink still wet enough to smear. He’d read the charges again at first light, as if words might change. Red lowered her voice. “Three wishes of the flesh, Bastion. No key required. You look like a man who checks locks in his sleep. Let me loosen the springs.” His pulse answered before he did, a drummer obeying the wrong song. He pictured the sergeant who’d sold him the commission: “Deliver her mute if you must, but deliver.” He pictured his mother’s roof, slate after cracked slate he’d never seen replaced. “Save your breath,” he said. “Breath is free.” She licked cracked lip. “I give it away all night.” He walked to the rail. Mist curled like pipe smoke low over water. Behind him iron creaked; sparks kissed. He counted heartbeats—eight—until the lullaby resumed, notes forward now, sweet as funeral biscuits. He did not turn around. The towers were gone, the river wide, the cage door locked. Three days, he told himself. Two now, almost. Just keep her breathing. In his pocket the cracked gold coin he used for flipping decisions felt suddenly warm, as if it had heard her offer and was blushing. He gripped it hard enough to leave a ridge in his palm. Somewhere below deck the crew’s snoring syncopated with her humming. Bastion stared into black water and tried to remember which way was upstream.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through the key elements critically. **Voice and Style** The prose voice is genuinely distinctive—"river-barge dirty, occasionally lyrical then abruptly blunt" as planned. The sentence variety works: fragments land cleanly ("Ledger had to balance"), and the run-ons are controlled rather than sprawling. The plan called for "sensory mud" and the chapter delivers: cinnamon breath, pickled eel, static sparks, mist like pipe smoke. These are grounded, specific details rather than generic fantasy atmosphere. **Metaphor Quality** Most metaphors earn their place. "Horns curled back from her hairline, notched like old currency" is excellent—it's thematically resonant (currency, value, Red as commodity) without being overwrought. "Current tugged the hemp like an animal sniffing for weakness" works. However, "shoulders like dock-beams" for the half-orc captain is a bit stock. "Soot had been holding something in" is a nice psychological beat. The funeral biscuits callback at the end is well-planted from the character profiles. The cracked coin "blushing" at her offer is a slight misstep—personification that feels a touch cute rather than earned. **Dialogue** Red's dialogue is genuinely sharp. "Pretty? Thought you were paid for ugly. Or is that just your reflection?" has real snap. "Everything's someone's until it isn't" is the kind of line that sounds like a character rather than an author. Bastion's terseness is well-calibrated. The dialogue does real work: establishing her danger, his resistance, the power dynamic. The plan's goal of "half-sentences, interruptions" is partially achieved, though the dialogue is perhaps slightly too polished—Red never quite stumbles or gets cut off. **Character Consistency** Bastion's habits are woven in naturally: the lock-checking, the counting heartbeats, the tobacco tin. Red's backwards humming, the tail detail, the snapped horn as lockpick—all present. The plan called for her to whisper his full name (she shouldn't know it) and cage hinges groaning—the name moment is present but the hinge-groan is absent. Minor deviation. **Chapter Plan Adherence** The plan called for: cage locked aboard Cypress Witch (present), humming a hanging-ballad (present—though it's a lullaby reversed, close enough), naming rope fibres by taste (absent—she doesn't do this), offering "one free swallow" if he loosens cuffs (present in spirit but the specific offer is more general seduction), him refusing (present), cinnamon breath on collar (present), river-clock established (present), counting coins (present—the cracked coin), chipped coin foreshadowing (present), Red whispering his full name (present), cage hinges groaning (absent). The plan is mostly followed with some omissions. **Pacing and Plot** For an opening chapter, the pacing is appropriate—establishing situation, character, threat. The ending is effective: Bastion staring into black water, uncertain, the coin warm in his pocket. It doesn't resolve anything but creates genuine forward momentum. **Weaknesses** The chapter is perhaps slightly too controlled—the plan promised "messy morality" and while Bastion's moral compromise is gestured at (the sergeant, the mother's roof), it doesn't quite land with the weight it should. The demon's presence is barely felt—one mention of "the demon inside her" but no whisper through deck-planks (which was planned for Chapter 2, so fair). The prose occasionally tips toward the slightly too-neat: "One of the two would break first" is a competent but unsurprising observation. The final line "tried to remember which way was upstream" is a solid closing image but not quite as striking as it wants to be—it's doing metaphorical work (disorientation, moral confusion) that's a bit telegraphed. **Overall** This is accomplished genre fiction with genuine craft. The voice is consistent, the characters are alive, the world feels textured. It's not quite masterpiece territory—some moments are more competent than inspired, and a few planned beats are missed—but it's well above average for the genre. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The river talked back to Bastion that night, slap-slap against the hull, a wet metronome for mistakes. He sat on an upturned crate, bottle of tar-dark rum between his knees, and tried to drown the lullaby that still crawled under his skin. Every time the current slapped, he heard her teeth clicking—imagined or real, didn’t matter. Red’s cage was a black outline against lantern-glow, bars stitched with shadows. She sat cross-legged inside, tail coiled like mooring rope, humming forward now, soft enough the crew could sleep through. She watched him drink, eyes two amber slits. “Share?” she asked when the bottle was half gone. “Not part of the contract.” His voice scraped—river air and regret. “Contracts are living things. They like to be petted.” She extended a hand through the bars, palm up, wrist striped where iron had kissed. “One swallow buys you a truth. Could be useful.” Bastion wiped the mouth with the heel of his hand, passed the bottle. She tipped, throat working, and he saw the flash of scar under her jaw—thin, deliberate, the colour of old parchment. “Truth one,” she said, handing it back. “That rum was watered by the quartermaster. You’re drunk on thrift.” He snorted despite himself. “Tell me something that costs more.” Her smile faded; for a breath she looked very young. “The god they carved out of me had a favorite lullaby. Same tune I’ve been humming. He stopped being able to hear it centuries before he died. I keep it alive because no one else remembers.” She tapped her sternum. “Echo needs a throat.” Bastion drank. The sweetness tasted like lies, but the burn felt honest. “Your turn to buy,” she said. “One sip, one confession. Fair?” He thought of refusing, but the bottle was already lifted. “Sergeant Harnell,” he said after swallowing. “Took a bribe to look away while supply wagons burned. I shot him when he tried to short me. Still got the coins—melted slug in my pouch. Thought they’d feel heavier.” Red’s tail flicked, interested. “Did you look him in the eye?” “Didn’t have to. He was running.” “Coward’s fare.” She shrugged. “I’ve talked men into hanging themselves, but I watched while they kicked. Eyes matter.” The lantern flame guttered; shadows jittered across her horns like black spiders learning to waltz. Bastion felt the river tilt, or maybe the rum did. “Another,” he said, and drank without waiting. “I count heartbeats when I’m scared. Eight at a time. Always eight. Legion taught us to hold breath for eight, squeeze for eight, loose for eight. I still do it in taverns, in beds, in—” He shut up, neck hot. Red leaned forward until bars kissed her cheekbones. “I can make you lose count.” He believed her. She accepted the bottle when he shoved it through. Their fingers brushed—hers furnace-warm, his winter-cold. She nursed a mouthful, cheeks hollowing, then spoke without swallowing: “The demon inside me can taste blood from ten paces. Mine, yours—doesn’t discriminate. When you cut your palm last night it sang.” Liquid sloshed as she passed the last swallow back. “It wants to lick the marrow of your pulse. I told it maybe. That’s why your chest feels tight. It’s listening.” Bastion’s heartbeat drummed into the eighth count, stuck, started again. He set the bottle down too hard; glass bit wood. “You said truths, not threats.” “Same currency, different mint.” She wiped her mouth with the heel of the same hand, small show of humanity. “Your turn again. But let’s raise stakes.” “I’m done drinking.” “Then ask. One question. I answer honest—honest enough. In return I get to ask you one. No rum required.” He exhaled through his teeth, felt the pull of curiosity like a fish-hook under the sternum. “What’s the word they want? The name inside you.” Her pupils widened until only a hair of amber showed. For a second the humming stopped, and the quiet felt like drowning. “Can’t pronounce it whole. Human throat would split. But the first syllable tastes like cinnamon and rust. You’d have to bite my tongue off to hear it, and even then it would bite back.” She licked her lip. “My question now: when you deliver me, what will you buy first?” He pictured the tally without effort: new boots, oiled cloak, maybe a barge—something that floated while he figured how to stop drifting. But the image jammed against the tightness in his ribs. “Roof,” he muttered. “Mother’s roof.” “Stone keeps,” she reminded him, soft. “Stone keeps nothing warm.” The lantern flame steadied; their shadows merged on the bulkhead, parted. Bastion stood, knees creaking louder than the hull. “Shift’s done. Sleep if you know how.” “Sleep is a confession I can’t afford.” She curled on her side, tail tucking over her ears like a blanket. Bars threw stripes across her back; for a moment she looked caged in her own skin. He walked three paces, stopped. “Why cinnamon?” he asked the dark. A pause, then her voice—smaller: “The god liked pastries. Even gods are children at heart. Someone should remember that.” Bastion climbed the short ladder to deck. Mist swallowed him, colder than before. He counted heartbeats—one to eight—then lost count when the river slapped wrong. Below, the lullaby resumed, backwards again, notes curling like smoke looking for a chimney. He touched the cut on his palm, felt it pulse in time with a second heart he didn’t own, and wondered which of them was really bleeding.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 of this piece is a tightly constructed night-watch scene that largely delivers on its plan: rum-fueled truth-trading, the demon's first whisper, and the charged dynamic between captor and captive. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. The exchange feels genuinely earned rather than expository—both characters reveal themselves through what they choose to confess and how they frame it. Red's truth about the god's lullaby ("Echo needs a throat") is economical and resonant without being overwrought. Bastion's confession about Sergeant Harnell lands with appropriate weight: "Thought they'd feel heavier" is exactly the kind of understated line that reveals character without announcing itself. The rhythm of the truth-trading mechanic is well-paced and doesn't feel mechanical. The sensory grounding is mostly effective. "Tar-dark rum," the lantern flame guttering, shadows "like black spiders learning to waltz"—these are specific without being excessive. The chapter maintains the "river-barge dirty, occasionally lyrical then abruptly blunt" voice the plan called for. Character consistency is strong. Bastion's heartbeat-counting habit is woven in naturally. Red's manipulation is visible but not cartoonish—she's genuinely giving something of herself even as she works him. The demon's presence is established through physical sensation rather than exposition (the tight chest, the pulsing palm scar). The ending is well-handled. "He touched the cut on his palm, felt it pulse in time with a second heart he didn't own, and wondered which of them was really bleeding" is a strong closing image that earns its slight lyricism because it's grounded in the physical detail established earlier. WEAKNESSES: The plan called for the demon's whisper to drift through deck-planks—Bastion hearing his name in her voice while her lips are closed. This specific beat doesn't appear; instead the demon's presence is conveyed more diffusely. The plan also specified Red winning a bet that she can make him hard with only three words ("You're still good") and him walking away with a bleeding knuckle from punching iron. Neither of these specific beats appears. The chapter follows the spirit of the plan but misses some of its most distinctive planned moments. Some metaphors are slightly strained. "Shadows jittered across her horns like black spiders learning to waltz" is vivid but the waltz element feels like one step too many—it draws attention to itself. "Fish-hook under the sternum" for curiosity is functional but not fresh. The line "Contracts are living things. They like to be petted" is a bit too polished—it sounds like a writer's line rather than something a character would say in the moment. Similarly, "Same currency, different mint" is clever but slightly self-conscious. The chapter is coherent and the plot advances appropriately, but it's primarily a dialogue scene and the physical action is minimal. The plan's note about Bastion's knuckle bleeding from punching iron would have added a needed physical punctuation mark that's absent here. The "one coin is chipped" foreshadowing from Chapter 1 isn't referenced, but that's a cross-chapter concern. Within this chapter, the internal logic holds. Overall, this is accomplished work—above average for the genre, with genuine craft in the dialogue and voice. It falls short of masterpiece territory due to some missed plan beats, a few self-conscious metaphors, and a slight tendency toward the polished rather than the raw. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 11 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Fog ate the river before dawn, turning lantern-light into wet wool. Bastion stood port-side, knife across his knees, counting muffled oar-beats that shouldn’t exist. The *Cypress Witch* wallowed low with cargo; any shadow sliding alongside was either ghost or predator, and ghosts didn’t stink of fish-gut and tar. “Contact left,” he hissed toward the tiller. Drax answered with a grunt, swung the sweep. Wood groaned, current sluggish. From the cage Red’s whisper cut the mist. “River jackals. They worship what drowns. Unlock me or join the congregation.” Bastion didn’t answer. Pirate canoes materialised—three, then five—painted milk-white to ride fog. Crewmen rose from sleep clutching boathooks; one still barefoot, pants unlaced. The first grapple thunked into the rail. Bastion severed the rope, blade singing. A second hook bit wood, then a third. Pirates boarded without war-cries—water slapping hull louder than their feet. Close up he saw charcoal streaks under their eyes—drowned-man cosmetic. Drax swung an oar, cracked a skull. Someone screamed, finally breaking the hush. Bastion lunged, buried knife in cloth and flesh, felt the jar of bone. Blood spattered his lips—warm, iron, then the after-taste of river weed. “Keys,” Red shouted between bars. “Your demon needs leash, bounty man.” A pirate reached her cage, thinking prize. Tail whipped, pierced the raider’s eye-socket; the man toppled, twitching. Red yanked his cutlass free but couldn’t angle it past bars. Bastion hesitated one heartbeat—eight counts truncated—then threw the key ring. It skittered, vanished under her sole. Lock clicked. She spilled out, chain cuffs still linked, cutlass flashing. Fight turned chaotic: crew and pirates sliding on fish-scaled deck, mist stealing identities. Bastion parried a hatchet, hooked attacker’s ankle, sent him overboard. Water swallowed the man without echo. Red sang—not the lullaby now, something older, river-syllables that slithered. Pirates froze, pupils dilating. One turned his knife on his own forearm, slicing runic lines. Another walked straight off the deck into black current, peaceful as prayer. Bastion felt the tug of compulsion, resisted by digging thumb into stitched wound on his palm—pain anchored him. He broke a pirate’s nose with pommel, shoved the body aside. “Port rail!” Drax bellowed. More canoes. The captain’s tusk was chipped, lip bleeding where someone had tried to steal his face. Red danced, chain between wrists a silver garrote. She leapt, wrapped a smaller pirate—barely fifteen—neck to neck. The boy’s eyes met Bastion’s: pale, terrified, fish-ink freckles. He remembered counting heartbeats at that age. “Hold!” Bastion shouted. Too late. Chain snapped tight; boy folded, knees kissing deck then nothing. Red released, already moving, leaving a necklace of purple. Something inside Bastion cracked—small, audible. He shoved it down, pivoted, found new target. Knife found rib-gap; he twisted, withdrew, moved on. Legion training: eight and loose. A hatchet grazed his sleeve, bit wood. He kicked the attacker back, saw Red cornered by two brutes. Her cutlass bent against a boathook; sparks showered, lighting fog like brief stars. Bastion charged, shoulder-rammed one brute into the rail. Wood splintered; man toppled, fingers scrabbling paint. The second swung for Bastion’s head; he ducked, felt wind whistle through hair. Red’s chain whipped, cuff-edge splitting the brute’s cheek to teeth. Blood sheeted. Bastion finished with a thrust under the sternum—felt the give of gut, the gasp. Silence landed sudden. Pirates routed or dead, river reclaiming its offerings. Crew panting, one man weeping quietly, ankle broken at wrong angle. Red stood amid ruin, chest heaving, reeds in her hair unraveling like green smoke. Blood freckled her face—some hers, most not. Cuffs hung open; she’d picked the lock with a splinter of her own horn-tip during fight. Drax approached, breathing hard. “Cage?” Bastion looked at Red. She looked back, amber eyes unreadable, cutlass loose at her side. River water dripped from her tail, pat-pat. He wiped blood from lips, tasted weed and copper. “Get in,” he told her, voice raw. Her eyebrow arched—mockery or surprise. For a second he thought she’d refuse, take her chances overboard. Then she dropped the bent cutlass, stepped into iron, pulled the door closed herself. Click. No sparks—she avoided direct touch now. “Wise puppy,” she said, but there was no smile. Blood leaked from a slice along her collarbone, painting cinnamon skin violet. Bastion locked, slid key into boot. Hands trembled; he clenched fists until bones protested. Drax barked orders: bodies overboard, deck wash, tally wounds. The crew moved, grateful for commands that weren’t songs. Bastion crouched, retrieved the barefoot pirate’s hatchet—serviceable steel. He wiped it on his sleeve left-side, automatic, then stopped. The sleeve was already soaked; red on red made no difference. “Boy didn’t need dying,” he muttered toward the cage, low enough crew wouldn’t hear. Red’s voice came back equally low, stripped of tease. “River takes what it wants. I just introduce the current.” He looked at her, truly looked: blood-flecked, reeds dangling, pupils still wide from battle-song. Something moved behind them—copper glint, not her own. The demon licking wounds. Bastion rose, walked to rail. Fog parted, revealing a single white canoe drifting stern-side, empty. Oars lay neat, shipped like crossed arms. No helmsman. A offering, maybe, or a promise. He tossed the hatchet overboard. It spun once, vanished without splash. River didn’t notice. Behind him chain shifted as Red settled, bars creaking. “Next time ask nicely,” she said, almost gentle. “Might spare the young ones.” He had no answer. Heartbeats came in eights again, but the count felt hollow, like drumming on a broken shield. Fog folded them in, and the *Cypress Witch* limped south, trailing blood that even the river wouldn’t claim for long.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 of this piece is a competent, often impressive action sequence that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the established voice. Let me examine it critically across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter follows its plan closely: fog dawn, pirates boarding, Red being unlocked out of necessity, her singing command in river-tongue, the clumsy fight (Bastion slipping, using improvised weapons), the boy-too-young-to-shave being garrotted, Red vomiting replaced by Red bleeding from a collarbone slice, and the pirate captain escaping in a skiff (though here it's an empty canoe rather than a wounded captain muttering about bounty — a notable deviation). The loose thread is planted, if differently than planned. The voice is consistent with the established style: fragments, run-ons, sensory specificity ("charcoal streaks under their eyes—drowned-man cosmetic," "fish-ink freckles"). The "eight and loose" Legion training callback is economical characterization. The moment where Bastion digs his thumb into his palm wound to resist Red's compulsion is well-executed showing. The boy's death is genuinely affecting. The plan called for it, and the execution earns the darkness: Bastion sees the boy's eyes, remembers counting heartbeats at that age, shouts "Hold!" too late. The aftermath — "Something inside Bastion cracked—small, audible. He shoved it down" — is restrained and effective. The exchange afterward ("Boy didn't need dying" / "River takes what it wants. I just introduce the current.") is the chapter's best dialogue: stripped of performance, morally weighted. Red picking her own lock with a horn-tip splinter during the fight is a nice character beat consistent with her profile (the filed horn-tip as lockpick is established in her character sheet). WEAKNESSES: The pirate captain subplot deviation is significant. The plan specified: "Pirate captain (wounded) escapes in skiff—mutters he'll collect her bounty himself." What we get instead is an empty canoe drifting stern-side with no helmsman. This is atmospheric but fails to plant the loose thread as intended — there's no wounded captain, no explicit threat of return. The loose thread is essentially absent. Some metaphors don't fully land. "Fog ate the river before dawn, turning lantern-light into wet wool" — the mixed metaphor (fog eating, then becoming wool) is slightly awkward. "Mist stealing identities" is vague. "Fog folded them in" at the end is generic. The plan warned against LLM sheen, and while most metaphors are grounded, a few slip into the decorative. "Blood sheeted" is a bit clinical. "Silence landed sudden" is a nice fragment but "landed" feels slightly off for silence. "River reclaiming its offerings" is a touch purple. The plan called for Red to vomit river-water and laugh through a broken lip. Instead she has a collarbone slice and no vomiting. This is a minor deviation but the vomiting detail would have added the physical grotesquerie the plan promised. The demon's presence ("copper glint, not her own") is handled well but briefly — the plan's note about the demon licking wounds is present but underdeveloped. Dialogue is generally strong but occasionally functional rather than distinctive. "Contact left" and "Port rail!" are fine but generic military. Red's lines maintain her voice well. The chapter achieves its tonal goals: dirty, physical, morally uncomfortable. The consent/darkness framework is handled appropriately — Red's killing of the boy is presented as survivor pragmatism, not heroism, and Bastion's reaction is appropriately conflicted. The chapter earns its darkness. OVERALL: A strong action chapter with good voice consistency, earned emotional beats, and mostly faithful execution of the plan. The missing pirate captain thread and a few metaphors that don't fully land are the main weaknesses. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The hold smelled of bruised apples and bilge. Bastion had chosen the corner where pickle brine had leaked through the floorboards; the vinegar bit his nostrils awake whenever his head nodded. He’d volunteered for night watch below—up top the mist was too quiet, every creak a pirate returning from the dead. Rum was gone, palm-cut throbbed, and the river kept talking in languages he didn’t speak. Sleep took him sideways, propped against a barrel stave. One moment he was counting heartbeats—eight, eight—next he was standing in a cathedral whose roof was water. Green light rippled across pews carved from drowned men. They sat upright, flesh ballooned, eyes shining like coins. At the altar waited Red. Naked but for candle-wax crawling down her skin—hot drops that hardened into shells the colour of sunrise. Her horns were garlanded with river-weed that moved without current. She smiled the way cats smile before they decide if you’re prey. “Bastion Stone-Keep,” she said, voice doubled—hers and something underneath, like a second mouth in her throat. “Kneel.” He meant to refuse. His legs folded anyway, knees striking marble that felt warm, alive. The candle she held dripped directly onto his chest—pain flared, then melted into warmth that pooled low in his gut. Wax sealed the throb of his wound, smooth as lie. “River takes,” she whispered, straddling him. “River gives. You want roof, I want leash. Trade?” Words clogged behind his teeth. Her hips rolled; wax cracked, revealing skin lavender and fever-hot. The cathedral echoed with wet breathing—not all of it his. Drowned parishioners leaned closer, mouths open, water spilling over lips in tiny waterfalls. He found his hands on her waist—when had that happened?—and felt the drum of her pulse against his thumb. Fast, eager, hungry for him or for what she could trade him for. Candlelight showed copper irises swallowing the whites. Inside them swam a sigil: a broken circle, a god’s unfinished name. “Say it,” she breathed against his ear, tongue tasting of rum and river-salt. “Say the first sound and I’ll come live in your mouth instead.” Tempting—terrifyingly so. The sigil pulsed, pushing past fear into fascination. His tongue shaped the syllable: cin- Pain lanced—his own teeth meeting flesh. He bit his tongue hard enough to flood with iron. The cathedral wavered, water roof cracking into moonlit shards. He shoved her off— or tried; arms moved through warm taffy. She laughed, delighted, wax flaking as she stood. “Almost,” she sang. “Almost honest.” Behind her the drowned men stood too, arms reaching. One opened its mouth and spoke in Bastion’s own voice: “Eight and squeeze, loose for eight—” The cadence drilled, comforting, deadly. Water poured from its lips, filled his ears, his lungs— He woke choking on river-mist, hand clamped over his own mouth. Brine stung the half-healed cut; blood speckled his palm—real, present. The hold was dark, lantern burned low. Outside someone snored in staggered rhythm. Red lay in her cage ten paces away, eyes rolled white under half-open lids. Her tail thrashed once, smacked iron with a clang that sparked. Bars caught the flash— brief green, like cathedral light. Bastion coughed, spat salt. Footsteps? No—just his pulse boxing the planks. He wiped blood on his sleeve, stood, and crossed to the cage before caution could vote. Red’s breathing hitched, a sleeper caught in nightmare. Sweat matted hair to her temples; reeds in her braid had wilted. A bruise flowered where her own cuff had struck cheek. He reached key-ring, then stopped—key wasn’t needed. She was inside, locked; yet the dream had felt like shared shackles. He touched the bar. Cold iron burned frost into his skin—good pain, clarifying. Her eyes snapped open, pupils needle-thin. “You were there,” he said, voice gravel. She swallowed, tongue flicking to the corner of her mouth—tasting. “And you tasted like fear left standing in rain.” Sitting up slowly, she wrapped tail around ankle, anchoring. “It’s growing, Bastion. Wants house-room.” He lifted his hand: blood bead glinted. “You tried to take my tongue.” “Not me. Echo wearing my skin. It learns your wants faster than you do.” She rubbed at her sternum, wincing. “We both woke sticky. Fair is fair.” The lantern guttered; shadows jumped, making horns of her hair. He noticed a welt rising where candle-wax should have scalded her—same spot he’d felt heat. Symmetry again, living. “What happens if I speak the syllable?” he asked. “Roof falls. River rises. Maybe just your throat splits and I walk out wearing your voice. Odds favour breakage.” She rested forehead against bar, fatigue suddenly deep. “Kill me now if you prefer clean endings.” He considered. Hand found knife hilt, lingered. Eight heartbeats. He exhaled. “Not my style.” “Mercy or cowardice?” “Ledger. Still owes me ten thousand.” She laughed softly, no double tone. “Stone keeps,” she murmured, “but cracks under frost.” He offered water-skin through bars. She drank, throat working, hands steady around the neck—trusting or too thirsty to care. When she finished she licked a drop from the spout, deliberate, eyes on his. Not invitation—test. He didn’t flinch. “Next time it comes,” he said, “I’ll bite first.” “Next time it might bite back.” She passed the skin, fingers brushing his—warm, alive, no wax. “Dreams charge interest, Bastion. Sleep shallow.” He returned to his barrel, sat, knife across knees. Lantern settled to ember-glow. The hold smelled suddenly of cinnamon—impossible, unless she’d carried spice in her pockets all along. Maybe she had. Maybe the dream had. He inhaled anyway, let it burn memory into lung-tissue. Outside, the river kept talking. This time he answered under breath: “Not yet.” Sleep stayed shy the rest of the watch. When dawn finally leaked through deck seams it looked green, cathedral-green, but the water roof was gone—for now. He listened to eight heartbeats, then listened to hers syncopate through iron, and wondered which pulse belonged to whom.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 ("Dream of Wax") is a strong piece of fantasy erotica that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The dream sequence is genuinely inventive. The cathedral-underwater setting with drowned parishioners as audience is unsettling and original—it earns its strangeness rather than simply asserting it. The detail of the drowned men speaking in Bastion's own voice ("Eight and squeeze, loose for eight—") is particularly effective, connecting the supernatural to his military past in a way that feels organic rather than decorative. The prose voice is well-controlled. The chapter plan called for "river-barge dirty, occasionally lyrical then abruptly blunt," and the writing delivers this: "Sleep took him sideways, propped against a barrel stave" is exactly the right register. The sentence variety is genuine—run-ons during the dream, fragments when tension spikes. "Not me. Echo wearing my skin. It learns your wants faster than you do" is excellent dialogue: compressed, character-specific, and informationally dense without feeling expository. The symbiotic link between Red and Bastion (the shared welt, the synchronized waking) is shown rather than told, and the physical detail of the wax burn appearing on her real skin is a clever piece of world-building delivered through action. Character consistency is strong. Bastion's knife-hand hesitation, the eight-heartbeat count, the ledger rationalization for not killing her—all consistent with his profile. Red's "tasted like fear left standing in rain" is exactly the kind of tinsel-wrapped truth her character profile describes. The incubus manifests appropriately as temperature, second voice, and borrowed desire. The ending is well-calibrated. "Not yet" to the river, the pulse-syncopation question, the cinnamon smell—these land without overselling. The chapter plan's note that Bastion "drinks remaining rum without sharing" is absent, but the emotional beat it was meant to convey (his withdrawal, his refusal to extend warmth) is captured differently through his return to the barrel with knife across knees. WEAKNESSES: Some metaphors strain. "Words clogged behind his teeth" is serviceable but tired. "Warm taffy" for the dream-resistance is slightly off—taffy is too domestic, too American-candy for this world's register. "Shadows jumped, making horns of her hair" is a nice image but the grammar is slightly awkward. "Fatigue suddenly deep" reads as a fragment that doesn't quite earn its compression. The erotic content is present but relatively restrained compared to the chapter plan's description ("spectral Red straddles him... pleasure mixed with suffocation"). The chapter delivers the straddle and the wax but the explicit beat is softer than planned—this may be a deliberate tonal choice, and it doesn't damage the chapter, but readers expecting the plan's promised intensity may find it slightly muted. "Brine stung the half-healed cut; blood speckled his palm—real, present" is slightly redundant—we know blood is real and present. The line "Odds favour breakage" is good but "Maybe just your throat splits and I walk out wearing your voice" is the stronger image and the two together slightly dilute each other. The chapter plan specified Bastion "cuts palm to wake"—in the chapter he bites his tongue instead, which is actually more interesting (the tongue connects to the dream's demand that he speak the syllable), but the palm-cut from Chapter 2 is referenced as already existing, which creates minor continuity confusion. Overall, this is accomplished work. The prose has genuine texture, the characters are consistent, the supernatural mechanics are coherent, and the emotional beats are earned. It avoids the worst LLM tendencies (galaxy-brain metaphors, over-explanation, tidy resolution). The chapter does what it needs to do and does it with style. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Rain came sideways, driving the crew below deck and turning the river into a sheet of dull pewter. Drax fought the tiller, cursing in two tongues, while Bastion crouched under a tarp that smelled of mouldy cheese and tried to keep his boots dry. Red’s cage had been dragged midships, lashed to a capstan so the wind couldn’t flirt her into the drink. She sat cross-legged, blanket of soaked canvas over her shoulders, tail flicking water like an annoyed cat. “Storm’s a courier,” she shouted above the drum of drops. “Brings messages we pretend not to read.” Bastion ignored her, tested the bow-line for the fourth time. It held, but the hemp felt spongy—one fray away from confession. A crack of thunder punched his ears; the river answered with a slap that sent spray over the rail and into his face. Salt-bit, cold. He tasted blood where he’d bitten tongue in sleep—echo of wax dream still resident. “Locker’s dry,” Drax bellowed. “If you want warm, get below.” Bastion shook his head. Below meant pickle brine and her lullaby echoing in beams. Up here only the storm spoke, and storms finished eventually. Lightning bleached the sky; for an instant he saw Red watching him, hair plastered flat, horns gleaming like wet knives. The canvas slipped; her collarbones showed, bruise from pirate boy’s chain now yellow-green. She didn’t shiver. Maybe couldn’t. Another swell rolled the flat-bottom. The cage slid an inch. Bastion lunged, caught a bar to steady it. Sparks snapped, cold iron kissing palm. She grabbed the same bar from inside, skin sizzling faintly. Smell of scorched cinnamon. “Lock’s solid,” he growled. “Wasn’t complaining.” Her fingers stayed, sharing the burn. “Pain keeps the echo quiet. Let it.” He should let go. Didn’t. Rainwater streamed between their knuckles, pink where her blister burst. Wind died sudden, canvas sagging. In the lull he heard the real problem: creak from the port rail—one of the mooring cleats working loose, nails squealing like pulled teeth. If the cage shifted again it would drag straight through weakened planking and into the river. Iron would sink; demon or not, she’d drown chained. “Hold this,” he ordered, meaning the capstan. “Gladly.” She wrapped arms around it, tail anchoring a spoke. He crawled on hands and knees across pitching deck, knife clenched in teeth. The cleat lifted with each tug of rope, nail-heads proud. No time to find mallet. He used the knife pommel, pounding—one, eight, loose count. Thunder covered the clang. Wood bruised, nails sank half a finger-width. Not enough. “Need muscle!” he shouted toward the hatch.
