Hands permanently gloved in rough skin; can pick nettles bare-handed. Wears faded camo under knit cardigan—looks like tree wearing grandma. Eyes pale blue, shoot with dominant left, writes with right—says it keeps enemies guessing. **Motive / ties** Preserve the young so something can sting tomorrow. Sees the mushroom herd as livestock the Lord insulted her with—she’ll tend them, butcher them, but won’t love them. Mab is “that girl who reads too much,” Pike “the cook who seasons like he’s scared,” Tadpole “the angel or the test, not sure which.” **Voice samples** Dialogue: “Ain’t murder if it’s already compost wearing sneakers.” Internal: I aim for the stem, not the cap—every living thing has a neck if you look hard enough.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
23 Jan – but the 3 keeps smudging because my thumb bleeds when I chew it. Cold count: three crackers left, one stuck to the cardboard like it’s trying to evolve a shell. Pike keeps picking it up, putting it down, wiping his hand on his thigh. The tin itself gave up yesterday—lid twisted off without complaint, almost polite. Inside: some orange dust that tastes like salt’s sick cousin. We licked it anyway. Outside the lodge windows the snow is a dirty quilt someone keeps punching. Wind finds the cracks, whistles like it’s calling dogs. Tadpole hums underneath—B-flat, always B-flat—snow eats the vibration before it hits the glass. We drew straws for who’d suit up and check the woodshed. Short straw jumped into my fist. I wore two of everything, still felt my heart rattling. The suit is duct-tape patchwork; every rip is a mouth. Before I left Gran June pressed a single bullet into my glove. “For you or the shed,” she said. Her eyes had that Sunday-school certainty that makes me want to sin. The snow path is a trench we’ve worn by starvation—boots, knees, sometimes bellies. Halfway there my legs started the twitch they do when calories turn to fumes. I counted fungus words under my breath—basidiocarp, sporocarp, basidiocarp—like a rosary that won’t save me. Inside the shed smelled of wet iron and something sweeter, almost kind. The beam of my headlamp jittered across the logs, found it stuck between a birch round and the wall: tall as Tadpole, cap the color of storm meat, gills pulsing like they’re sucking air. Arm-thick stalk rooted through the floorboards—mycelium veins glowing faint as watch hands. When it saw me it didn’t lunge, just leaned, like it’s curious what hunger looks like in a person. I thought: data. I thought: sample. Then I thought: meat. My knife is a Mora I’ve shaved labs pencils with; blade went in squeaky, like carving cold butter. The thing sighed—actual sigh—spores puffed, smelled like steak left in a hot car with a bouquet of garlic. Mouth responding before brain, flood of spit so sharp it hurt. I stabbed again, kept stabbing until it folded, leaking grey jelly that steamed in the cold. Sounded wetter than it should. When it stopped moving the glow died, like someone turned off a nightlight. I hacked free a hunk the size of my fist. Fibres tore like cooked chicken, only iridescent. Part of me waited for revulsion; nothing arrived except maybe curiosity wearing hunger’s coat. I should’ve bagged it, labelled, weighed. Instead I held the piece to my face, breathed. Truffle, iron, something floral I couldn’t name—like the university greenhouse the day they bloomed the corpse flower, only edible. A crack behind me—Pike, face grey, lips colour of wet cement. “Jesus, Mab.” But his eyes were on the lump in my glove, not the corpse. He licked his lip, quick flick. “We taking it or… tasting here?” I realised I’d already bitten it. Juices down my chin, warm as new blood. Flavour detonated—maple, venison, msg. My knees folded; not fainting, genuflect. Pike caught the chunk before it hit snow, tore a strip with his teeth. Eyes rolled white. We stood there chewing like church, no amen, just swallow. Back inside we didn’t speak except logistics: cast-iron, rainwater, onion powder one shake only. Tadpole dragged the skull-cap like a sled, left spore prints that looked deliberate—maybe they are. Gran June eyeballed us, knew something had changed, chambered the bullet instead of asking. Heat hits meat, kitchen fills with that car-steak-garlic cloud but intensified, almost too much, like the world trying to crawl inside your nose. Fat renders silver then gold. My stomach howled, clawed. We portioned polite, lasted three seconds, forked more. Tenderness obscene; I bit my tongue, didn’t care, blood added body. Pike made a sound halfway between giggle and sob, kept repeating “It’s in my teeth, it’s living in my teeth.” When the pot shone empty we scraped with fingers, burned tips, kept scraping. I waited for cramp, nausea, hallucination. Nothing arrived except a warm hum starting in my feet, rising like floodwater, until even the cold windows felt kind. For the first time in months the wind sounded far away, almost somebody else’s problem. Tadpole placed the cap-hat on his head, sat under the table, humming louder. Gran June cleaned the rifle slow, eyes soft the way I’ve seen in people who just prayed or just killed. She didn’t ask what next; we already knew. I meant to write scientific observations: weight, spore count, risk matrix. Instead I wrote: “We are still chewing. Nothing has died that isn’t us.” Ink smeared where I wiped my mouth. The pencil smells of garlic now; I can’t tell if that’s the food or me. Pike’s asleep upright, fork handle against lip like a cigarette he never lights. Outside the snow keeps falling, but the shed is empty, and the world feels suddenly, dangerously, edible.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through the key elements critically. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Mab's voice is genuinely distinctive and consistent with her profile. The mycologist's vocabulary deployed as a rosary ("basidiocarp, sporocarp, basidiocarp"), the compulsive data-gathering that collapses into appetite, the self-aware irony ("Part of me waited for revulsion; nothing arrived except maybe curiosity wearing hunger's coat") — these feel earned rather than performed. The detail of the pencil smelling of garlic at the end is a small, perfect touch. Pike's characterization is efficient and consistent: the fork handle against lip like an unlit cigarette, the "It's living in my teeth" line that captures both his cook's sensibility and his unraveling. Gran June's single action — chambering the bullet instead of asking — is excellent show-don't-tell. Tadpole's spore prints that "looked deliberate" is quietly chilling. METAPHORS AND POETRY: Most of the figurative language lands. "Snow is a dirty quilt someone keeps punching" is vivid without being overwrought. "Curiosity wearing hunger's coat" is genuinely good. "We stood there chewing like church, no amen, just swallow" is the chapter's best line — it earns its weight. However, there are moments where the density of metaphor becomes slightly self-conscious: "every rip is a mouth" on the duct-tape suit is a bit too neat, and "flavour detonated — maple, venison, msg" is effective but the MSG specificity feels slightly calculated for quirk. "Genuflect" used as a verb without "I" is a stylistic choice that works but draws attention to itself. The "souls changing shift" line from Pike's internal voice (referenced in the plan) doesn't appear, which is fine — the chapter doesn't need it. HORROR AND COZY TENSION: The chapter successfully walks the line the plan demands. The warmth that rises "like floodwater" after eating, the windows feeling "kind," the world feeling "dangerously, edible" — these are genuinely unsettling because they're presented as comfort. The horror is in the ease of the capitulation, not in any monster-movie moment. This is sophisticated. DIALOGUE: Sparse, which suits the chapter. Pike's "Jesus, Mab" followed by his eyes going to the meat rather than the corpse is excellent. Gran June's "For you or the shed" is perfectly in character. The dialogue doesn't overstay its welcome. WEAKNESSES: The phrase "blood added body" is a bit too clever, a wine-tasting term applied to self-injury that feels slightly forced. "Tenderness obscene" is a fragment that works but sits in a cluster of similar staccato fragments that occasionally feel like a tic rather than a choice. The ending — "the world feels suddenly, dangerously, edible" — is strong but the adverb pile ("suddenly, dangerously") is slightly slack for an otherwise precise writer. The chapter is dense with good writing to the point where a few moments feel like they're competing for attention rather than accumulating. PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows the plan closely — three crackers, Pike's shaking hands (here it's his eyes and lips rather than hands, a minor deviation), the woodshed discovery, the twelve stabs (here it's multiple stabs without the specific count, another minor deviation), the grey flesh steaming, Pike licking the knife (here he tears with his teeth instead), the skull-cap hat, the fingertip-shaped growths (mentioned in plan but absent from chapter — a notable omission), and the POSSIBLE PARASITE note (also absent). These omissions are minor but real. OVERALL: This is high-quality literary horror writing that mostly earns its ambitions. The voice is consistent, the horror is genuinely unsettling, and the cozy-horror tension is well-managed. A few moments of over-density and a couple of plan elements dropped, but these are minor complaints against a strong execution. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Later—same page, edge gone soft from steam. I tell myself I’ll log symptoms: heart rate, time to salivation, any visual disturbances. Instead I keep licking the pen tip, tasting iron and maple. The fire crackles like it’s taking notes for us. We ate the whole knob, maybe two pounds. Should have stretched it—ration math says that’s four days protein. Hunger vetoed arithmetic. Pike keeps rubbing his stomach the way you rub a dog that’s done good: surprised, proud, a little scared. Tadpole wedged the skull-cap over his ears. Spores sift down, dusting his eyelashes gold. He doesn’t blink, just stares into the flames, humming that single note. I wonder if the note is a lighthouse or a dinner bell for whatever’s outside. Gran June hasn’t spoken since scrape three. She cleaned the rifle, re-cleaned, now sets it butt-down against the table like a third guest. Finally she says, “Tastes like the ham my mama boiled with Coca-Cola,” voice soft, cracked. Then: “We got more?” Her eyes are wet, angry-wet. I shrug. “The shed’s got roots through the floor. Could be others.” My voice sounds borrowed. Pike stands too fast, chair skids. “Then we harvest. Tonight. Before sun—” He stops, remembers sun is meaningless, laughs through his nose. Laughter smells of the meat; I taste it in the air. We divvy jobs. Me: identify, slice, bag. Pike: heat, render, store. Gran: guard, count bullets, pretend she’s not licking her lips every breath. Tadpole gets the lantern—he follows spores the way kids once followed fireflies. Outside smells different now, like the world turned its pocket inside out and revealed sweeter lining. Snow still there, wind still razor, but every shadow looks edible. I lead with the headlamp, beam shaking. The shed door creaks open on something breathing—just night air, maybe. Inside: the carcass sagged, grey gone ivory, juices frozen into caramel sheets. Around it the floorboards ripple with mycelial lace—delicate, bridal, glowing heart-beat faint. I kneel, knife ready. The lace reaches for my boot, stops short, as if remembering manners. I hack a steak anyway. No protest, only a sigh of spores that taste the same only weaker—like memory of food rather than food. Pike hovers, whispering skillet temps: “Medium rare’s one-thirty-five, but this ain’t beef, could be tree for all we know—” He’s talking to stay brave; I let him. We saw off chunks, fill a pillowcase. Weight feels like wet laundry, smells like Christmas dinner we never had. Back indoors the fire’s down to embers. Gran feeds it the last dry maple; sparks climb her beard of breath. She doesn’t flinch. “Smoke’ll carry,” she mutters. “Carry to who, what, I don’t know.” We drop the pillowcase on the table—thud, juice blooms through cotton. Pike’s already heating the pan, no oil, just the meat’s own fat seeping. First slice hits iron: hiss, that garlic-truffle cloud again, stronger, like it multiplied in the bag. My knees try to fold; I lock them. Tadpole crawls onto counter, cross-legged, watching bubbles. He picks a sizzling shard, juggles hot potato style, pops it in mouth, smiles with tears tracking spore-dust. No blisters—immune or numb, file that for never. We taste. It’s better—aged in its own death, fibres relaxed, sweetness concentrated. I wait for nausea; instead my vision sharpens: every brick in the hearth stands out like a tooth. Heart drums four-four. I write: “Effect—euphoric, pupil dilation, possible amphetamine analogue.” Then I underline possible three times because the pen digs through paper. Plate comes around again; we don’t pass, just fork. Conversation is chewing, swallow, exhale. Outside, wind slams the siding, jealous. Inside, grease coats the air, our lungs, the inside of every word we don’t say. Gran breaks first: “We’re farming them now.” Not a question. She wipes her mouth with back of hand, leaves a silver streak. “Need corral, need fence, need—” She stops, realising she’s planning ranch logistics for creatures that used to be us. Shudder or shrug, can’t tell, she keeps eating. Pike starts listing dishes: beer batter, jerky strips, confit in canned lard if we find any. Voice high, kitchen-drunk. I join, can’t help it: “We’ll need salt, smoke, cold storage—snow works short term.” We sound like entrepreneurs. Horror is there but muffled, mouth full. When the pan is clean we scrape with spoons, then fingers, then Pike lifts the skillet and drinks the drippings straight, eyes closed, grease on his chin like war paint. He sets it down, exhales smoke-shaped breath. “I’m still empty,” he says, surprised. We all nod. Bellies distended, hearts racing, but the hollow is louder—like hunger upgraded, learned new language. Tadpole taps my knee, offers a handful of silver skin he’s peeled from inside the cap. Looks like lamella, gills, whatever. I take it, chew. Tastes metallic, intimate—like licking a battery that used to be a person. He watches my face, nods, satisfied, then curls under the table, humming fading to purr. Gran pours rainwater into the greasy pan, swirls, drinks. “Stock,” she says, defending herself to no one. I record: “Utilising full carcass—zero waste model.” My hand trembles so hard the word model becomes modem. Night edges in, or maybe morning; clocks died same day the internet did. Fire settles to red geography. I meant to save samples, label, store. Instead we ate the evidence, licked the bags. All that’s left is a skull-cap bowl drying by the flames, faint glow pulsing like it still remembers breath. I tell myself tomorrow we’ll be scientific—measure, test, wait. But my tongue keeps worrying the gap where I bit it, tasting iron mixed with maple, and I know I’ll be first out the door at first light, knife warm in my pocket, ready to see what else the world is willing to become.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is Chapter 2 of a post-apocalyptic survival horror story told through the notebook of Mab, an ex-mycologist. The chapter follows the group's second encounter with eating the spore mutants, deepening their relationship with this new food source. STRENGTHS: The voice is remarkably consistent and well-executed. Mab's character comes through in every sentence—the compulsive logging ("Effect—euphoric, pupil dilation, possible amphetamine analogue"), the self-aware irony, the way scientific notation keeps collapsing into appetite. The detail "the word model becomes modem" because her hand shakes is exactly the kind of specific, earned detail that distinguishes strong writing from competent writing. Character differentiation is excellent. Pike's kitchen-speak ("Medium rare's one-thirty-five, but this ain't beef, could be tree for all we know"), Gran June's practical horror ("We're farming them now"), and Tadpole's wordless communion with the food all feel distinct and consistent with their profiles. Gran June's line about her mama's Coca-Cola ham is a perfect cozy-horror beat—nostalgic, warm, immediately undercut by the wet angry eyes. The horror escalation is handled with real craft. The mycelial lace that "reaches for my boot, stops short, as if remembering manners" is genuinely unsettling without being overwrought. The progression from disgust to craving to something more troubling ("hunger upgraded, learned new language") follows the chapter plan's arc faithfully. The "licking a battery that used to be a person" line is one of the best in the chapter—concrete, disturbing, and it earns its metaphorical weight because it's grounded in physical sensation first. WEAKNESSES: Some metaphors don't fully land. "Outside smells different now, like the world turned its pocket inside out and revealed sweeter lining" is slightly strained—the pocket metaphor is a reach. "Fire settles to red geography" is evocative but borders on the kind of atmospheric filler that sounds good without meaning much. "Laughter smells of the meat; I taste it in the air" is a synesthetic leap that works conceptually but feels slightly forced in execution. The line "Gran pours rainwater into the greasy pan, swirls, drinks. 'Stock,' she says, defending herself to no one" is excellent, but the following "I record: 'Utilising full carcass—zero waste model'" feels like it's trying slightly too hard to be darkly funny—the joke is telegraphed. "Every brick in the hearth stands out like a tooth" is a bit of a cliché for altered-state perception. The chapter is slightly long for what it accomplishes—some of the middle section (the outdoor retrieval) could be tighter. The plan called for noting "fingertip-shaped growths inside the cap" but this doesn't appear explicitly, though the gills/lamella detail partially substitutes. The ending is strong but the penultimate paragraph ("I tell myself tomorrow we'll be scientific") is slightly on-the-nose in spelling out the theme of rationalization giving way to appetite—the chapter has already shown this without needing to state it. OVERALL: This is genuinely accomplished work. The voice is sustained, the horror is earned, the characters are vivid, and the cozy-horror tension is maintained throughout. The weaknesses are minor—occasional over-reach in metaphor, slight redundancy in theme-stating—but they don't significantly undermine what is a strong, confident piece of writing. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
2 Feb – pencil point broke, so I’m writing with the stub, licking the wood to darken the letters. The snow’s gone hard, crust you can walk on if you weigh less than hope. We weigh less every day. We’ve become butchers. Not hunters – butchers. There’s a difference: hunters chase, we wait with knives like kids at a birthday cake, ready for the slice that won’t wiggle back. Pike found the trick: heat the crowbar, stab, twist – the stem pops like a cork. He practised on a half-frozen Shamblecap by the outhouse, came back grinning, cheeks freckled with spores. “No bullets, no noise, no sharing,” he said, licking his lip where a fleck stuck. The fleck dissolved, his eyes got wide, he laughed once – sharp, surprised – then went quiet. I wrote: “Contact high via microparticle? Monitor for mania.” Then I ate the fleck that landed on my knuckle. Tasted like burnt toast and electricity. Wrote nothing after. Gran June’s turned the clothesline into a kill rack. Twine, actually – baling twine she rescued from the shed years ago, smelling of mouldering hay. Things hang there now, dripping slow grey tears that freeze into glass beads. Tadpole collects them in a tin, rattles like candy when he walks. I asked why. He shrugged, which means keep going, so I did. We work in shifts. Dawn: me and Pike trek the tree line, follow the glow – mycelium lights under the crust like runway lamps for the damned. We walk soft; snow holds our weight, creak complaints but no crack. When we spot a fruiting body we circle, sniff – truffle-garlic means edible, copper-penny means back away. I still don’t know what copper ones do; we left one standing, marked with red spray paint (last can, stolen from a road-crew truck). It waved as we left – or maybe the wind did, hard to tell which is which anymore. Noon: Gran covers us from the porch, rifle barrel resting on the rail she swaddled in old quilt so the metal won’t stick to skin. She counts shots fired: zero. Counts bodies hauled: seven today. Her lips move, prayers or math, same difference. Evening: butchery by lamplight. Pike’s knife work has grown surgical – he found a rhythm: slit the veil, hook two fingers under the gill plate, peel like deboning trout. The flesh steams, smells of Sunday roast nobody remembers. We strip, salt, hang. Salt is running low; we scrape the last from the bottom of a cardboard Morton's cylinder, crystals pink with someone’s blood – can’t recall whose. Tadpole’s job is fire and jars. He arranges mason jars – my abandoned canning hobby – in front of the hearth, warms them slow so they won’t crack when we pour grease. His face glows orange, eyes reflect twice – once in glass, once in reality. He labels with pictures: smiley for tasty, stick-figure with X-eyes for made-us-dizzy. No letters, but we learn his code fast. We eat as we work. “Quality control,” Pike says, tossing me a seared strip. Chew, swallow, wait for the kick: heart sprint, vision iris-wide, colours tasting louder. I noted first time, now I don’t bother. The notebook fills with recipes instead of warnings: - Sighmorel fritters: slice thin, dredge in Bisquick dust, fry in mutant lard. - Hearttruffle medallions: sear 90 seconds, rest 60, drizzle with Weepshroom reduction (tears + snow + pinch of stolen sugar). - Jerky: air-dry three days above stove, snap when it moans. I read them back and can’t tell if I’m scientist or prophet of a greasy new religion. Night three of the new routine, Gran caught me talking to the carcass. I was trimming silver skin, saying, “Sorry, sorry,” under my breath. She laid a hand on my shoulder – glove sticky – and told me, “Save sorry for the living. These ain’t that.” Her voice cracked on “living,” though, so the advice felt borrowed. Pike sleeps with a skillet now. Cast iron, keeps it under the cot like a teddy bear. Claims cold metal helps him dream recipes. This morning he woke up screaming “Deglaze!” and nobody laughed. We’re all tasting things that aren’t there – garlic in the snow, maple in the gun oil. I found Tadpole licking the windowsill yesterday, said it tasted “bright.” I licked after; just tasted like frost and old paint, but I kept at it anyway. Salt gone. We tried snowpack fermentation – pack raw slices in drift, weight with rocks, wait. Three days: smells like cider, texture of pear. We gorged. No cramps. I waited for science to feel victorious; mostly felt impatient for the next batch. Bullets: nine left. Gran polishes them each dusk, lining them like chocolate bars you’re saving for heartbreak. I asked who gets them. She answered, “Whatever stops tasting like food.” Raiders – distant ones, we think. Tracks found: boot prints circling our red-sprayed copper-penny Shamblecap, boot prints leaving too. They didn’t touch it. Means they know the code, or they’re learning. We doubled the twine line, added noise-makers: empty cans, broken plates, a child’s rattle Tadpole found in the attic. At night the clatter is lullaby. I dream of grocery aisles where everything breathes but no one minds you carving. Hunger hasn’t left; it’s shape-shifted. Now it’s a chef standing over us, tapping the counter: “Again, faster, tenderer.” We obey. Belly distended, cheeks filling, ribs still showing – contradiction made flesh. I weigh myself on the bathroom scale we dragged from the cellar: same as last week. Where does it go? Into the glow under my skin? Into the dreams where mushrooms talk legal disclaimers? Today I cut one open and found a tooth – human, molar, cavity black. I almost dropped the knife. Pike looked, shrugged: “Calcium’s calcium,” and popped the slice in his mouth. I didn’t write that down. Instead I pocketed the tooth. It clicks against the pencil when I walk, metronome for the next course. We are ranchers of the formerly human, diners on disaster, and the menu is expanding faster than our ability to feel ashamed. I keep the notebook because paper used to be trees, and trees used to be us, and us used to be sorry. Not anymore. Outside, something new pokes through the snow—glow pink, smells of bubblegum and iron. I’m already tying my boots, knife between teeth, tasting tomorrow.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of post-apocalyptic horror-cozy writing that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Mab's voice is consistently rendered—the mycologist-turned-scrounger who catalogs everything, whose scientific impulse has been colonized by appetite. The notebook format works well: the recipe list mid-chapter ("Sighmorel fritters," "Hearttruffle medallions") is genuinely clever and disturbing, showing the slide from warning-writer to prophet of consumption. Pike's "Deglaze!" nightmare is a small perfect detail. Gran June's "Save sorry for the living. These ain't that" with the cracked voice is well-executed—the crack doing the emotional work the words deny. Tadpole's jar-labeling system (smiley vs. X-eyes) is consistent with his non-verbal characterization and genuinely charming in a queasy way. METAPHOR AND POETRY: The writing has a high density of figurative language, and most of it lands. "Mycelium lights under the crust like runway lamps for the damned" is earned. "Bullets like chocolate bars you're saving for heartbreak" is excellent. "Hunger hasn't left; it's shape-shifted. Now it's a chef standing over us, tapping the counter" is thematically resonant and not merely decorative. However, there are moments where the riffing strains: "colours tasting louder" is a bit of a reach, and "paper used to be trees, and trees used to be us, and us used to be sorry" is the kind of chain-logic that sounds profound but dissolves under scrutiny—it's the chapter's weakest moment, a closing paragraph that tries too hard to be aphoristic. "Tasting tomorrow" as a final line is also slightly too neat, too writerly. FOLLOWING THE PLAN: The chapter plan called for: Gran June baiting with honey (absent—replaced with the clothesline kill rack), gas pockets and nerve-like filaments noted (partially present in the general butchery description but not specifically), screaming through gills (absent), grilling fillets with canned lemon pepper (absent—replaced with more general cooking), Pike filming on dead phone (absent), "second kill easier, third feels like habit" (present thematically). The chapter diverges significantly from the specific plan details—the honey bait, the lemon pepper, the phone filming are all dropped. What replaces them is coherent and good, but the plan's specific texture is largely abandoned. The chapter does capture the "hunt becomes habit" arc, which is the plan's core. HORROR AND COZY BALANCE: The tooth in the flesh is the chapter's best horror beat—understated, Pike's "Calcium's calcium" response perfectly calibrating the group's moral slide. The copper-penny Shamblecap "waving" is nicely ambiguous. The horror is well-earned rather than gratuitous. DIALOGUE: Sparse but effective. Gran June's lines are the strongest. Pike's "Deglaze!" is more action than dialogue. The dialogue-to-narration ratio is appropriate for the notebook format. WEAKNESSES: The closing paragraph is the chapter's most amateurish moment—the "paper used to be trees" chain feels like a student writer reaching for profundity. The "tasting tomorrow" closer is a cliché of the "poetic ending" variety. Some of the sensory details pile up without quite landing (the "colours tasting louder" synesthesia is asserted rather than felt). The chapter is slightly long for what it accomplishes—the middle section with the shift descriptions (dawn/noon/evening) is competent but less vivid than the chapter's best moments. OVERALL: A genuinely accomplished piece of genre-bending horror writing with a strong, consistent voice, good character work, and mostly earned figurative language. The deviations from the chapter plan are significant but the replacement material is largely of equal or better quality. The ending slightly overshoots. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 10 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
5 Feb – pencil snapped clean, so I’m scratching with the metal ferrule. Paper’s greased; words skate. My hand glows faint under the skin, veins like blue Christmas lights somebody forgot to unplug. Three sets of boot prints in the drift, heading in. None heading out. They came while the sun was a white smear behind slate, when the wind howls loud enough to cover the sound of a round being chambered. Gran saw them first—rifle scope, squint, curse. “Living,” she said, which used to mean relief. Now it means appetite of a different shape. She counted: one limping, one tall, one bleeding into the snow. She didn’t shoot. Said the limper’s coat was too thin, blood trail too bright. “Might be decent,” she muttered. Decent gets you killed slower. We met them at the porch rail, guns up, pans behind us still hissing with lunch. The smell drifted—truffle-garlic-sweet—hit them like a slap. Their knees folded all at once, face-first into the drift. Didn’t get up. We dragged them in, checked pockets: two shells, one Hershey bar bloomed white, a photo of a dog folded so many times the creases have holes. They thawed by the hearth, mouths working like fish. Pike spooned broth—Shamblecap stock, thick as gravy. First swallow lifts their eyelids; second brings tears; third and they’re animals, grabbing the pot edges. Tall one tries to say grace, forgets the words, keeps going with “uh-men, uh-men,” until the bowl’s empty. I write: “Cross-addiction potential high.” Names: Tall is Jonah, limper is Luisa, bleeder is just Boy—maybe sixteen, cheeks still soft with baby fat gone grey. Luisa’s foot is a frost-bitten club, toes black pearls. She doesn’t flinch when Gran unwraps it, which is worse than screaming. Jonah keeps staring at the empty pot like it might refill if he believes hard enough. Boy’s eyes skate past us, fix on the hanging racks—strips drying, gills curling like ugly ribbons. His tongue touches his lip. Pike asks the question we never agreed on: “You hungry for more?” Jonah answers by dropping to his knees, not prayer, begging. Luisa grips my wrist, nails cracked and cold. “Trade,” she rasps. “We got metal, got ammo, got—” coughs, spits pink. I feel her pulse jumping against my thumb, fast as a sparrow’s. Alive, still alive. Gran lifts a smoked strip, waves it like incense. “One bite buys honesty. Who you running from?” Jonah: “Our own.” Vague, but scope widens—other camps, south, where the river bends. They ran out of canned goods, started on rats, then on each other. He doesn’t say the last noun, we hear it anyway. Luisa’s eyes flick to the red-sprayed mutant still standing in the yard—our warning marker. “You cultivate,” she whispers, reverent, terrified. I nod, feel like a priest confessing the altar is edible. Boy speaks first actual sentence: “Teach us, we work.” Voice breaks on work, comes out chirp. Evening splits us. Pike and Jonah haul snow to boil, whispering recipes—Jonah knows spices we lost: rosemary, smoked paprika. His pupils widen as Pike describes the glaze you get from Weepshroom reduction. I see the future: spice raid, flavour arms race, kingdoms built on who controls the salt. Luisa limps to the window, watches the pink-glow newfruit we haven’t harvested. “It sings,” she says. I tell her it’s wind. She shakes her head, hair crusted with old ice. “Sings names.” I don’t ask whose. Boy stays by the hearth, stoking, feeding strips of dried cap into his mouth slow as cigarettes. Each chew makes him lighter; colour crawls back to his cheeks like sunset. When he thinks no one watches he licks the grease off his fingers, then the finger itself, teeth testing skin. I pretend not to see, write: “Cues of auto-cannibal ideation—monitor.” But my pencil lingers, because hunger knows hunger, and the line between watcher and watched is thinner than frost on a knife. Night: we bunk them in the pantry—no lock, just trust seasoned with fatigue. Gran keeps first watch, rifle across knees, humming hymns she murders with minor chords. I lie on the kitchen floor, head against cast-iron oven still warm. Heart races, won’t slow. Images strobe: tooth in meat, Luisa’s black toes, Boy’s tongue on himself. I taste maple in my saliva, wonder if I’m exhaling spores, if the room is colonising me while I breathe. A scream—short, clipped like bad audio. I’m up, knife out, slipping on grease. Pantry door open, Jonah on floor holding his hand, blood threading between knuckles. Boy backs away, eyes wide, mouth red. Not his blood. Jonah gasps: “Bit me—fucking bit me—” Luisa cradles the boy, whispers Spanish too fast for my rust. His pupils are dinner plates, lips smack wet. Pike storms in, skillet raised like shield. Gran clicks safety off. Stand-off: five heartbeats, fire ticking, blood dripping on biscuit-tin floor. Boy’s tongue darts, cleans Jonah’s blood off his chin. Sound is obscene, intimate. I taste copper in the air—then realise it’s in my mouth; I bit through my cheek sometime. Gran lowers rifle first. “Chain him or teach him, pick fast.” Luisa hugs tighter, foot dragging useless. No one looks at the hanging strips, but we all feel them calling, taunting: plenty, plenty. I step forward, show empty palms. “Hunger hijacks,” I say, voice strange, sermon-calm. “We’ve all chewed our own cheeks.” I offer a strip of jerky. Boy’s eyes flick—predator math. He takes it, tears with side teeth, never breaks eye contact. Jonah wraps his hand in dishcloth, glare hot enough to melt snow. Decision made: tomorrow we show them the kill line, the twine, the silent pop of hot iron through stem. If they work, they eat. If they eat—well, we’ll see what grows. Back in main room Pike stares at skillet like it betrayed him. “We ain’t unique anymore,” he mutters. I want to argue but the maple smell on his breath answers for me. We are a diner that’s franchising horror, and the customers are bringing their own teeth. I sit to write, hand shaking so hard ferrule skitters. Margin note: “Bite mark—human. No infection observed—yet.” Another note, smaller: “He liked it.” I underline twice, ink cuts paper. Wind rattles the cans outside—our alarm, our lullaby. Somewhere in the dark the red-sprayed copper-penny Shamblecap still stands, boot prints around it now doubled. I listen for singing, hear only my stomach answering in fluent want. Tomorrow we’ll teach, or we’ll chain, or we’ll be the next course. Pan sizzles on coals, keeping warm, ready, always ready.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely impressive piece of post-apocalyptic horror writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Mab's voice is consistently rendered—the notebook conceit works, the ferrule-scratching detail is earned, the mycologist's clinical notation ("Cross-addiction potential high," "Cues of auto-cannibal ideation—monitor") creates exactly the right tension between scientific detachment and visceral horror. The voice never slips into generic first-person narration; it maintains the specific texture of someone who processes terror through taxonomy. Pike, Gran June, and Tadpole are absent here (Tadpole entirely, Gran briefly), which is appropriate given the chapter's focus on newcomers, but Pike's "We ain't unique anymore" lands with genuine weight. Gran's "Chain him or teach him, pick fast" is perfectly in character—economical, pragmatic, slightly biblical. The newcomers (Jonah, Luisa, Boy) are introduced with enough specificity to feel real: the dog photo with crease-holes, Luisa's black-pearl toes, Boy's chirped "work." The bite scene is the chapter's centerpiece and it earns its horror—the detail of Boy licking Jonah's blood off his chin while making eye contact is genuinely disturbing and not gratuitous. FOLLOWING THE PLAN: The chapter plan called for: strangers arriving, leader puking then asking for seconds, trade of rifle rounds for smoked haunch, showing the greenhouse corpse-farm, Gran counting bullets and saying "We're ranchers now." The chapter delivers the strangers, the trade negotiation, the food-as-currency dynamic, and Gran's rancher sensibility—but the specific "leader pukes then asks for seconds" beat is replaced by the more interesting bite incident, and the greenhouse corpse-farm showing is deferred to tomorrow. The "We're ranchers now, Lord help us" line doesn't appear. These are meaningful deviations, though the substitutions are generally improvements or at least lateral moves. PROSE QUALITY: The writing is mostly excellent. "Decent gets you killed slower" is a perfect line. "Truffle-garlic-sweet—hit them like a slap" works. "Kingdoms built on who controls the salt" is sharp. The maple-smell-as-infection-marker is well-seeded. However, there are moments where the metaphor-density becomes slightly strained: "mouth working like fish" is cliché, "heart races, won't slow" is flat, "images strobe" is a bit generic. "Diner that's franchising horror" is clever but slightly too clever—it calls attention to itself. The "fluent want" ending is good. FORCED POETRY: Some metaphors land imperfectly. "Pupils are dinner plates" is a cliché. "Colour crawls back to his cheeks like sunset" is slightly overwrought. "Blood threading between knuckles" is good. "Tongue touches his lip" is effective restraint. The ratio of successful to unsuccessful metaphors is favorable—maybe 80/20—which is strong for this density of figurative language. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is generally strong and character-specific. "Sings names" from Luisa is excellent. "Teach us, we work" with the voice breaking on "work" is well-observed. "You cultivate" as reverent/terrified is perfect. Jonah's failed grace ("uh-men, uh-men") is a wonderful detail. The weakest dialogue moment is Mab's "Hunger hijacks" speech, which feels slightly too composed for the moment—a bit sermon-ready. SHOW VS. TELL: The chapter mostly shows rather than tells, but "I feel like a priest confessing the altar is edible" is telling-with-metaphor rather than showing. The "future" paragraph (spice raid, flavour arms race) is Mab speculating, which is in-character but slightly expository. COHERENCE: The chapter is coherent and well-paced. The escalation from arrival to feeding to trade negotiation to bite incident to uneasy resolution is logical and well-structured. DARKNESS/LIGHTNESS: The darkness is well-earned. The bite scene doesn't feel gratuitous; it emerges naturally from the starvation logic established throughout. The "plenty, plenty" from the hanging strips is effective. The chapter maintains the cozy-horror balance—warmth of fire and food against the horror of what the food is and what hunger does to people. OVERALL: This is a high-quality chapter with a strong, consistent voice, effective horror, and mostly successful prose. Its deviations from the plan are minor and often improvements. The main weaknesses are occasional clichéd metaphors and one slightly over-composed speech. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
9 Feb – the pencil is down to a nub I pinch with pliers. Paper smells of onion snow and hot iron; I keep wiping grease off the page so words can stick. We found the Hearttruffle at dawn, poking through the crust like a bruised knuckle. Humanoid only in outline: shoulders, curve of spine, but the skin is cracked walnut, veins glowing rose-gold under the fissures. It was still breathing – a see-saw motion that hissed spore-dust from fissures every exhale. Smell: filet left in a hot car with a bouquet of roses. My stomach answered before my brain. Jonah wanted to shoot it. One round, stem severed, done. Gran lifted a hand. “Waste of lead. Cut it living, it bleeds sweeter.” She said it flat, like reading weather. I felt the world tilt: we weren’t scavengers anymore – we were ranchers discussing slaughter technique. Luisa sat on the sled, foot blacker every day, eyes brighter. She kept whispering to it – Spanish lullabies. Maybe she thought pain recognises pain. The thing didn’t answer, but the glowing pulse quickened when she hummed. We set up quick: tarp, knives, pots heated on a portable burner we found in the cellar. Pike whetted his cleaver the whole walk, steel singing. Sparks landed on the snow, hissed, died. Tadpole arranged mason jars in a half-moon, labels already in his head – picture of heart with teeth, smiley face underneath. No one told him what we’d harvest; he already knew. I did the incision. Tip of the Mora between fourth and fifth rib – or where ribs would be. The tissue parted like hot dough, let out a sigh that smelled of iron and maple. No scream, just a drop in pressure, like opening a soda bottle. Blood – if that’s blood – welled thick, dark, then clarified to demi-glace. It steamed, almost too pretty to waste. I caught the first pour in a jar, set it aside to reduce. Notes flew: “Viscosity 15 cP, aroma volatiles – maltol, vanillin, cadaverine?” I tasted mid-sentence – sweet, round, finish of rare beef. My knees went loose; I locked them, kept cutting. Pike peeled the plate back, revealed the core: a fist-sized knot of flesh beating slow, each throb sending marbled colour through the surrounding meat. He looked at me, pupils blown. “That’s the money,” he said, voice cracked high. Cleaver lifted, paused – theatre habit, or last shred of decency – then came down. The beat stopped; the lodge went silent, like every fire in the county held its breath. We worked fast after that. Strips, medallions, cubes for stew. The flesh cooled, firmed, became actual meat – no trace of gill or stem. Gran bagged portions in freezer paper we tore from a long-dead refrigerator, writing weights with a sharpie that kept clogging. She hummed too, but hers was hymns, minor keys, words about blood everlasting. Luisa asked for the heart-knot. We gave it. She held it in mittened palms, rocked. Later she’ll sear it for just thirty seconds each side, eat it alone by the woodshed door. She’ll say it tasted like every Christmas she’ll never see again. She’ll lick the plate, cry, then sleep twelve hours straight and wake with toes pinker than they’ve been in weeks. I’ll pretend I didn’t watch. Boy stayed inside, chained ankle to stove leg – his choice, he insisted, eyes wild. Chain length lets him reach the hearth, the chopping block, the hanging billets. He’s learning: slices thin, seasons with ash, asks if smoke is holy. We tell him yes because the word keeps him calm. We rendered the “blood” – two hours low simmer, skimming grey foam that smelled of roses gone sour. End product: one pint of glossy glaze, colour of old port. I dipped a fingertip, touched tongue – flavour exploded: iron, caramel, something floral I still can’t name. My heart rabbit-thumped; vision tunneled then widened to impossible clarity. I saw dust motes as constellations, heard Gran’s heartbeat from across the room. Wrote: “Psychoactive – dose small, titrate.” Underlined three times, hand shaking. Supper: medallions seared ninety seconds, rested sixty, drizzle of heart-reduction. Table silent except for chewing, small moans. Outside wind slams shutters; inside we chew, swallow, glow. I feel capillaries open, skin warm, scalp prickle. My tongue maps the roof of my mouth like it’s new property. I catch Jonah watching me, same awe he once reserved for angels. We are chewing on the core of something that used to think, maybe, and none of us care because it tastes like forgiveness. After, we sit around the hearth, bellies round, eyes shining. No one speaks. The lodge smells like a steakhouse in February, windows fogged, snow piled outside. For a minute it feels like before – like maybe the world is just late, not ended. Then I see the chain around Boy’s ankle gleam with grease, and the illusion folds. Gran breaks the quiet: “We need more.” She doesn’t specify more what – meat, glaze, heartbeats – we all understand. She’s already scanning windows, calculating how many days until the next one fruits. Her finger taps the rifle stock, counting. I meant to save samples for microscope, culture, safety. Instead I licked the plate clean, tongue sweeping up molecules of whatever soul once lived here. Data feels abstract when flavour this loud is singing in your blood. Bedtime: I write by firelight, hand unsteady. Margin smear: “No adverse effect – unless euphoria is adverse.” Another note, smaller: “Harvest sustainable if we keep them alive while cutting.” The sentence scares me, but not enough to cross it out. I taste metal – maybe the pencil, maybe the future. Heart still racing, I listen to the lodge breathe: rafters settling, Tadpole humming, Boy’s chain clinking as he dreams. Somewhere outside something pink and knuckled is pushing through snow, learning our names, practising the pulse we’ll stop for dinner. I should fear it. I’m too full to move. The glaze has dried on my lips like lipstick. I lick it again, and again, and sleep comes greasy and sweet.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of post-apocalyptic horror-adjacent writing that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Mab's voice is well-maintained throughout. The mycologist's instinct to take notes mid-slaughter ("Viscosity 15 cP, aroma volatiles – maltol, vanillin, cadaverine?") is exactly right for the character profile, and the self-aware horror of licking the plate while still writing scientific observations is the chapter's central tension, well-executed. The detail of tasting mid-sentence and her knees going loose is genuinely good. Pike's "That's the money" with the cracked-high voice is economical and effective. Gran June's "Waste of lead. Cut it living, it bleeds sweeter" is chilling and consistent with her profile. However, there are some character consistency issues. The chapter introduces several characters not in the original profiles: Jonah, Luisa, and "Boy" (chained to the stove). These feel like additions that weren't in the plan or profiles. Luisa's subplot (blackening foot, Spanish lullabies, eating the heart-knot alone) is actually quite affecting, but it's unexplained who these people are. "Boy" chained to the stove leg is disturbing and interesting but also unexplained. This creates coherence problems — the reader of this chapter in isolation doesn't know who these people are or how they arrived. PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter plan called for: Pike waking chewing his knuckle, Weepshroom syrup reduced to caramel, Mab smoothing it on her lips like lipstick, Tadpole's drawing of stick figures, and urine smelling like maple. The actual chapter diverges significantly — it's about hunting and butchering a "Hearttruffle," a new creature not mentioned in the plan. The lipstick detail appears (glaze on lips) but in a different context. The maple smell appears (in the blood/sap). Tadpole's mason jar arrangement is present. But the core plan elements (Pike's knuckle-chewing, Weepshroom caramel, the drawing) are absent. This is a notable deviation. PROSE QUALITY: The prose is generally strong. "The tissue parted like hot dough, let out a sigh that smelled of iron and maple" is excellent. "Blood – if that's blood – welled thick, dark, then clarified to demi-glace" is precise and horrifying in the right way. The psychoactive glaze sequence ("I saw dust motes as constellations, heard Gran's heartbeat from across the room") is effective without being overwrought. FORCED POETRY/PURPLE PROSE: There are some moments where the writing reaches too hard. "We are chewing on the core of something that used to think, maybe, and none of us care because it tastes like forgiveness" — "tastes like forgiveness" is the kind of metaphor that sounds profound but doesn't quite land on examination. "Somewhere outside something pink and knuckled is pushing through snow, learning our names, practising the pulse we'll stop for dinner" — this is better, genuinely eerie. "Sleep comes greasy and sweet" is a good closing image. The Jonah-watching-with-angel-awe line is a bit much. HORROR AND COZY TENSION: The chapter does well at the cozy-horror balance. The steakhouse smell, fogged windows, round bellies, and then the chain gleaming with grease — that's the prompt's core tension executed well. The "illusion folds" moment is earned. DIALOGUE: Sparse but mostly effective. Gran's lines are the best. The dialogue doesn't overstay its welcome. TELL-DON'T-SHOW: Luisa's Christmas memory is told rather than shown ("She'll say it tasted like every Christmas she'll never see again") — but this is filtered through Mab's anticipatory narration, which is a legitimate technique. The psychoactive effects are shown through specific sensory detail rather than just stated. COHERENCE: The unexplained new characters (Jonah, Luisa, Boy) create genuine confusion. Who is Boy? Why is he chained? Why is Luisa there? This feels like the model invented characters mid-chapter without grounding them. CREATIVITY: The Hearttruffle as a creature is inventive. The demi-glace blood, the psychoactive reduction, the heart-knot — these are creative and specific. The chapter earns its darkness. OVERALL: A chapter with genuine strengths in voice, sensory detail, and tonal control, but weakened by significant deviation from the chapter plan, introduction of unexplained characters, and occasional overreach in the prose. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 10 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 7 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 11 Followed Chapter Plan: 7 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
12 Feb – the stub is gone; I write with a charred matchstick dipped in heart-glaze. Paper drinks it brown. My letters smell like iron and roses when they dry. We overate. Not quantity – quality. The glaze, the heart, the hope. Now the bill arrives. It started before dawn: cramps folding me like bad origami. I staggered to the porch, vomited neon pink that hissed through snow, left a hole you could see the dirt in. Pike followed, knelt beside me, sweat beading though the air bit twenty below. His pupils pulsed – dilate, contract, dilate – like someone flicking a light switch that isn’t hooked to a bulb. He grinned, teeth purple. “Tastes even coming up,” he said, and licked his lip. I wanted to hit him, couldn’t raise my arm. Inside, Boy’s chain clattered. He was yelling numbers – 37, 22, 9 – over and over, voice cracking. When I stumbled in he held his belly, skin moving under the shirt like mice under a rug. Gran pressed her ear to him, listened, stepped back slow. “Something’s knocking from the inside,” she whispered. Not metaphor – a rhythmic tap, soft, deliberate. Luisa sat by the window foot propped on a stool, black toes now iridescent, skin splitting to show gill-like lamellae that fluttered when she breathed. She didn’t speak, just hummed the lullaby backwards, notes falling instead of rising. The smell around her: old honey left in a hot hive. I wrote: “Conversion underway – pain nil, euphoria high,” then the matchstick snapped and I couldn’t find another. Jonah’s reaction hit next. He’d hoarded half a jar of reduction, drank it straight while we slept. Now he knelt before the hearth, arms out, palms up, eyes rolled white. Every exhale released spore dust that sparkled in firelight. He spoke in overlapping voices – his, and something softer, choral: “We thank you for the harvesting, we thank you for the host.” Pike recorded on dead phone, narration slurred: “Subject exhibiting… ecclesiastical overflow.” Tadpole was the calm eye. He moved jar to jar, tightening lids, sealing new flesh we couldn’t stomach to look at. His pupils were huge but even, skin taking on faint glow like moon through thin cloud. He touched my cheek, left a handprint that tingled, warm. I tasted maple for an hour wherever his fingers had been. Then the raiders came back – three, same number, different shapes. Guns first, hunger second. They kicked the door at first light, rifles scope-scoped on us like we were exhibits. Leader – big, beard like burnt wire – saw the hanging strips, the glaze pot still warm, and his face collapsed into want. “Drop the meat,” he barked. Pike laughed, high and cracked: “Can’t drop what’s already inside.” Gran fired once – warning into the ceiling. Plaster snowed over raiders. They didn’t flinch, just stepped forward, nostrils flaring like hounds. The leader’s eyes found Luisa’s foot-gills, pupils dilated in unmistakable craving. “You’re farming them,” he breathed, reverent. “Share crop.” Violence should have been quick but hunger slowed it. One raider lowered rifle, reached for a strip, bit. Eyes rolled back, knees hit boards. Another grabbed skillet, scooped glaze bare-handed, shoved it into his mouth until it dripped from chin like port-wine stain. Third swung lead pipe at Pike; Pike took the hit on shoulder, didn’t drop ladle. He backhanded raider with hot iron, smell of scorched hair, sizzle. Man fell, still chewing air. Leader lifted rifle to Gran. Jonah stepped between, arms wide, spores still streaming from his mouth. “Eat,” he offered, voice layered. Raider pulled trigger – click. Frozen firing pin or empty chamber, same result. Jonah pressed a handful of raw medallion into the man’s mouth, forced jaw closed with both thumbs. Leader bit down reflex, eyes widening, glaze running from nose. He chewed, sobbed, chewed. The fight ended in swallowing sounds. All three raiders on floor, eating strips we threw, glaze we ladled. They wept while they ate, begged for more between curses. We fed them until their stomachs domed, until they vomited pink, then fed them again. At some point rifles were kicked aside, forgotten like toys outgrown. I watched myself ladle, felt separate, scientist and accomplice. Notes appeared on the wall in matchstick ink: “Addiction threshold – single bite. Compulsion overrides self-preservation.” My handwriting, not my choice. Arm kept moving. When they couldn’t eat anymore they curled around the hearth, hands cradling bellies, humming in B-flat. Tadpole joined, harmony drifting. The sound was lullaby, funeral, factory line. Gran sat on the table, rifle across knees, eyes empty. “We’re the kitchen now,” she said. “Whole damn world’s lining up for seconds.” Boy’s chain had gone slack. Conversion complete: skin split along ribs, revealing delicate gill fans that fluttered with each breath. He breathed spores into the raider’s faces; they inhaled like it was incense. No one reached for keys to unlock him. Maybe he unlocked himself into something we don’t chain. Pike stood over the skillet, adding scraped flesh, stirring, tears carving clean lines through grease on his cheeks. “I can’t stop tasting,” he whispered. “Even the air is dinner.” I touched his arm; muscle jumped under skin like something caged that knows the door is paper. Midday: we voted without voting—no ropes, no locks. Raiders stayed, became indentured stomachs. They work now: haul snow, chop wood, clean strips. Payment: bite per hour, glaze on Sunday. They thank us, throats raw. I keep waiting for guilt; instead I feel efficient. Luisa walked today—foot shed black shell, new tissue underneath peach-soft, veined rose. She left the pieces in a neat pile, like a snake that apologises. Where she steps, floorboards glow faint, fade by morning. She doesn’t talk, but her humming guides the raiders— they follow her around the yard like ducklings, weeding the smaller fruits she points at. Night now. The hearth is never cold; something’s always rendering. Boy sleeps chained mostly from habit— the chain’s become jewellery, anklet of rust. My own gut ripples, not cramp, more like conversation. I rest the matchstick, lick the last word shut. Outside the cans rattle— wind or warning, hard to care. I taste iron in every exhale, feel something knock politely beneath my ribs. Answer or don’t— dinner bell rings either way.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely ambitious piece of post-apocalyptic horror-cozy writing that largely succeeds on its own terms, though with some notable weaknesses worth examining carefully. **Strengths:** The voice is consistently Mab's—scientific, detached, self-aware about her own complicity ("I watched myself ladle, felt separate, scientist and accomplice"). The notebook conceit is maintained well: the matchstick ink detail, the wall-writing she doesn't remember choosing, the snapped stub. These feel earned rather than decorative. The horror escalates organically. The chapter moves from physical illness (the neon pink vomit hissing through snow) to social collapse (raiders becoming indentured stomachs) to something more existential—the quiet domestication of violence into routine. "Payment: bite per hour, glaze on Sunday" is genuinely chilling in its bureaucratic normalcy. The characters are largely consistent with their profiles. Pike's cook-brain persisting through crisis ("You wanna eat it, call me—bland and I'll haunt you" energy is present in "Can't drop what's already inside"). Gran's pragmatic fatalism ("We're the kitchen now") rings true. Tadpole's calm, tactile presence—tightening lids, touching Mab's cheek—is exactly right. The chapter plan called for raiders returning, Brother Cal's death, and the "Keepers" dynamic. The chapter diverges significantly: there's no Brother Cal, no gut-shot, no rendering of a named character. Instead we get a more diffuse raider confrontation. This is a meaningful deviation from the plan, though the chapter substitutes its own coherent logic. **Weaknesses:** The chapter introduces several new named characters—Boy, Luisa, Jonah—who don't appear in the character profiles and weren't in the chapter plan. This creates coherence problems. Who is Boy? Who is Luisa? Who is Jonah? The reader is dropped into relationships with no grounding. This feels like the model either forgot the established cast or improvised new ones without justification. It's a significant structural flaw. Some metaphors don't land. "Pupils pulsed – dilate, contract, dilate – like someone flicking a light switch that isn't hooked to a bulb" is overwrought and slightly incoherent as an image. "Skin moving under the shirt like mice under a rug" is effective; "beard like burnt wire" is fine but generic. "Conversion underway – pain nil, euphoria high" works as notebook voice. But "bad origami" for cramps is a bit too cute. The Jonah scene—"ecclesiastical overflow," the choral voices, the spore-streaming—tips into purple territory. It's trying to do too much at once: religious ecstasy, body horror, comedy, and tragedy simultaneously, and the tonal juggling becomes muddled. The dialogue is sparse but mostly functional. "Can't drop what's already inside" is the standout line. "We're the kitchen now. Whole damn world's lining up for seconds" is good Gran. Some of Pike's lines feel slightly too writerly for a man in crisis. The ending is strong—"dinner bell rings either way" closes the chapter with appropriate dread without over-explaining. The "something knock politely beneath my ribs" is effective body horror. The chapter is somewhat longer than the plan's wobbling structure suggests, and the density of incident (illness, raiders, conversion, new characters, social reorganization) makes it feel slightly overstuffed. The cozy-horror balance is maintained but the "cozy" elements are thinner here than in earlier chapters presumably were. **Overall:** A chapter with real craft and genuine moments of horror, undermined by unexplained new characters, occasional purple excess, and meaningful deviation from the established plan. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 11 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 9 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 8 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 9 Followed Chapter Plan: 7 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
15 Feb – writing with a sliver of charcoal from the hearth. Flakes fall into the crease, mixing with grease; words feel temporary, like snow that remembers being smoke. Brother Cal arrived at twilight, hands up, coat inside-out to show no weapons. Face kind in the way that expects beatings but keeps showing up anyway. Behind him the pink-glow orchard of newfruit swayed, humming low B-flat we’ve all stopped noticing. He walked between them untouched, like something already consecrated. We met him cautious—rifles low, pans hot. He smiled, asked for shelter, not food. Said he’d eat “whatever Heaven provides,” then glanced at the hanging strips and his pupils dilated like cathedral doors. I marked: “Faith overlay – possible mitigation of addiction?” Charcoal snapped; I licked dust, tasted altar bread and ash. Jonah and the raiders—now dubbed the “Seconds” because they take them—dragged a table, set a place for five. Cal insisted on grace. We bowed heads more from exhaustion than reverence. His voice filled the lodge: “Bless this bounty, born of us, returning to us.” Pike muttered “Amen” around a mouthful, grease christening his chin. Supper: seared heart medallions, drizzle of reduction thinned with snowmelt, side of pickled gills Gran brined in leftover apple-cider vinegar. Cal chewed slow, eyes closed, tears sliding unashamed. “Communion,” he whispered. No one corrected him. After plates, he asked to see the orchard. We led him, lanterns swinging, snow creaking under new weight—our boots plus the weight of decision. The newfruit bodies pulsed, pink light syncing with his heartbeat I swear. He touched one cap, gentle as blessing a baby. It leaned into his palm. Spores rose, haloed his head. He inhaled, smiled beatific, said, “They are ready to give themselves.” I felt the words burrow, sprout something like relief. Night: Cal volunteered for dish duty—washed pans with snow, dried them over coals, humming hymns in Latin he half-remembered. I sat opposite, charcoal poised. Asked why he came. He told: his flock south of the river ate everything canned, then each other, then the walls of the church itself. “We prayed for manna,” he said, scrubbing a plate until the iron shone. “ Heaven answered in mushrooms wearing our faces.” He looked up, eyes reflecting fire. “I’m here to bless the harvest, or be it.” Silence grew teeth. No one reassured him. Eventually I nodded, like permission is a thing I own. Next dawn he helped us reap. We showed the hot-crowbar method—poke, twist, pop. He winced at the hiss but did it, tears mixing with sweat. Each fall he murmured a name—someone from his parish, I guess—so the thing dies remembered. Productivity rose; the Seconds worked harder under his gentle thank-yous than under our ladles. Lunch: raw slivers dipped in heart glaze, altar-style. Cal served on a cracked communion plate we found in the cellar. We knelt without being asked. Tongues met meat, eyes rolled; somewhere a dog barked miles away and nobody cared. I felt the glaze travel, lighting candles in my veins. For a moment the lodge smelled of incense instead of sweat. Boy’s chain came off—Cal asked, we agreed. Skin at his ankle raw, gills there fluttering like small hands. Cal anointed the wound with reduction, said prayer. Boy didn’t speak but the gills slowed, seemed to listen. That night Boy slept free, curled beside Cal like parishioner beside confessor. Evening service: Cal insisted. We arranged logs as pews. He preached on loaves and fishes, on bodies broken, on life poured out for friends. Every sentence tasted of what we’d eaten. When he lifted a strip of dried heart, tore it, passed it, we chewed slow, tears sliding. “This is my body,” he said, and meant it literally. I waited for sacrilege to sting; instead I felt forgiven by something that didn’t care about sin, only supper. He asked for volunteers to “return the gift.” Silence thick. Then Jonah stepped forward, eyes bright from spore-dust and revelation. “I’ve tasted Heaven,” he said. “Might as well go whole.” Cal placed hands on him, thumbs pressing temples, whispering absolution. I felt time tilt, history collapsing into one long table. Plan shaped quick: Jonah would be the offering, partial, surgical. Heart-knot removed while he lived, shared among all, his flesh kept growing if fed. Medical madness, but my mouth watered even as my brain screamed. I wrote: “Ethical collapse – document only.” Charcoal trembled. Day of: kitchen became chapel. Table scrubbed white, knives boiled, reduction simmering like incense. Jonah lay shirtless, smiling, pupils blown wide. No anaesthetic but the glaze we fed him by spoon. Cal cut—slow, prayer between each incision. Blood welled, clarified, we caught every drop. Jonah grunted once, then hummed with Tadpole’s B-flat, voice layering, chord building. The knot came free—fist-sized, pulsing weak. Cal lifted it, eyes shining wet. “Behold,” he said, voice cracked. We ate. First bite: velvet iron, flowers left in a casket, sweetness after. I saw colours that don’t have names, felt my heart sync with every other heart in the room. For a moment the lodge breathed as one organism, and the organism was glad. After, we seared the wound shut with skillet edge. Jonah lived—barely, breathing shallow, smile wide. Cal sat beside him, feeding strips of his own cooked muscle, Jonah chewing what was once him, eyes closed in bliss. I should have looked away. I took notes instead. Night: snow heavy, sky low. Chain sound gone, replaced by soft humming, many voices. The Seconds, Cal, Tadpole, all harmonising that single note. I joined without deciding, throat vibrating, felt the sound leave mouths, travel walls, settle into beams. Lodge itself answered, timber creaking chord. I tried to count heartbeats but they overlapped—one big drum. My skin itched, not unpleasant, like something preparing to bloom. In the hearth the last log split, revealed inside: pale threads, already glowing. We’d burned infected wood all week, breathing it. I wrote: “Colonisation – respiratory route confirmed,” then the charcoal crumbled and I didn’t fetch another. Cal washed dishes, movements slow, ceremonial. Blood on his cuffs looked like wine, like rust, like acceptance. He caught my eye, smiled soft. “Tomorrow we harvest again,” he said. “The table must be full.” I nodded, mouth still tasting him, me, us, everything. I lay down but sleep felt like a rehearsal for dinner. Something knocked gently beneath my ribs— polite, persistent. I placed a palm there, answered yes without voice. Outside the orchard pulsed pink, keeping tempo with the knock. Wind carried the smell of seared steak and incense. I breathed it in, breathed it out, licked my lips, waited for morning service.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkable piece of sustained dark fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. VOICE AND EXECUTION: The first-person notebook voice of Mab is maintained with impressive consistency. The charcoal-writing conceit (replacing ink as supplies dwindle) is a clever material detail that reinforces the colonisation theme—she's already running out of the tools of documentation. The voice is clipped, observational, self-aware about its own failures ("I wrote: 'Ethical collapse – document only.' Charcoal trembled."). This is genuinely good craft. FOLLOWING THE PLAN: The chapter plan called for "infection check: soft lace under my skin, faint glow. Pike's gums silvered. Gran June coughs spores like flour. We argue once—then mouths too full to speak. Outside: first thaw; mushrooms taller than spruce. They bend toward the lodge like sunflowers. I try to write a warning, keep listing ingredients instead." The actual chapter diverges significantly—Brother Cal dominates, the Jonah surgical scene takes center stage, and the specific infection details (Pike's gums, Gran June's spore-coughing) are largely absent or minimized. Gran June barely appears. The chapter plan's intimacy and character-specific infection markers are replaced by a more elaborate ritual/communion narrative. This is a meaningful deviation, though the thematic territory (colonisation confirmed, warning replaced by recipe-listing) is honored in spirit. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Cal is introduced here as a new character not in the profiles, which is fine—the plan mentioned him. However, the existing characters are somewhat flattened. Pike appears briefly. Gran June is nearly absent. Tadpole appears but his distinctive behaviors (Fibonacci spirals, texture-sorting, bird calls) are reduced to humming B-flat, which is consistent but thin. Mab's voice is the strongest and most consistent with her profile—the compulsive documentation, the scientific notation bleeding into appetite, the self-aware horror. POETIC LEAPS AND PURPLE PROSE: This is where the chapter has real problems. There are numerous instances of metaphor-stacking that don't quite land or that feel strained: "silence grew teeth," "faith overlay – possible mitigation of addiction," "pupils dilated like cathedral doors," "lighting candles in my veins," "history collapsing into one long table," "sleep felt like a rehearsal for dinner." Some of these work. "Sleep felt like a rehearsal for dinner" is genuinely excellent. "Pupils dilated like cathedral doors" is trying too hard—pupils don't dilate like doors, and the cathedral reference is reaching for religious resonance in a clumsy way. "Lighting candles in my veins" is the kind of bioluminescent poetry the plan explicitly warned against. The chapter occasionally tips into the purple prose it was meant to avoid. The communion/Eucharist extended metaphor is both the chapter's greatest strength and its most dangerous gambit. It works thematically—the horror of literal communion, bodies broken and shared—but it's deployed so heavily and so explicitly that it loses some of its power. The reader is told "This is my body" and "meant it literally" rather than being allowed to feel the horror arrive on their own. The plan called for "cozy is the trap, not the blanket"—here the religious horror is announced rather than crept up on. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is sparse but mostly functional. Cal's lines ("Bless this bounty, born of us, returning to us," "They are ready to give themselves," "Tomorrow we harvest again") are appropriately eerie without being overwrought. Pike's "Amen" around a mouthful is a good small moment. The dialogue is thin but not weak—it serves the notebook format where Mab wouldn't transcribe everything. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The chapter is genuinely disturbing and emotionally effective in places. The Jonah scene—surgical removal of a "heart-knot" while he's conscious, fed his own cooked flesh—is genuinely transgressive and horrifying. The moment where Mab takes notes instead of looking away is perfectly in character and devastating. The final image of something knocking beneath her ribs, her answering "yes without voice," is excellent. COHERENCE: The chapter is mostly coherent but the timeline is compressed in ways that strain credulity—Cal arrives, they harvest, they perform surgery, all within what seems like two or three days. The "Boy" reference (presumably Tadpole, though called "Boy" here inconsistently with his established nickname) and the chain detail feel like they reference events not established in this chapter or the plan. WELL-EARNED DARKNESS: The darkness is largely earned. The Jonah scene doesn't feel gratuitous because it's been built toward through the chapter's religious framework. The final colonisation confirmation ("pale threads, already glowing" in the log) is chilling and well-placed. UNSURPRISING/UNCREATIVE: The communion metaphor, while executed with some skill, is not a surprising choice for a story about eating flesh. It's the obvious religious framework to reach for. The chapter is creative in its details but somewhat predictable in its structural conceit. OVERALL: A strong chapter with genuine moments of excellence, marred by occasional purple prose, significant deviation from the chapter plan, and over-reliance on an obvious (if well-executed) religious metaphor. The voice is the chapter's greatest asset. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 12 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 9 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 8 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 11 Followed Chapter Plan: 8 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
18 Feb – writing with a feather I pulled from my own skin. Tip is soft, ink is me. Paper tastes like bread if you chew it slow. The lodge breathes. Beams swell, shrink, swell – eighty beats a minute, my heartbeat, our heartbeat. We are one mouth now, one stomach, many teeth. Outside the orchard stretches pink to the river, fruiting bodies bobbing like congregants waiting for wafer and wine. They glow brighter at dusk – lantern parade for the last supper on earth. Inside: long table built from doors we no longer close. Plates are skillet lids, cups are bone. Cal stands at head, robe sewn from ration sacks, collar daubed heart-red. He lifts the chalice – an old thermos, bottom coated black. Inside: reduction thinned with melted snow, steam curling like censer smoke. We sing the one note, B-flat, voices layered, no beginning, no amen. Jonah sits beside me, mid-section caved but healing. The missing knot regrew – smaller, pear-shaped, beats visible under skin. He feeds himself to us in slivers, eyes rolled white with bliss. Each cut sears shut immediately, skillet always hot. His lips move: “Take, eat,” around mouthfuls of himself. We obey. Chew, swallow, glow. Tadpole tends the orchard now. He walks rows barefoot, feet leaving phosphor prints that fade by morning. He prunes with a bone knife, whispers coordinates; the stalks lean, comply. Yield is up three hundred percent – maths I did on the back of my hand, numbers crawling like ants. When fruit drops he catches, never bruises. We call it communion, he calls it garden, both are true. Pike is sauce-master. He lives at the hearth, naked but for an apron stitched from feed sacks. Skin glossy with fat, hair matted, eyes two black olives rolling in steam. Tastes with finger, adjusts: pinch ash for bitterness, snowmelt for brightness, his own blood when iron is lacking. He hasn’t spoken in two days – communication is spoon lifted, nod, satisfied grunt. Language boiled away, leaving flavour only. Gran no longer counts bullets – rifle hangs above the door like retired icon. Her hands make jars now: pack, seal, set on hearth ledge to pasteurise. She labels with pictures – heart, smile, cross – no numbers. Counting implies ending; we have stepped past. Her foot, once black, blooms rose-gold gills; she wears it proudly, sandal hacked to display. When she walks the boards glow faint, echo her hymn hummed under breath. The Seconds farm themselves. They take turns on the cutting table, offering quadriceps, loin, sometimes a sliver of ear – hearing tastes salty, they say. Cal marks incisions with ash crosses, sears shut, returns them to chores. No infection, no pus, only quick healing and gratitude. They queue willingly, eyes bright, stomachs drum-hungry for their own flesh served back holy. I am recorder, ingredient, diner. My ribs show but skin glows translucent, veins blue as old hymns. Inside, something knocks polite – four limbs budding, or maybe wings. I note changes on any surface: wall, tablecloth, my own forearm. Letters spread, join, make lace. The story writes me now. Today we host the valley. They came at dawn – ragged dozens, footprints weaving like ant trails. Smelled us on the wind, followed the pink glow. They stood outside the rail, eyes wide, mouths working. Cal welcomed them, arms out, robe flapping. “Come, taste, be remade.” No force, just invitation. They entered one by one, coats sloughed, weapons dropped like skins. We served breakfast: seared strips on cedar shards, drizzle of reduction, side of pickled gill. First bite: pupils blow, knees buckle, tears fall – universal response. After swallowing they line up for work – chopping wood, hauling snow, offering their own cuts when asked. The valley is converting, quietly, deliciously. Midday: I stand on the porch, address no one, everyone. Voice comes out layered, chorded. “This is not the end. This is the table set for forever. Bring your hunger, leave your bones, return as flavour.” Applause is the sound of chewing continuing. Evening: orchard sways, exhales spore cloud that drifts, settles on roofs, fields, river ice.
