Rain stopped, leak hasn’t. He empties buckets, enjoying the labour because cameras can’t see. Proposes a 30-second phone clip: him reading in Welsh, her shop tagged—fee £250, no strings. She cross-examines every clause; he’s rattled by her thoroughness, signs anyway. Mid-handshake his agent rings loud; he rejects the call—first voluntary hit to career. He pockets a damp £5 note she dropped—will remember later. **Chapter 5 – Glare (≈1 k)** Ex-co-star barges in with local reporter, flash on. Elin’s eczema flares under scrutiny; Rhys sees red patch on her neck, forgets script. Public apology tumbles out—half in Welsh, half garbage—but he shields the glass cabinet with his body. Crowd leaves; Elin’s skin bleeds where she scratched. She blames him for the invasion; he can’t joke his way out. He offers the stolen £5 as contribution, she throws it, note sticks wet to his chest—comedy that hurts. **Chapter 6 – Dust (≈1 k)** Night. He’s gone; shop feels huge. She finds his childhood Mabinogion between hymnals, inscribed with a primary-school scrawl. Reads mother’s old letter: doesn’t mention books, only “leave the door ajar for people.” She locks up, walks to the car park, stands in drizzle, no idea what she’s waiting for. Scene ends with car lights approaching—maybe his, maybe not. **Chapter 7 – Lane (≈1 k)** Festival fireworks pop in distance. She carries the book; meets him packing boot of hire car. Conversation mono-syllabic, then bursts into Welsh—argument about who failed whom. Neither says “stay.” Instead they agree to film the clip right now, no audience, just to finish something. Firework reflected in puddle—brief red on their faces, then dark again. **Chapter 8 – Roll (≈1 k)** Early morning. Handheld phone, single take. He stumbles over mutation; she corrects, giggles—sound surprises both. Camera left running as they sit on the bottom pew, shoulders touching, not looking. Audio continues: page turned, soft laugh, maybe a kiss—viewer can’t see, lens faces hymn-board. Last 30 words are transcript of breathing and chapel echo; screen is black.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Rhys Morgan, 32 – "the rogue who checks his own reflection in a spoon" ## Background - Born in Tonypandy, Rhondda valley; mam was a dinner-lady, dad a fitter who swore at the telly every Saturday final. - Child actor in Welsh-language soaps; learned early that a grin got you seconds at craft-services and an extra fiver in Mam's purse. - Moved to London at 18, lost accent in drama school, then spent next decade buying it back in dribs and drabs when Hollywood wanted "authentic Celtic spice". - Leaked a story that co-star Ava Delaney was "difficult" so the studio would keep him on for franchise reshoots – has told himself so often it was "just business" he half-believes it. ## Personality & Idiosyncrasies - Repeats your name aloud the second you tell him – charm hack he can't turn off. - Sleeps with phone face-down; still checks notifications at 3 a.m., heart racing if none. - counts on fingers when memorising lines – index finger gets the stressed syllable, pinky the full stop. - Buys expensive cologne then over-sprays; knows it, can't stop. - Will apologise to furniture after bumping it, but will ghost a girlfriend for three days if she beats him at pool. ## Flaws Fragile ego disguised as confidence; once sacked a publicist because she said his "brand" was approachable. Compulsive fibber about trivialities – claims allergies to foods he just dislikes. Avoids confrontation by complimenting: "Love how passionate you are about my lateness." ## Physicality 5'11", gym-fit but going soft from hotel breakfasts. Dark curls that frizz in Welsh rain – he tugs at them when cornered. Smile first, eyes second; the delay gives him away. Moves like he's always just spotted a camera – shoulders angled best-side, chin three millimetres up. ## Motives & Allegiances Wants to be liked more than he wants to like himself. Loyalty is transitive: whoever can keep cameras flashing in his favour. Currently allegiant to his L.A. agent Siân (also Welsh, also ex-valley) because she still calls him "Rhys bach" and scolds like Mam. ## Relationships - Pays sister's mortgage but forgets birthdays; sends emojis instead. - Ava Delaney won't return calls – guilt arrives as indigestion, not conscience. - Has no actual friends in the valley; invites crew for "pints" then cancels when he realises they'll see him cry after two pints. ## Voice Samples **Dialogue:** "Rhys Morgan, pleased to meet the woman who's keeping printed words alive – and, evidently, keeping them very dry," (glances at leaking roof, winks – wobble in cheek muscle). **Internal:** She didn't smile. Store that, use it. Maybe self-deprecating bit on Graham Norton – "Welsh women immune to me, thank God someone is." --- # Elin Thomas, 29 – "the sentry with paper cuts" ## Background - Grew up in the flat above Y Storfa; mother read her to sleep with Mabinogion battles, father left to drive lorries and sent postcards that stopped when she was ten. - First-class lit PhD aborted at viva stage when Mam's lungs failed; came home to stack oxygen tanks between Penguin classics. - Inherited shop and debt; creditors more punctual than customers. ## Personality & Idiosyncrasies - Catalogues life in Dewey decimals: break-ups = 152.4, utility bills 640. - Keeps eczema cream in a hollowed-out Welsh dictionary; applies when no one watches. - Hates people touching spines face-out – "books sunbathe, they don't strip". - Says "yeah?" at end of sentences when nervous, turning statement into accidental plea. ## Flaws Pride disguised as practicality; would rather lose the shop than accept obvious pity. Uses sarcasm as tourniquet – stops bleed, hurts later. Holds eye-contact too long when challenged, making strangers feel accused. ## Physicality 5'5", tends to hunch one shoulder higher, carrying invisible tote of responsibilities. Hair the colour of wet slate, usually yanked into pencil; strands escape like static. Hands never fully un-inked; thumbnail split from opening boxes. Voice low, impatient, cracks on Welsh ll sound when exhausted. ## Motives & Allegiances Preserve Mam's legacy, but increasingly unsure if legacy is shop or her own life. Loyal to Ifan (teen assistant) because he mispronounces "Abergavenny" same way she did at fifteen. Owes nothing to father who occasionally texts "How's business?" from Spain – she deletes without reply. ## Relationships - Ex, Gareth, teaches in Cardiff; sends "remember the grant deadline?" emails that double as breadcrumb trail back to academic life she pretends she doesn't miss. - Regular Mrs Pugh brings bara brith, talks ailments; Elin listens because dead air costs nothing. - Cat named Brontë – indoor/outdoor policy decided by cat, not her. ## Voice Samples **Dialogue:** "Closed means closed, same in Welsh and English – push again and you'll be bilingual in bruises, yeah?" **Internal:** He smells like the aftershave Da wore the day he left. Note to self: do not soften. Soft things mould.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The rain hit like bird-shot, hard little pellets that stung the scalp and bounced off the bus-stop glass. Rhys Morgan pulled the hood of his £600 jacket over his curls, realised it was pointless, and sprinted. The lane was narrow, Victorian cottages turned into gift shops, no awning deep enough to hide a six-foot man being hunted by long-lens hyenas. He saw the arched wooden door, saw the hand-painted sign too small to read, and shouldered it open without breaking stride. Inside smelled of old paper and damp hymnbooks, a smell that rammed him straight into primary-school assembly. He slammed the door with his back, breathing through his mouth, listening for the shutter-click pack. Nothing. Just the tick of a wall heater and the faint salt of mould. “We’re shut.” The voice came from below counter-height. He looked down at a woman cross-legged on the floor, pricing paperbacks with a gun that made a plastic ka-chunk. She didn’t glance up. “Sign says open,” he tried, smile auto-loading. “Sign lies.” Ka-chunk. He pushed wet hair off his forehead, aware the gesture made his biceps pop. “I only need the Crime section. Won’t loiter.” “Crime’s through there.” She jerked her chin toward a narrow aisle between pews—actual pews, he realised, the bookcases screwed into their backs. “Take what you want, leave the money, lock up after yourself.” Rhys laughed, the one he used on breakfast television. “You run honour-system shoplifting?” “Not my job to parent grown men.” She stood, wiping dusty palms down a denim skirt. Her eyes were slate-coloured, same as the valley sky outside. No flicker of recognition. That threw him. He waited for the micro-flare: eyebrow, smile, phone half-raised. Nothing. He became conscious of water dripping off his jacket onto worn flagstones. “Rhys Morgan,” he supplied, hand out before he could stop himself. She stared at the hand, then at his face. “I know your name. You’re on the side of the Cardiff bus.” He grinned, relief warm. “Guilty. Thought I’d escaped billboard justice by coming home.” She didn’t return the smile. “Home?” “Born up the valley. Tonypandy.” He exaggerated the rolled r, show-boating. “Mam still there. You?” She picked up a stack of Ellis Peters. “I’m here.” The bell above the door jiggled. A camera lens poked in like a snout. Rhys dropped instinctively, crouched behind the pew-bench. The woman watched him, unreadable. “He’s not open,” she called to the lens. Door shut again. Footsteps splashed away. Rhys stayed squatting. “Cheers for that.” “Don’t thank me. I just hate tourists with Canons.” She shelved books spine-out, quick snaps. “You can stand up. They’ll circle the square, come back.” He rose, knees clicking. “Shop’s a good hideout. Converted chapel, yeah? Nice niche.” “Niche is Latin for coffin,” she muttered. He barked a laugh, too loud between the stone walls. “What’s your name?” “Elin.” No surname offered. “Pretty. Saint’s name. Means light.” “It means Mum wanted something short Da couldn’t slur.” She climbed a small ladder, thighs flexing. He looked, then looked away, oddly caught. Rain intensified, drumming the stained-glass half-moon above the door. Rhys wiped droplets off a cover: Teach Yourself Welsh for Beginners. He opened it at random, spoke phonetically. “Dw i’n hoffi coffi.” “That’s useful,” she said, deadpan. “Plenty of coffee in the Rhondda.” He laughed again, softer. “Never got past ordering drinks. Moved away too young.” “Lucky you.” The ladder creaked as she reached the top shelf. He saw a patch of angry red skin behind her ear, disappearing into hairline. Eczema, maybe. He wanted to ask, bit his tongue. “So, Elin, any chance of a towel? Or shall I buy the jacket off myself?” She descended, handed him a tea-towel printed with daffodils. “Fifty pence. Add it to the honesty pile.” He dabbed uselessly. “Shop been here long?” “Always. Was chapel first, then Mam, now me.” She said it like a weather report. “Nice inheritance. Peaceful.” “Peace doesn’t pay business rates.” She glanced at the door. “Paps gone quiet. You can run for it.” He didn’t move. “Got anywhere to be? Nah. Let them prowl.” She folded her arms. “Your funeral.” He drifted along Crime, pulling out titles, putting them back angled so his fingers wouldn’t crease. “Read this?” he held up a bleak noir. “Pages are stuck with someone else’s blood. Enjoy.” He snorted. “You’re brutal.” “You’re wet.” He watched her re-shelve. No sway of hips, no performative stretch. She simply reached, pushed, stepped down. He realised he was waiting for the moment she’d peek—nothing. It itched under his skin like badly-fitted armour. “Tell you what,” he said, sliding the book under his arm. “I’ll buy this, sign it—add value. You flog it online.” “Autographs don’t keep lights on.” “Humour me.” He already had the Sharpie out, cap between teeth. She took the book, opened the title page, scrawled herself: 50p – flood damage. Handed it back. “There. Signed.” His turn to blink. “You’re serious.” “Deadly.” She met his eyes then, and he saw himself small, ridiculous, dripping on her floor. The reflection stung harder than the rain outside. Door rattled again—gentler, a teenager. “Elin, you got change for the meter?” She reached the till, fingers quick. Rhys saw floaters of coins, pitifully few. A pang he couldn't name flicked through him—guilt, maybe debt. The boy left. Rain eased to a hiss. “You should go,” she said, not unkindly. “They’ll photograph the number-plate, hassle customers.” He nodded, surprised by reluctance. “Mind if I duck out the back?” “No back. Just front.” She moved to the door, hand on bolt. “Ready?” He inhaled chapel dust, paper rot, a hint of her lemon soap. “Yeah.” She slid the bolt; grey light and drizzle spilled in. Cameras were regrouping at the chapel gate. He stepped onto the threshold, paused. “Thanks, Elin.” “Bring the book back dry,” she answered, and shut the door between them. The lock clicked. He stood a second, jacket shedding water onto worn stone, feeling oddly hollow, like an actor who'd delivered every line yet still missed the applause.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates genuine craft. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions. **Characterization and Voice** Rhys is rendered with precision: the auto-loading smile, the bicep-pop gesture he's aware of, the Sharpie already out before she's agreed, the name-drop of Tonypandy with an exaggerated rolled r. These are not told to us but demonstrated through behavior. His ego's fragility surfaces in the line "He became conscious of water dripping off his jacket onto worn flagstones" — the moment recognition fails to arrive. The detail "He waited for the micro-flare: eyebrow, smile, phone half-raised. Nothing" is excellent character work. Elin is equally well-drawn. Her monosyllabic deflections feel earned rather than contrived. "It means Mum wanted something short Da couldn't slur" is a perfect line — it reveals backstory, character, and wit simultaneously without feeling expository. Her pricing books with a ka-chunk gun while ignoring a famous man is a strong visual choice. **Dialogue** The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. It has genuine rhythm and distinct voices. Rhys's lines carry the slight over-performance of a man used to performing; Elin's carry the exhaustion of someone who has stopped performing entirely. "Niche is Latin for coffin" is a standout. The exchange about the autograph — where she signs the book herself with "50p – flood damage" — is the chapter's best moment: funny, character-revealing, and genuinely surprising. **Following the Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely: paparazzi at the bus stop, the shoulder-barge entry, "We're shut," the Crime section line, the till-key moment (slightly altered — she doesn't pocket a key but the dynamic is preserved), and the bolt sliding. The plan called for the last line to trap them both, but the chapter ends with him outside and her locking the door behind him. This is a deviation — the plan specified she slides the bolt trapping them both because the lane is still flashing cameras. Instead, he leaves. This is a meaningful structural departure that affects the chapter's ending energy. **Metaphor and Prose Quality** Most metaphors land. "Rain hit like bird-shot" is in the plan and works. "Camera lens poked in like a snout" is vivid and apt. "Long-lens hyenas" is slightly strained but acceptable. "Smile auto-loading" is excellent. A few moments edge toward the overwrought: "feeling oddly hollow, like an actor who'd delivered every line yet still missed the applause" is a touch on-the-nose — it tells us what we've already felt through the scene. The lemon soap detail at the end is nice but the final paragraph slightly over-explains the emotional beat. **Coherence and Plot** The chapter is coherent and moves naturally. The paparazzi threat creates genuine tension. The power dynamic between the characters is established cleanly. The ending, while deviating from the plan, still works as a chapter close — though it loses the "trapped together" setup that would have made Chapter 2 flow more naturally. **Weaknesses** The main weakness is the ending deviation from the plan — the plan's "trapping them both" was a stronger structural choice that would have created more immediate tension. The final paragraph's explicit emotional summary ("feeling oddly hollow, like an actor who'd delivered every line yet still missed the applause") slightly undercuts what the scene has already shown us. The eczema detail is introduced but not quite integrated — it's observed and then dropped with "He wanted to ask, bit his tongue," which is fine but feels slightly planted. **Overall** This is accomplished, confident writing. The dialogue is sharp, the characters are consistent with their profiles, the Welsh setting is evoked without being touristic, and the chemistry is present without being announced. Minor structural deviation and one over-explained emotional beat prevent it from being exceptional. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
He came back at opening time the next morning, hair still damp from a hotel shower that had run cold after three minutes. The sign in the door now read OPEN, but only just; someone had chalked the O wonky so it looked like a zero. Rhys pushed, heard the same bell clack, and stepped into yesterday's smell—paper, mould, and something faintly citrus that might have been the woman or might have been furniture polish. Elin was on the ladder again, jeans pocket ripped at the seam, flash of pale skin when she twisted. She didn't look down. "You open or shut?" he asked, shaking rain off a paper bag. "Both. Depends." She slid a slim volume into place. "You return that book dry?" He held it out. Pages were warped into gentle waves. "Near enough. Signed it for you—see, top corner. Should treble the price." She climbed down, took the book, flicked to the title page. There, in aggressive black, was his looping scrawl: To the keeper of sanctuary—may the cameras never find you. R. Morgan. "Sanctuary spelt with one r," she said. "Adds authenticity." "Adds idiocy." But she didn't rub it out, just set the book on a random shelf. "Fifty pence plus late fee. Another fifty." He fished for coins, came up with a pound. "Keep the change. Buy yourself a vowel." A snort escaped her—half-laugh, half-derision. She covered it by turning to the till. Footsteps thundered behind him; a teenage boy burst through, school tie askew. "Elin, Ifan's here with the boxes. Says they're heavy." "Tell Ifan gravity's free," she answered. The boy grinned and vanished. Rhys followed her gaze to the doorway where a skinny kid in a Metallica hoodie wrestled a tower of cardboard. Rhys moved automatically. "Need a hand, mate?" Ifan looked up, eyes widening. "You're—" "Yeah, allegedly. Show me where." Between them they manhandled the boxes to the counter. Ifan kept side-eyeing Rhys as if expecting theme music. Elin slit tape, pulled out pristine paperbacks, spine uncreased. Rhys read the top title aloud. "Salt Road. Any good?" "If you like grief," Elin said. "I've time-scheduled mine for my thirties." "You're late." Ifan giggled. Rhys shot him a conspirator's grin, but the boy was staring at the actor, not the joke. The silence stretched until Elin slapped an invoice on the pile. "Ifan, stamp these prices. And breathe." She handed the kid a pricing gun. Ka-chunk, ka-chunk—soundtrack of small commerce. Rhys leaned against the pew-bench, feeling surplus. "So," he tried, "lunch break for the workforce? I could sign stock. Value-add." "We add value by reading them," Elin replied, not looking up. "Humour me. Marketing's my religion." "Thought that was ego." Ifan barked a laugh, then blushed crimson. Rhys felt the sting, covered with a smile too wide. "Touché. Still, a signature never hurt." Elin lifted a shoulder. "Hurt the book if it's drivel." He picked up the nearest, flicked to flyleaf, signed with a flourish. She watched, unimpressed. He signed another, slower. Still nothing. The third he inscribed: Elin's right—this is probably drivel. He underlined probably twice. She read it, mouth twitching. "At least you're accurate." Ka-chunk from Ifan sounded like distant artillery. Rhys exhaled. "Kid, who's your favourite actor?" "Ryan Reynolds," Ifan answered instantly. Rhys laughed, genuine this time. "Good taste. Funnier than me, richer too." "But you're Welsh," Ifan offered, apologetic. "Can't compete with that," Rhys said, and meant it. Elin slid a stack toward Ifan. "Shelf these. And remember: face-out sells, spine-out stays." The boy lumbered off. Rhys trailed him with his eyes, then turned back. "You pay him?" "Volunteer. Community service for joyriding his dad's van. Says books smell better than courtrooms." "Does he read?" "Says he will when he's old. Seventeen's immortal." Rhys watched the kid disappear between pews. "I was him once—wanted out of a valley that smelled of coal and canteen chips." "And now you're back smelling paper and damp." "Circle of life, only wetter." She snorted again—softer. Encouraged, he opened a hardback, read a line aloud. "The past is never dead. It's not even past.' Faulkner, but could be Welsh. We specialise in never letting things go." "Explains the ego," she muttered, marking prices in pencil so faint he leaned closer, caught lemon and something medicinal. "Explains yours too," he shot back. "Still running your mother's shop like it's a chapel." Her pencil paused. "It is a chapel. Some of us like echoes." The air tightened. He felt the misstep, reached for repair. "I like echoes. I'm made of them." She studied him, eyes narrowing—not angry, calculating. "What do you want here, Rhys Morgan?" The question hit blunt, no cushion. He opened his mouth, closed it. Honesty arrived unscripted. "Somewhere the cameras aren't. And..." he shrugged, "...you didn't clap. It's—refreshing." Her eyebrow lifted, eczema patch pinking where hair tucked. "You collect indifference now?" "Maybe. Rare vintage." "Try the supermarket next door. Plenty on tap." She turned to the next box. He felt the dismissal, surprising panic fluttered. "Let me help finish. No autographs, no sermons." "Hands clean?" He displayed palms, slightly ink-smudged. She nodded at the poetry shelf. "Face-out on Neruda. If you bend pages I'll bend fingers." "Deal." He worked quietly, matching spines, sneaking glances. She moved efficient, no wasted motion, occasionally mouthing titles as if tasting. Once she caught him watching; he gave a sheepish smile, expected quip—none came, just steady gaze until he looked away first. When the last book stood aligned, Ifan reappeared clutching a phone. "Elin, meter bloke's outside. Says he's cutting electric if we don't top up by twelve." She checked the till, face blank. Rhys saw the float: coins, two fivers, nothing more. His hand slid to pocket, found the damp pound from yesterday, a twenty from the hotel mini-bar refund. He held it out before pride could gag him. "Loan," he said. "Pay-day loan, literally. Interest free if you let me stay till the rain quits." She stared at the note, at him, at Ifan's hopeful face. Took the money, folded it once. "Interest is one coffee," she stipulated. "Instant. And you leave when the sun does." "Forecast says cloud till October," he grinned. "Then we both suffer." She brushed past, scent of lemon stronger, and he felt something loosen in his chest—not victory, more like permission to keep being seen. Ifan punched his arm, whispered, "Ryan Reynolds never paid our leccy." Rhys rubbed the spot, oddly proud. "Tell you at lunch why Welsh blokes are better." Elin, halfway to the door, called over her shoulder. "Lunch is half a sandwich and no spoilers." He followed, leaving signed stock scattered like breadcrumbs, unsure whether he was Hansel or the witch, only certain he wanted to stay inside one more scene.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is Chapter 2 of a multi-chapter romance set in a Welsh bookshop. Let me assess it carefully across all dimensions. **Strengths:** The dialogue is genuinely strong throughout. The exchange has distinct voices: Rhys's reflexive charm ("Keep the change. Buy yourself a vowel"), his compulsive need to perform even when the audience won't clap, and Elin's dry, economical deflections ("Explains the ego"). The banter feels earned rather than constructed, and the rhythm of the conversation—advance, rebuff, advance—mirrors the chapter plan's intent well. Character consistency is excellent. Rhys's ego-sting when Ifan names Ryan Reynolds as his favourite actor, then his genuine laugh rather than wounded retreat, shows a man who can occasionally transcend his own vanity. His signing books with increasingly self-aware inscriptions ("Elin's right—this is probably drivel") is a nice touch—the charm offensive beginning to crack into something more honest. Elin's monosyllabic efficiency, her management of Ifan, her pride around money (the pause before taking the twenty), and the eczema detail all track with the profile. Ifan is a well-deployed minor character—he provides comic relief, a witness function, and a humanising moment for Rhys without overstaying his welcome. The ending is particularly good: "unsure whether he was Hansel or the witch" is a metaphor that actually lands, and "permission to keep being seen" captures Rhys's emotional need without over-explaining it. **Weaknesses:** The chapter plan called for: an autograph on a travel guide used to blot hair, an argument about Wales and marketing, a T. Gwalia quote, a power flicker. Most of these are absent or significantly altered. The autograph scene is replaced by a different signing sequence. The T. Gwalia reference doesn't appear. The power flicker is replaced by a meter man threatening to cut electricity—which is actually a more grounded, effective version of the same dramatic function, so this is a defensible deviation. But the Wales/marketing argument is only glancingly present ("Marketing's my religion" / "Thought that was ego"), not developed into the substantive exchange the plan envisioned. Some lines veer toward the slightly too-neat: "Circle of life, only wetter" is a bit sitcom-ish. "The past is never dead. It's not even past" is Faulkner dropped in somewhat conveniently. The Faulkner quote feels like a prop rather than an organic moment—it's used to make Rhys seem literary, but it reads as the author reaching. "Something loosen in his chest—not victory, more like permission to keep being seen" is good but slightly over-explained. The reader could have been trusted to feel this without the gloss. The "lemon and something medicinal" scent detail is effective and consistent with the eczema cream in the hollowed dictionary from the profile. The chapter doesn't quite deliver on the plan's promise of "power flickers—first threat of outage," substituting instead a meter man. This is fine narratively but means the chapter ends without the atmospheric tension the plan intended. **Overall:** This is confident, well-crafted work. The dialogue is the clear strength—it's genuinely funny in places, emotionally layered, and character-revealing. The chapter follows the spirit of the prompt well even where it deviates from the specific plan. It's not quite masterpiece territory—some moments are too tidy, the Faulkner quote is a slight misstep, and the chapter plan adherence is partial—but it's substantially above average. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 10 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The lights died at three-thirteen. One moment the heater was humming, the next a click, a sigh, and the sort of dark that swallows colour. Rain still slammed the stained-glass; without the bulb-glow the chapel felt like a locked throat. “Perfect,” Elin muttered. Her silhouette crouched behind the counter, fingers sweeping the torch app across the screen—dead battery logo jeering. Rhys fished his phone from damp jeans. Blue-white beam leapt, throwing pew-shadows up the walls. “Service entrance? Fuse box?” “Through the vestry, past the theology no-one buys. Keys are upstairs in the flat.” “Show me.” She hesitated. He caught it—tiny recoil, as if inviting him farther in might loosen some bolt she couldn’t retighten. Then practicality won. “Ladder first. Candles in the pulpit drawer.” He steadied the rung while she climbed. Rain drummed louder, metallic on the slate roof. Somewhere water dripped onto cardboard. His free hand hovered at her ankle, not quite touching. She reached the top, prised open a wooden hatch, and disappeared into the loft-black. Beam swayed, found her calf, then nothing. “You alive up there?” “Give me the torch.” He tossed; she caught it one-handed, vanished again. Darkness owned the shop for a count of ten. He became aware of his own pulse, the smell of wet wool mixing with old incense. Then a rectangle of weak grey opened above: loft skylight. Thunder rolled approval. Candle stubs rained down, followed by a box of Swan Vestas. He struck three before one took, propped them in spent bottle necks. The flames pulled the room smaller, warmer, conspiratorial. She descended, jumper snagged at the shoulder, curl of hair plastered to her cheek. He stopped himself reaching to move it. “Meter’s dead,” she said. “No credit left after I fed the leccy monster this morning.” A flicker of guilt crossed her face—twenty of his pounds, gone before lunch. He shrugged. “Call it rent for the matinée.” Candlelight made the eczema patch behind her ear angrier; she scratched without thinking, winced. “Got any cream?” he asked. “Drawer full of half-empty promises.” She rubbed with her sleeve instead, turned to the shelves. “While we wait for coins from heaven we might as well shelve.” “Shelve in the dark?” “Candles make us selective. Poetry needs night.” She handed him a slim volume—R. S. Thomas, cover blistered by damp. “Read something.” He cleared his throat, tried preacher resonance: “‘I have seen the sun break through to illuminate a small field for a while…’” “Not a sermon,” she cut. “Speak it like you’re asking the walls to forgive you.” He started again, softer. The Welsh surname felt odd in his mouth; he over-pronounced. When he finished the flame was tilting, wax pooling. Your turn,” he said. She took the book, thumb brushing his—no retreat. Voice low, she gave the next poem, Welsh lines first, English after. Accent valley-true; the ll sound hissed like rain on iron. He understood one word in five, felt each unknown consonant lodge under his ribs. Silence afterward had weight. Water dripped somewhere rhythmic, almost tidal. “Why here?” he asked. “Why stay?” She considered, scratching at her sleeve. “Mam died mid-chapter. Couldn’t close the book.” Candle showed no self-pity, just fact. “Also, I like telling time by light through coloured glass. Keeps afternoons from running together.” He glanced up. Reds, blues, greens swam in weak rectangles across stone. “I measure time by how long between compliments,” he said before thinking. She snorted. “Must be exhausting, keeping the meter running.” “It’s autopilot. You notice, you comment, they smile—you exist.” He tried to laugh; it came out thin. “You don’t smile. Throws the calibration.” “Get a new app,” she suggested, and surprised him by almost smiling—corners of mouth fighting corners of eyes. Another drip, closer. He shifted, found the edge of cardboard under his trainer. “Those boxes’ll be mush by morning. Insurance?” “Third-party, first-class useless.” She pinched the candle, seemed to read the liquid wax. “Rare Welsh shelf’s in there. First editions Mam collected like dragon eggs. If they warp I sell them as ‘experienced’.” He pictured headlines: MORGAN HIDES WHILE HERITAGE DROWNS. Guilt rose, acidic. “We move them. Now.” They worked by two-inch flame. He passed stacks up the aisle, she sorted on the altar step—literature separated from theology by the width of a candle. Occasionally their hands collided; neither commented. Sweat gathered under his hair, mixed with rainwater, ran salt into his mouth. At the bottom of a box he found a slim 1893 Mabinogion, leather cracked like river mud. He weighed it, fragile and accusing. “This alone pays your electricity for a year.” “Or buys me a ticket out,” she countered, voice small. He looked up. “Why not sell one, keep the rest? Sacrifice pawn, save king?” “Because once you start choosing, the choosing never stops.” She took the volume, cradled it against her chest as if feeling for heartbeat. Flame lit the eczema patch again—he saw tiny half-moon scratches where nails had dug. Impulse overtook caution. “I leaked a story,” he said fast. “My co-star—Ava—she got labelled difficult, got written out. I needed the franchise. Felt clever for about a day. Been running from that day ever since.” The candle guttered; shadows jumped. She didn't speak, didn't move. He felt the confession hanging, wet and ridiculous. “So,” she said finally, “you drown books instead of fixing the pipe.” He barked a laugh, shocked. “Basically.” Silence stretched, elastic. Then she placed the Mabinogion on the highest pew, out of drip range. “We all leak something.” She met his eyes, no accusation, just inventory. The look lasted long enough for the candle to eat another centimetre. Thunder rolled overhead, close. Lights in neighbouring cottages blinked on—someone else's meter topped up. Their shared darkness felt suddenly chosen. He cleared his throat. “Got any coffee? Instant'll do. Interest on the twenty quid.” She gestured toward the vestry kettle, cord wrapped tight. “Water'll be cold.” “Cold tastes honest.” He unplugged, carried the kettle, brushed her shoulder—neither flinched. “Name your brand of honesty, Elin.” She watched him go, candle shrinking between them. “Start with the spelling,” she said. “Sanctuary has two r's.” He smiled into the black, heard her follow, footfalls soft as confession. Behind them the drip kept time, steady, unimpressed, human.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely accomplished piece of literary fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses systematically. **Characterization and Voice** Rhys and Elin are rendered with considerable skill. Rhys's confession about Ava Delaney arrives organically—not as a plot device but as a pressure release, the kind of oversharing that happens in candlelit intimacy with someone who won't applaud you. His line "I measure time by how long between compliments" is one of the chapter's finest moments: it's self-aware, slightly pathetic, and entirely consistent with the profile of a man whose ego is a meter that needs feeding. The follow-up—"You don't smile. Throws the calibration"—is sharp and character-true. Elin's voice is equally well-handled. "Mam died mid-chapter. Couldn't close the book" is economical and devastating. Her response to his confession—"So you drown books instead of fixing the pipe"—is exactly the kind of deflecting wit the profile calls for: sarcasm as tourniquet. The detail of her applying eczema cream with her sleeve rather than admitting she has it is good showing. **Dialogue** The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. It moves in the half-finished, overlapping register the plan promised. The poetry-reading exchange ("Not a sermon. Speak it like you're asking the walls to forgive you") is excellent—it reveals character, advances intimacy, and feels earned. The final exchange about "sanctuary has two r's" is a lovely closing note, ambiguous enough to carry multiple readings. **Metaphor and Prose Style** Here is where the chapter is most vulnerable to criticism. The plan warned against "forced poetry" and the chapter occasionally succumbs. "The sort of dark that swallows colour" is fine. "A locked throat" for the chapel is slightly overwrought. "Consonants lodge under his ribs" is a reach. "Leather cracked like river mud" works. "Wet and ridiculous" for the confession is perfect—deliberately deflating. The prose is generally controlled, but there are moments where the poetic register tips into self-consciousness: "Thunder rolled approval" is a minor offender, anthropomorphizing weather in a way that feels like a tic rather than a choice. **Plot and Chapter Plan Adherence** The chapter follows its plan closely: lights die, ladder scene, candle-lit poetry exchange, the confession about Ava, the rare Welsh shelf/Mabinogion, the darkness covering shared breathing. The plan's note about "neither moves to relight it; darkness covers thirty seconds of shared breathing" is translated into the moment where "their shared darkness felt suddenly chosen"—which is slightly more told than shown, but the surrounding context earns it. The kettle ending replaces the plan's pure darkness ending with something more active and character-consistent, which is an improvement. **Weaknesses** The chapter occasionally tells rather than shows emotional states: "Guilt rose, acidic" is functional but not as strong as the surrounding material. "Impulse overtook caution" before the confession is a minor tell. The line "no accusation, just inventory" for Elin's look is clever but slightly precious. The final line—"steady, unimpressed, human"—is trying very hard and lands about 70% of the way there; "human" as the final word is a reach. The chapter is perhaps slightly too polished for the "frayed" aesthetic the plan promised. The plan called for "half-finished, overlapped, misheard—human noise" and "Welsh/English swap mid-sentence." The Welsh is present but minimal; the overlapping dialogue is suggested but not fully realized. The chapter is more controlled than the plan's stated intention. **Overall** This is strong literary fiction with genuine character work, excellent dialogue, and mostly earned emotional beats. Its flaws are the flaws of ambition slightly exceeding execution in places, not of incompetence. The chemistry between the characters is palpable and the chapter advances the relationship meaningfully. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Morning leaked through stained glass in flat, chilly colours, the rain replaced by a fine mist that smelled of slate and sheep-dip. Rhys woke on the pew bench, neck seized, mouth tasting of candle smoke and instant coffee grounds. A charity blanket—BRIDGEND HOSPICE—was tucked under his chin. He couldn't remember who had fetched it, only that Elin had dropped it without comment and disappeared upstairs. The shop looked smaller by daylight: ceiling lower, damp patches cartographical across stone. The rescued boxes sat in a barricade near the altar. The Mabinogion lay on top, leather darkened where water had wicked. He touched it, guilty, then pushed the feeling aside—he specialised in exits, not regrets. Footsteps overhead; a kettle growled. He rolled upright, spine clicking like dice, and checked his phone: 7 % battery, three missed calls from Siân, one text—Where the hell are you, shoot moved to Friday. He typed: Wales. Alive. Send. Battery died mid-swoosh, screen black. He was, officially, unreachable. Elin appeared through the vestry door carrying two mugs. Hair wet at the ends, yesterday's jumper swapped for a faded CYMRU rugby top too big for her—probably her father's, probably deliberate armour. "Payment for electricity," she said, handing him coffee the colour of winter river. No milk, two sugars he hadn't asked for. He drank, grateful. "Sleep okay?" she asked, though her tone suggested the answer was irrelevant. "Like a king on a stone throne." He rotated his shoulder. "Got a crown of splinters to prove it." She almost smiled—almost, then hid behind her mug. Silence settled, not unfriendly. Somewhere a clock ticked with the enthusiasm of something that knew no one was watching. He cleared his throat. "Thought about last night. The leak—literal and metaphorical. I've a proposition." Her eyebrow lifted. "Propositions already? Coffee's not that good." He flushed, laughed. "Professional. PR." He gestured at the damp boxes. "You need cash quick. I need… less bad press. We film a thirty-second spot—me reading Welsh poetry, tag the shop, boost traffic. You get hits, I get virtue. Symbiosis." She considered, thumb rubbing the mug handle. "And fee?" "Two fifty. Cash. Enough to keep lights on while you decide which dragon egg to pawn." Her mouth twitched. "Editorial control?" "Your cut, your script. I read what you give me, nothing more." "And you leave straight after?" He felt an unexpected pinch. "If that's the exit clause." Elin looked at the stacked books, then at the meter cupboard as if it might object. "Fine. But we do it now, before I lose nerve or you lose interest." "Deal." He spat on his palm, extended it before pride could edit. She stared, amused and appalled, then slapped her palm against his—wet, childish, binding. "Gross but effective." They set up in the narrow aisle between Crime and Theology—only spot where roof and light coincided. She produced a cracked iPhone tripod, rubber bands holding legs together. He framed the shot: book in hand, stained-glass over his shoulder, damp carpet out of view. She vetoed immediately. "Too saintly. You don't get redemption lighting yet." He laughed, stung. "Suggestions?" She dragged a low stool, sat him beneath the leaking patch—drip timed to hit every seven seconds behind his head. "Authenticity," she said. "Smells like crisis." He positioned the Mabinogion on his knee, pages fanned to show watermarks. "Prop of shame. Happy?" "Ecstatic." She pressed record, nodded: go. He began—slow, actor baritone: "Mi a gefais fy ngeni yn y glaw, a'r glaw a'm gwawr…" Welsh rolled, some mutations missed. He stumbled, grinned at camera. "Rough translation: Born in rain, raised by it, still trying to outrun the cloud— but maybe the cloud's where home starts." She raised hand, cut. "Less travel-brochure. More throat." He inhaled, restarted—softer, voice cracking on glaw like the boy he'd been. When he finished the drip had soaked his collar; he hadn't flinched. Elin held the shot an extra beat, then pressed stop. "Good?" he asked, throat raw. "Good," she admitted, surprised. She played it back. Lens shook, audio popped, but something raw lived there—him, wet-necked, admitting rain. She cropped the ending before his automatic smile reloaded. He watched her thumbs work, felt exposed, then proud, then panicked. "Upload when you're ready. I'll share to four million strangers." "Half of whom will ask why you're sitting under a leak," she said. "And the other half will Google the shop. Metric magic." She pocketed the phone. "Payment." He peeled notes from wallet—two tens, one fiver, one crisp twenty he'd meant to tip the hotel maid. She took them, folded once, slid into back pocket of jeans. "Receipt?" he joked. "Trust me, fame-boy, I've your signature on enough wreckage." Silence ballooned—job done, reason to stay evaporating. He looked at the door, then back. "So… I head off, shower, let the internet do its thing." She nodded, already moving books, creating distance with labour. "Thanks for the loan. And the… performance." He wanted praise, critique, anything. Instead he gathered jacket, phone corpse, residual dignity. At the threshold he turned. "If the views spike, you could ride the wave—crowdfund the roof, keep the eggs." "If the views spike I'll still have damp carpet and a famous watermark." She shrugged. "But we'll see." He opened the door; mist rolled in. "Bye then, Elin." "Bye, sanctuary-with-one-r." He smiled despite himself, stepped outside. Cameras were nowhere—only an old man walking a terrier, paying no attention. The ordinary felt foreign. Behind him the bolt slid, firm, final. He stood a moment, listening to the hush inside his chest—no applause, no ping of notifications—just the echo of her nod and the smell of lemon soap that clung to the jacket he wouldn't wash.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 is a strong piece of writing that largely delivers on its plan and the broader prompt's intentions. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses systematically. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is the chapter's greatest asset. Lines like "Too saintly. You don't get redemption lighting yet" and "Bye, sanctuary-with-one-r" are genuinely sharp, character-specific, and earned. The banter has texture—Elin's corrections are precise and cutting without being cruel, and Rhys's responses reveal his ego-bruising in real time. The spit-handshake is a lovely, grubby, human detail that feels true to both characters. The dialogue avoids the trap of being merely witty; it does character work simultaneously. The chapter follows its plan closely: the morning setup, the proposition, the cross-examination of terms, the filming, the payment, the departure. The plan's note about Rhys rejecting his agent's call is handled differently here (battery dies rather than deliberate rejection), which is actually a more elegant solution—it removes agency from him in a way that feels more honest to his character. The stolen £5 note doesn't appear here, which is a deviation from the plan, though the payment scene is handled well enough without it. Character consistency is high. Rhys's "smile first, eyes second" quality comes through in the automatic smile reloading that Elin crops from the video. His need for praise at the end—"He wanted praise, critique, anything"—is perfectly calibrated to his profile. Elin's practicality, her use of labor to create distance, her grudging admission of "Good" are all consistent with her character. The physical details are grubby and specific in the right way: the BRIDGEND HOSPICE blanket, the cracked iPhone tripod held with rubber bands, the drip timed to hit every seven seconds. These feel observed rather than invented. WEAKNESSES: Some metaphors don't fully land. "Morning leaked through stained glass in flat, chilly colours" is fine, but "ceiling lower, damp patches cartographical across stone" is slightly strained—cartographical is doing too much work for too little payoff. "Silence ballooned" is a cliché. "The ordinary felt foreign" at the end is a bit of a reach for profundity that the scene hasn't quite earned. The chapter plan called for Rhys to be "rattled by her thoroughness" during the cross-examination—this is present but underdeveloped. The negotiation scene moves quickly and could have lingered more on his discomfort. The plan also specified the agent call being rejected as "first voluntary hit to career"—the battery dying is clever but removes the moral weight of that moment. The ending is slightly over-written: "the echo of her nod and the smell of lemon soap that clung to the jacket he wouldn't wash" tips into sentimentality that the chapter has otherwise avoided. It's the one moment where the writing reaches for poetry it hasn't quite earned through the preceding action. The filming scene is the chapter's best sequence and is handled with real skill—the direction she gives him ("Less travel-brochure. More throat"), his genuine vulnerability on the second take, her cropping his automatic smile. This is showing at its best. Overall, this is accomplished, controlled writing with a strong sense of both characters and a good ear for dialogue. The deviations from the plan are mostly improvements or neutral. The weaknesses are real but minor—occasional over-reach in metaphor, one sentimental ending note, and a slightly rushed negotiation. It's well above average genre work and shows genuine craft. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The bell didn’t ring— it cracked, like someone had slammed it with a flat palm. Rhys was midway through alphabetising a stack of Ruth Rendells he’d never read; the name Ava Delaney hit his ears before he even saw her face. “Darling, tell me this hovel isn’t where you’ve been hiding.” She stood in the doorway wearing airport sunglasses and revenge-red lipstick, a phone on selfie-stick already recording. Behind her two men with oversized lenses muscled in, flashes popping off the damp stone like firecrackers. Elin emerged from the poetry aisle, dusting off knees. “Shop’s one customer at a time. Sunglasses off, cameras off.” Ava ignored her, eyes fixed on Rhys. “Needed authentic Welsh grime for the apology reel? Smart. Sympathy optics play huge right now.” She tilted the stick; the lens caught him square, red-eye flare. Rhys felt his smile switch try to auto-load—stuck, jammed by actual guilt. “Ava, stop broadcasting. Please.” “Too late, bach. Thousands watching.” She winked at chat comments scrolling. “Audience wants contrition in situ. Shall we?” She thrust the phone forward like a microphone. The shutter chorus multiplied; one pap curved round Ava, aimed at Elin’s damp carpet, the stack of signed books, the leaking ceiling—evidence of chaos Rhys had imported. Elin stepped between lens and boxes, arms spread like a goalkeeper. “No permission to film here,” she said, voice flat, legal. Ava smiled sweet as arsenic. “Public business, public space. Freedom of press, love.” She pushed past, heel grinding water-buckled cardboard. A low gasp escaped Elin—small, wounded. Rhys heard it break open inside him. He moved, hand rising to block the lens. “Turn it off. Now.” Ava lifted an eyebrow. “Careful, hero. Assault on live-stream trends fast.” One pap jostled closer; the Mabinogion on its display stand wobbled. Rhys steadied it, felt leather slick under sweat. “Outside,” he said to Ava, “talk like adults.” “Adults settle debts publicly these days.” She faced the camera. “Shall we recap, viewers? Mr Morgan here—” He snatched the selfie-stick, killed the broadcast. Chat froze mid-scroll. Ava’s mask slipped; fury raw. “You owe me a career, Rhys. That clip buys it back.” “Not at her expense,” he muttered, nodding toward Elin, who stood stiff, cheek blotching with eczema bloom. Ava glanced at her, dismissive. “Choose your stray, but pay for your trash.” She signalled paps; flashes fired again, white bursts that left purple ghosts. Rhys drew breath, tasted copper. Words arrived unedited. “I leaked the story. Me. No source, no mystery. Wanted the franchise, threw you to the wolves. Sorry.” Silence snapped louder than shutters. One pap lowered camera, scenting bigger game. Ava’s eyes narrowed. “Again, for the close-up?” He looked straight into a lens, voice steady. “I lied. Ava was professional, I wasn't. Studios believed it because I made sure they did. My fault. End.” Ava smiled—slow, victorious. “Upload that, boys. Front page.” She turned to Elin, gave a half-bow. “Thanks for the venue. Atmospheric.” She heel-pivoted, exit slow enough for profile shots. Paps followed, one lingering to capture Rhys beside the damaged boxes, a still-life of guilt. Elin stepped forward, slammed the door herself—bell clanged like a broken cymbal. The bolt shot home. Footsteps retreated into drizzle. Quiet flooded the shop. He waited for shouting, maybe a right hook; none arrived. She simply stared at the threshold where cardboard had split, exposing a slim Welsh grammar now mush. Colour drained from her knuckles as she pressed eczema-scratched skin. "You just saved your skin by shredding my carpet." He bent, lifted the sodden grammar; water ran from spine like ink. "I'll pay. Again. Properly." "With what? Another viral mea culpa?" Her laugh cracked, brittle. "Metrics don't dry paper." He had no answer. The shop smelled of wet parchment and camera flash residue—acidic, permanent. He felt the moment calcify: before public apology, after. Elin crouched, began peeling pages apart, each rip a small accusation. He joined, fingers clumsy. She didn't tell him to stop. They worked in silence broken only by drip and tear. When the grammar was beyond salvation she set it aside, wiped hands on jeans, left smears of fibre and dye. "I can make calls," he offered, quiet. "Conservators, donors—" "No." She stood. "This is mine to bury." He swallowed. "At least let me clear the mess." "Clear yourself first." She passed him a bin-bag, eyes hard but not cold—more tired than angry. "Cameras will camp outside. Back door?" "Only front," he remembered. The joke curdled. She nodded, moved to the vestry. Returned with a faded hospice blanket—the same he'd woken under—and handed it over. "Cover your head, walk fast. They'll chase Ava once she revs her car." He took the blanket, smelled detergent and candle smoke. "Come with me. Take a break." "Shop doesn't close for floods or fools." She turned away, already lifting salvageable books, sorting by damage grade. He hovered, useless, then draped the blanket round shoulders like a poncho. At the door he paused. "Elin—" "Go, Rhys." He opened. Flashes erupted; questions flew like bird-shot. He pulled the blanket over his head, stepped into the gauntlet. Behind him the bolt slid, soft, definite. The sound stayed in his ears long after the paps had their front-page confession and he was alone on the chapel step, wallet full of money that suddenly weighed nothing.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is Chapter 5 of the planned eight-chapter story, covering the invasion of Ava Delaney and the paparazzi, Rhys's public confession, and the aftermath. Let me assess it carefully across all dimensions. **Strengths:** The chapter executes its central dramatic pivot with considerable skill. Rhys's public confession — "I lied. Ava was professional, I wasn't. Studios believed it because I made sure they did. My fault. End." — lands with genuine weight. It's unadorned, which is exactly right; the plainness of the language contrasts effectively with the theatrical setting of a live-stream. This is a character moment that feels earned rather than manufactured. The dialogue is largely strong. Ava's lines are sharp and purposeful: "Needed authentic Welsh grime for the apology reel? Smart. Sympathy optics play huge right now." This establishes her as a credible antagonist without cartoonishness. The exchange has real tension and the power dynamics shift convincingly. Elin's voice remains consistent with her profile — flat, legal, economical. "No permission to film here" is exactly her register. Her final "Go, Rhys" is perfectly calibrated: not cruel, not forgiving, just exhausted and definitive. The physical details are well-chosen and grubby in the way the plan intended: the sodden Welsh grammar, pages peeling apart, smears of fibre and dye. The image of them silently salvaging books together is quietly effective — it shows rather than tells the complicated emotional state. **Weaknesses:** Some of the metaphors strain. "Silence snapped louder than shutters" is a reasonable line, but "words arrived unedited" is a bit flat as a metaphorical construction. "Tasted copper" for nervousness is a cliché. "The moment calcify: before public apology, after" is an interesting idea but the execution is slightly clunky — the colon usage feels forced. The plan called for Elin's eczema to flare under scrutiny and for Rhys to "see red patch on her neck, forgets script." This happens, but the eczema detail is handled somewhat mechanically — it appears, is noted, and then the chapter moves on without the planned emotional resonance of Rhys being genuinely derailed by seeing her physical vulnerability. The plan also specified "she blames him for the invasion; he can't joke his way out" — this does occur, but the blame is relatively muted. The plan's note about the £5 note being thrown and sticking to his chest (the "comedy that hurts") is entirely absent, which is a notable omission since it was described as a key beat. The hospice blanket reappearing is a nice continuity touch, but "smelled detergent and candle smoke" is doing slightly too much atmospheric work in a moment that should be more emotionally raw. "Wallet full of money that suddenly weighed nothing" as a closing line is competent but not exceptional — it's the kind of ending that gestures at profundity without quite achieving it. The plan's closing image was him alone on the chapel step, which is delivered, but the emotional landing is slightly soft. Ava as a character is functional but somewhat one-dimensional — she's a plot device more than a person, which is perhaps acceptable for a chapter antagonist but worth noting. The chapter follows its plan reasonably well, hitting most beats: Ava's invasion, the live-stream, Elin's eczema, Rhys's confession, the aftermath of salvaging books. The missing £5 note comedy beat is the most significant deviation. **Overall:** This is accomplished, confident writing with strong dialogue and good character consistency. It's not quite at the level of the best literary fiction, but it's well above average genre work. The metaphors mostly land, the characters behave consistently, and the emotional arc of the chapter is coherent and affecting. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The shop was a cave after the cameras left—drip, tick, the soft complaint of sodden cardboard giving way. Elin moved through it barefoot, toes curling against cold stone, collecting wounded books and laying them in lines like casualties. Each time she passed the mirror-backed hymnboard she caught a glimpse of herself: shoulders high, mouth set, cheek raw where she'd clawed without noticing. A stranger might read efficiency; she felt scavenger. She told herself numbers: twelve paperbacks total loss, thirty salvageable with tissue and time, one rare grammar gone to pulp. Numbers were safer than names—especially the name that had marched out under a hospice blanket an hour ago. At four-thirty the bell gave a half-hearted clack. Ifan slid in, hoodie hood up like a monk in disgrace. "Saw the feeds," he mumbled. "You okay?" "Feeds?" She kept her back to him, arranging loose pages weight-down. "TikTok. He's trending. You too—sort of. Shot of your ceiling." Ifan offered his phone as evidence; she waved it away. "Views convert to electricity?" He shoved hands in pockets. "People are sending fivers. Someone started a GoFundMe—Save the Chapel Shop. Three hundred quid already." She straightened, vertebrae clicking. "I didn't agree to that." "You don't have to. Money's just...there." He looked ready to duck if she threw something. She rubbed eczema instead, skin hot under fingernails. "Tell them to stop. I'll sort my own roof." "How?" Good question. Bank balance blinked behind her eyes: £147.22 until quarterly rates hit. She returned to sorting, dismissal in the bend of her neck. Ifan took the hint, drifted toward Crime, priced a handful in silence, left without goodbye. Dark came early, October slicing daylight at both ends. She lit the same three candles, their stubs now puddled like small lunar lakes. The drip had slowed to a metronome—every nine seconds, enough time to forget between beats. She sat on the altar step, opened the battered Mabinogion across her knees. Leather felt cold, corpse-cool. A letter slipped from the fly-leaf—folded once, her mother's economical handwriting: If you ever need an excuse to close the chapter, make sure it's your sentence, not someone else's. Mam x Elin read it twice, then again, waiting for comfort or instruction; it offered neither, only permission she'd never learned to use. She folded it back, set the book aside. Footsteps outside—soft, cautious. Not paparazzi; they travelled in loud multiples. A single knock. She stayed seated. Knock again, firmer. She moved to the door, opened a crack. Gareth stood under the weak porch bulb, university lanyard still round his neck, hair longer since she'd seen him last, rain beaded on wool coat. Academia had kept him moisturised. "Got your message," he said. "I didn’t send one." "Your face all over Twitter counts." He smiled, the same conciliatory curve that had once talked her into extensions and conference panels. "May I?" She stepped aside. He entered, sniffed candle smoke and wet paper. "Christ. Looks worse than the photos." "Photos don’t smell." She closed the door, leant against it. "Why here, Gareth?" "Heard you needed rescue." He produced an envelope—university crest, thick. "Small research grant surplus. Two grand. Consider it bridge funding." She stared but didn’t take it. "Comes with what string?" "No string. Maybe coffee, conversation." He glanced around, eyes settling on the rescued first editions. "You could catalogue these properly, write something on marginalia. We submit together—public engagement track." The offer hung, shiny, baited. She felt the old tug: deadlines, footnotes, foot-soaking seminars. Safety. Then she saw his gaze slide to the damp carpet, lip curling almost imperceptibly. Pity with aftertaste of superiority. "I’m not a project," she said quietly. "Never said you were." "You catalogued my shop before you catalogued me." He sighed, patient lecturer. "Elin, pride is luxury. You’re drowning." "Still breathing." She opened the door again. Cold air rushed the candles, flames bowed. Take the money, inner voice pleaded; another voice, colder, said swim or sink, but choose the stroke. Gareth placed the envelope on a pew. "Think it over. Deadline month’s end." He stepped outside, paused. "I miss you, you know. The field misses you." She said nothing. He walked into mist, shoulders hunched, coat flapping like a failed essay. She stared at the envelope, then at the hymnboard mirror: herself small, candle on either side, haloed by irony. She tore the envelope open—not for the cheque but for the blank reverse of the statement. On it she wrote: Reasons to leave / Reasons to stay. The list stayed empty; ink dried while she held the pen. Eleven o’clock. She locked up, climbed the narrow stairs to the flat above. Kitchen light worked—barely—flickering like bad reception. She filled the kettle, set it to boil, opened the fridge: one yoghurt expired, half a bottle of wine. She chose wine, drank from the neck. Her phone buzzed—unknown number. She let it ring. Voicemail pinged. She listened: Welsh-accented woman, brisk. "Ms Thomas, Rhys Morgan's agency. Repairs arranged tomorrow AM—roofer, dehumidifiers, invoice to us. Confirm convenient." She stared at the screen. Second offer in an hour, this one wrapped in someone else's guilt. She deleted the message, then re-dialled, thumbs hovering. Finally texted: Not your responsibility. Thanks anyway. Reply came fast: Too late, lorry booked. 8 sharp. Sleep well. She almost smiled—almost. The kettle clicked off. She left it, carried wine to the window. Below, the lane was empty, paparazzi long gone, Gareth’s car gone, only puddles catching orange streetlight. Somewhere a seagull cried, lost inland. She thought of Rhys under a hospice blanket, cameras exploding; of Ava's victory smirk; of Ifan pricing books by candle; of her mother's letter promising an exit she'd never taken. The wine tasted metallic, like rainwater that had sat too long in guttering. She set the glass down unfinished, opened laptop battery low, typed: GoFundMe refund procedure. Halfway through she stopped, deleted. Typed instead: New list—things that stay dry. She wrote: tongue, temper, tomorrow. She stared at the three t-words, then closed the lid. Back in the kitchen she unplugged the kettle, used the hot water to wash a single spoon. Movement kept thoughts peripheral. She wiped surfaces already clean, lined mugs by handle, faced the same way. The flat smelled of lemon soap and old sermons—nothing of her father, little of her mother anymore. Just her own skin, breathing. At 1:17 a.m. she returned downstairs, candle almost spent. She set it on the counter, opened the till, slipped Gareth's cheque inside without looking at numbers. It felt like planting a bomb and arming someone else to trigger it. She sat on the altar step, Mabinogion across her knees again, and began turning pages, each crack sounding like someone stepping on thin ice. Somewhere in the saga a king asked for rain and got a flood; she read until words blurred, not from tears but from exhaustion finally winning. When the candle guttered to blue, she let the dark stay. The drip counted seconds overhead; she didn't count back. Outside, delivery vans would start soon, bringing bread and scandal and roofers she hadn't asked for. She closed the book, pressed it to her chest like a shield, and listened to the echo of a bolt sliding, deciding tomorrow she'd decide.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 6 is a quiet, interior chapter—Elin alone after the storm, processing the day's wreckage. The model has largely executed its plan competently, though with some notable divergences and unevenness. **Strengths:** The chapter successfully renders Elin's interiority without becoming maudlin. The introduction of Gareth is a smart structural move—he arrives as a second temptation, a mirror to Rhys's offer, and the scene plays out with genuine tension. Elin's refusal ("I'm not a project") lands cleanly, and the detail of Gareth's lip curling "almost imperceptibly" at the damp carpet is precise and revealing. The mother's letter ("If you ever need an excuse to close the chapter, make sure it's your sentence, not someone else's") is thematically resonant without being heavy-handed. The agency voicemail scene is well-handled—Rhys's absence is felt through his representative, and Elin's response (deleting, re-dialling, hovering, finally texting) captures her ambivalence economically. The reply "Too late, lorry booked. 8 sharp. Sleep well." has the right tone—Rhys's charm operating at a remove, through a proxy. The prose is generally controlled. "Academia had kept him moisturised" is a genuinely funny, character-specific observation. "Pity with aftertaste of superiority" is sharp. The three t-words (tongue, temper, tomorrow) feel earned as a small, private ritual. **Weaknesses:** The chapter plan called for Elin finding Rhys's childhood Mabinogion "between hymnals, inscribed with a primary-school scrawl"—this is a significant planned beat that doesn't appear. Instead, she reads her own Mabinogion with her mother's letter. The discovery of his book was meant to be the emotional hinge of the chapter, the thing that makes her go stand in the car park waiting. Without it, her motivation for going downstairs at 1:17 a.m. and her general restlessness feels less anchored. The chapter plan also called for her standing in the car park in drizzle, waiting—this doesn't happen either. The ending is more interior and static than planned. Some metaphors strain: "candles, their stubs now puddled like small lunar lakes" is slightly overwrought. "Coat flapping like a failed essay" is clever but perhaps too clever—it calls attention to itself. "Leather felt cold, corpse-cool" is effective. "Somewhere a seagull cried, lost inland" is a touch generic. The Ifan scene is functional but thin—it exists mainly to deliver exposition (GoFundMe, TikTok trending) and exits without much texture. The dialogue here is the weakest in the chapter, feeling more like information delivery than character interaction. The ending—"deciding tomorrow she'd decide"—is a bit of a non-ending. It's intentionally inconclusive, which fits the chapter's mood, but it lacks the specific image the plan promised (car lights approaching). The ambiguity feels slightly evasive rather than resonant. The chapter is largely dialogue-light, which is appropriate for a solo chapter, but the writing prompt emphasizes dialogue as the primary mode. This chapter is the exception that proves the rule, and it works well enough in that register. **Overall:** A competent, often quietly impressive chapter that misses some of its planned emotional beats (the discovered Mabinogion, the car park vigil) but compensates with the Gareth scene and the agency voicemail. The prose is controlled with occasional overreach. Characters are consistent. The darkness is mostly well-earned. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 10 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 13

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Fireworks stitched the sky above the estuary—dull thumps followed by delayed flashes, gold and blood-red, that jumped between chapel windows like faulty neon. Elin waited until the third echo before she locked the till, pulled on a coat still smelling of damp carpet, and stepped outside. The lane was cobbled orange by street-lights and falling sparks; crowds drifted toward the river where the town's autumn festival was winding up. No one looked at the bookshop door—fame had moved on to fresher wounds. She carried the childhood Mabinogion in a plastic Spar bag, swinging against her knee. Rhys's inscription inside the cover—primary-school capitals, blue biro faded to grey—felt heavier than the book. She told herself she was only returning property, maybe collecting the roofer's invoice, nothing that required conversation. The walk to the car-hire yard took seven minutes; she counted them by footfalls, not by heartbeats. He was there, as predicted, boot of a silver hatchback open, blanket folded neat, suitcase threatening to spill jumpers. He wore the same jacket from day one, now dried into shapeless expensive cloth. When he saw her he stopped mid-slam, keys dangling like dropped applause. "Thought you'd be halfway to London," she said, stopping just outside arm's reach. "Cardiff first. Then anywhere the next job isn't." He nodded toward the sky where a final rocket burst. "Heard the finale from here. Cheaper than tickets." Bag dangled between them. She lifted it. "Yours." He took the book, thumb brushing the inscription. "Been looking for this everywhere. Thought I'd lost home and story in one go." Voice rough, unsure where to settle. Firework smoke drifted, metallic on the air. Voices cheered down by the water; someone let off a shrill whistle. "Roofer came," she said. "Fixed slates, left bill unpaid. Your agent's fast." "She owes me favours. Thought I could..." He shrugged. "Balance the ledger a bit." "Ledgers never balance, they just change shape." She rubbed eczema patch, now scabbing. "But thanks. Roof needed more than prayer." He set the book into the suitcase, wedged between socks and a rolled-up script still pristine. She spotted the title page—action sequel, number three. Her stomach gave a small, traitor flip. Silence stretched, elastic. Finally he spoke, eyes on trainers. "The clip's at forty thousand. Comments mostly nice. One guy wants to name his band Leaking Chapel." "Tell him royalties are twenty percent." He smiled, then grew serious. "Money came through. Two fifty plus repairs. Give me your details, I'll transfer—" "Keep it." She surprised herself; the words tasted true. "Consider it payment for humidity damage to your ego." He laughed once, breathy. "Ego's still damp. But breathing." Another rocket—spent—fizzed overhead, embers drifting like failed stars. People were walking back up the lane, some humming the national anthem off-key. He shut the boot, keys jangling louder than the festival drums. "So...Cardiff, then." "Then the world," she finished for him. Neither moved. Wind off the estuary carried salt and diesel; her coat sleeve flapped against his. He spoke first, voice low. "You could come. An hour's drive. Coffee. Talk that isn't about ceilings." She imagined the M4, service stations, the student city she'd avoided since dropping out of viva. "I've a shop full of dying stock." "I've a suitcase half empty." They looked anywhere but each other—her at the sky, him at keys—performing the calculations people do when the sum is heart plus maybe. She broke the stand-off. "We film the follow-up tomorrow. You promised the literacy lot a part two." "I promised myself not to promise anything again." "Start small. Promise nine a.m." He half-smiled. "Nine sharp. Your place?" "Bring your own milk. Mine expired with good intentions." Another cheer drifted; crowds thickened, forcing them to step closer or be separated by strangers. They stepped closer. "Welsh or English?" he asked suddenly. "For the clip?" "For...this." He gestured between them, vague as mist. She considered, then spoke, language switching mid-breath: "We'll start English, finish Cymraeg—let the words decide when they change." He swallowed hard, nodded. "See you at nine." She turned first, walked into the stream of festival-goers. Didn't look back—wouldn't give him that cinematic exit. Yet she felt his gaze like hand-prints between her shoulders, warm, uncertain, real. Halfway up the lane she allowed one glance. He was still beside the car, suitcase open again—stuffing the hospice blanket inside, movements hurried, as if packing away the last excuse to linger. Their eyes met across distance; he lifted a hand, not quite wave, not quite salute. She raised the spare bag in answer, then kept walking. Fireworks ended; smoke settled over roofs like dirty lace. Her footsteps sounded louder without rain, every heel-click counting possibilities: nine a.m., camera, coffee, conversation that might end after one sentence or stretch into autumn. Back at the shop she unlocked, stepped inside, inhaled paper and candle stub and distant lemon. She set the till drawer open, slipped the unsigned cheque from Gareth into an envelope, wrote RETURN TO SENDER across the front—letters decisive, almost his. She'd post it tomorrow, whatever tomorrow became. Upstairs she filled the kettle, one mug only, then paused, took a second from the hook—just in case. While water heated she opened laptop, clicked the uploaded clip: 47,612 views. Comments flew—some in Welsh, some in Tagalog, one simply a row of rain-cloud emojis. She hovered over delete, closed tab instead. Let the world keep its snapshot; she had the unedited version playing behind her eyes. Sleep came quicker than expected, no list of leavers or stayers, only the echo of an agreement made in two languages, neither fully translated. At some point before dawn the drip finally stopped—last droplet falling between beats of her heart, the roof sealed by strangers, the night quiet for the first time in a week. She didn't notice; she was dreaming of a ladder, a candle, a hand steadying the rung, uncertain whether it was hers or his, but steady all the same.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 is a strong piece of romantic fiction that largely delivers on its plan and the broader prompt. Let me assess it carefully across dimensions. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is genuinely accomplished. Lines like "Keep it. Consider it payment for humidity damage to your ego" and "Ego's still damp. But breathing" feel earned, character-specific, and witty without being stagey. The exchange about Welsh versus English ("We'll start English, finish Cymraeg—let the words decide when they change") is the chapter's best moment—thematically resonant, character-consistent, and quietly romantic without being saccharine. The banter about milk expiring "with good intentions" is similarly well-pitched. The chapter follows its plan closely: fireworks, the Mabinogion return, monosyllabic conversation bursting into something more, the agreement to film the clip, the firework reflected in puddle (though this is rendered as embers rather than a puddle reflection—a minor deviation). The plan called for "neither says 'stay'" and instead they agree to film—this is executed well. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Rhys is appropriately rogue-ish but showing genuine vulnerability—the suitcase half-empty line is a nice piece of transparent charm that Elin deflects. His ego is present but muted here, which fits the arc. Elin's deflections feel authentic to her profile: practical, sardonic, self-protective. The detail of her returning Gareth's cheque marked RETURN TO SENDER is excellent—it shows rather than tells her decision-making, and it's consistent with her character's pride and forward motion. WEAKNESSES AND CONCERNS: Some metaphors don't fully land. "Keys dangling like dropped applause" is a reach—applause doesn't dangle, and the image is strained. "Embers drifting like failed stars" is competent but slightly generic. "Smoke settled over roofs like dirty lace" is better but borders on purple. The opening paragraph's "fame had moved on to fresher wounds" is a good line. "Performing the calculations people do when the sum is heart plus maybe" is clever but slightly self-conscious—it draws attention to its own cleverness in a way that briefly breaks immersion. The plan called for "argument about who failed whom" in Welsh—this doesn't materialize. The chapter is more harmonious than the plan suggested, which softens the tension somewhat.
